Original_Search_Title: Contractors Of Niger Delta Varsity Urged To Speed Up Work
Headline: Nigeria: Contractors Of Niger Delta Varsity Urged To Speed Up Work
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — Contractors handling project work at the Niger Delta
University site have been directed to put in extra effort to
translate the state government vision for the institution into
reality.
The Commissioner for Education Dr. Mark Atei Okorobia gave the
directive while on an unscheduled visit to the university site at
Wilberforce Island to assess the progressive made so far.
Dr Okorobia said the directive became necessary as the university
must commence full academic work by the year 2001.
The education commissioner said government was committed to providing
an ultra modern university for the state and urged the contractors to
co-operate with the construction manager to achieve the desired
result.
He advised the contractor not to hesitate to report any obstacle in
the execution of their job to his office for necessary action.
The commissioner was accompanied by the Vice Chancellor of the
university, Prof. John Buseri and the registrar, Mr. Perelade
Apotala. Conducting the commissioner and his team round the site, the
Niger Delta University project engineer, Amos Waritimi assured the
commissioner that the project would be completed on scheduled.
Meanwhile, residents of Ovom in Yenagoa have been warned against the
vandalisation of the fence of Bishop Dimeari Grammar School.
The state Commissioner for Education who gave this warning expressed
disappointment that inspite of the agreement reached with the
representative of the community some resident from the area have
resorted to vandalising the newly constructed fence.
Dr. Okorobia warned that he would hold the representative of the
community responsible for any further damage due to the school fence.
He disclosed that the state runs the risk of losing its allocation
from the Education Tax Fund (ETF) if the fence is not completed.
The commissioner also directed the contractor handling the
construction of Gwegwe road to come to his office for consultation in
respect of the school fence pulled down in the process of
constructing the road.
He also advised the Winners Chapel and yam sellers occupying part of
the school premises to vacate the place as directed by the state
governor recently.
He recalled that they were given enough time to find alternative site
for their various activities nothing that illegal occupation of the
premises has contributed to the insecurity of lives and property in
the school.

Original_Search_Title: Music Is The Food Of Matters Of The Heart
Headline: Zimbabwe: Music Is The Food Of Matters Of The Heart
Body_Text:
Harare — "It started with a kiss in the back row of a classroom"- so
go the lyrics of one of Hot Chocolate's most popular tunes, a song
that several years ago marked the beginning of some steamy and
youthful relationships.
In Zimbabwe and South Africa in the 1970s, the Hurricanes had
everyone crooning: "Whisper to me, tell me how much do you love me?
Tell me, tell me that you love me true, let me know about your inner
feelings. I will believe whatever you say even if you lie baby."
But if you are today's kid on the block, then you would probably say:
"I don't care who you are, where you're from, where you've been as
long as you love me," and that would be courtesy of the Backstreet
Boys.
Ringo Madlingozi, in Into Yam, says: "For a long time I have wanted
to speak to you but I don't even know your name. I'm not one of those
guys who mess around. I just want you alone."
Madlingozi will go on to claim that his love is so strong even his
brains can no longer function. And he adds that what is his is yours
and yours is his.
These words - brothers and sisters - have become part of everyday
repertoire of men and women in love or those trying to win over
someone.
Music plays a very important role in articulating our feelings about
various issues, especially the affairs of the heart.
When things work out, you find yourself imagining all sorts of
passionate things about your new lover. This is when Don Williams
comes in and tells you that last night he dreamt he held you in his
arms. You made love and it lasted so long.
Dolly Paton and Porter Wagoner will make their contribution in
Together You and I.
"My love is deeper than the depth of an ocean, as faithful as the
stars that grace the night and as constant as the sun's journey
through the heaven, that's why we will always be together you and I,
" goes the song.
So far folks our love life is sparkling but then at some point things
will slow down or you get attracted to another person. But this is
not to say you want to give up what you have. Remember there is such
a thing as having your cake and eating it too.
RoyC will tell you that he's got to get enough of your sweet love,
even though he knows that you are married. He sings that he can't see
anything wrong with making love to another man's wife as long as you
do it right. Wow! It is indeed getting steamy and UB40 will admit
that "I'm guilty of loving you, even though I know you are engaged to
someone else. Love is a thing that's a lot like quicksand, the more
you wriggle the more you sink".
And when you do succumb to the temptation of an extra-marital affair,
then you have to be very careful because you do not want your man to
spot you walking with his best friend laughing and talking.
RoyC comes back with She Kept on Walking, a song about a man who
finds out that his best friend has taken over his woman. With tears
in his eyes, he wonders why she should leave him. Is it because the
friend makes love better than he does? But if you do find out that
your friend's woman is cheating on him, keep it to yourself because,
as Percy Sledge says, when a man loves a woman he can't see anything
wrong with her.
When a man loves a woman, he would even sleep in the rain if the
woman said it was the right thing to do.
"You say you're gonna leave me? I'm begging you to stay but baby I
can't get through to you. When I wake up in the morning, I find you
gone. Baby please don't make me cry because I know there will be
nothing left for me," this is UB40, finally admitting that the end
has come.
Now my brother or sister, you have to deal with the pain of losing
someone but you still want them back and Dolly Paton gives you the
words in Come Back To Me.
"Come back to me and kiss me as only you can," she says. "Touch me
like nobody else can. Come back to me and hold this torch that's
burning me. Come back to me and never leave me, come back and mend
this broken heart in me."
But for those who cannot bow out gracefully, Dolly Paton has the
perfect song for you in I hope You're Never Happy.
It goes: "I wish you all the luck, I wish you only the best:
satisfaction, love and true happiness. I wish you blue skies and
rainbows. I wish you joy but only if you are with me. I hope you're
never happy with anybody but me. I wish you nothing but trouble if
you decide to leave."
This song holds true for many people, which is why you hear stories
of people who torch themselves or their spouses if they are ditched.
When we fall in love, at times friends or relatives tell us that we
have made a bad choice, to which Randy Crawford says: "They told me I
was such a fool to love you. They said you were the kind that will
never be true. I gave you so much love and you pretended you loved
me. I will never leave you - that's how heartaches are made."
Relationships are very complex so half the time when you have a good
thing it is hard to realise it. Don Williams reflects on this in his
song In The Shelter of Your Eyes.
"It took me so long to realise I just can't make it on my own. Words
are only what they say but this feeling is not wrong. I'm so glad I
found my way, it's good to be where I belong and I'm gonna stay right
here because I'm in rhythm with your mind."
While trying for a second time might work for others, megastar
Madonna believes there is power in saying goodbye, and Luther
Vandross simply says: "Give me a reason to love you back. Why should
I love you again? Tell me how to forgive and forget."
And when you are really over him or her, then you can go the Dolly
Paton way and whenever his or her picture falls out of your wallet,
you tell people he's just someone you used to know.
Paton's Just Someone I Used To Know is a very powerful song and I
know so many people who will tell you that this is just a flame that
lost its glow. They will never tell you about the nights they cry
without the ex.
While we can vow that we will never love again, you should be grown
up enough to admit as Sybil does that "we loved each other we just
couldn't get along, take a good look at me the love that I lost. I'm
so sad and lonely, I'm sorry to see you go your way and I will go
mine."
Like Al Green, you will one day get tired of being alone and you
start all over again. If you have never been lucky in love, Gerald
Levert might help you find your Mr. Too Damn Good.
Levert wants to be your sunny day, your favourite tune, your
breakfast and oh, baby, he wants to be your food, basically your Mr.
Too Damn Good.
So there you are folks! Go out and buy your loved one or yourself
music this Christmas.
I have had to rephrase some of the music lyrics that I have used due
to space restrictions. In the future, I will take you on another
musical journey but this time with our local musicians.
More love!

Original_Search_Title: Who's Crying - The North Or The Rest Of Us?
Headline: Nigeria: Who's Crying - The North Or The Rest Of Us?
Body_Text:
Lagos — "The North shall not cry. Those who believe there can be
Nigeria without the North are merely idling away. The North is the
principal stakeholder in Nigeria. No North, no Nigeria""
That is a part of a "powerful" speech made on behalf of the North by
its appointed spokesman, Abdullahi Adamu, lawyer, governor of
Nassarawa State, at a recent reception in Kaduna for Vice-President
Atiku Abubakar.
Adamu was the Minster of State for Works under General Abdulkarim
Adisa in the Sani Abacha dispensation. He has always been a
politician. I make bold to say that if you take politics out
Abdullahi, there will be nothing left. Not even his Law. What he has
done has been to subjugate his law studies to his politics. If you
ask me, it is serving him right.
I may not be strictly correct to surmise that he must have been a
substantial partaker in, if not the sole writer of, the drafting of
the speech. Because the speech was not, to my mind, directed at
reason; nor the reality of the Nigerian situation. It was calculated
to disorientate, in the manner of the devil's advocate, the man in
the dock - regardless of the merit or the demerit of the case of the
litigant the cross-examining advocate represents. Counsel who adopt
that kind of tactics usually intend to gain technical points in the
hope that the presiding judge may look at their clients favourably.
But when the chips are down, the facts of the case usually carry with
the bright and the upright judge.
The rhetoric was grandiloquent but bared of its bone, what did
Abdullahi say on behalf of the North he represented. To me, it means
the North is crying already because the milk is spilled. The Yoruba
knew that through centuries of life experience. They say: Ko dun mi,
ko dun mi, agbalagba ng bu opa l'e mefa nitori iyan ana (It doesn't
matter, it doesn't matter, yet the elderly man curses six times on
account of missing the pounded yam last night). The North is
definitely crying because of lost opportunity - opportunity to rid
its people of ignorance; opportunity to take its people out of
poverty. Who must they blame but themselves?
"Those who believe there can be Nigeria without the North are merely
idling away", Abdullahi intoned. He knew the converse of his
rhetoric: who says there will be a Nigeria without the Southwest;
without the Southeast; without the South-South; without the
Middle-Belt, if it comes to that?
"The North is the principal stakeholder in Nigeria", thundered
Abdullahi. What is the investment? How is the North investment higher
than that of the others? Nigeria surely did not come about at the
North's instance - either by its political efforts or by conquest.
What has been the North's contribution to the commonwealth that makes
her the principal investor in our fortune since the union began in
1914?
"The incontestable fact remains that in the political equation of
this country, the North holds the ace", asserted Abdullahi. Yes, he
made a statement that should make some people think. That assertion
is correct and has history behind it. I cannot say how the North has
been able to hold some people in vice grip but it is a fact that
Adamu's North has gone that far because it has used the East and the
South- South to mount and to retain power. Even in the face of
decimating their people, time and time again. If this is not the
whole truth, let people of the Southeast and the South-South show me
what they have gained from their retaining the chains on their ankles
and on their hands. Of course, the people of the Middle-Belt have
always been the cat's paw. Recently, they have been making some
ineffectual noises about breaking their yoke, however. But can they
or would they?
Adamu's speech at Atiku's reception was a blackmail that was clear
for what it is. I grant that the North has a right to live the way it
wants to live but the South also has that self-same right.
I hope, however, that Adamu's cutting insults get to the people of
the South-South and the Southeast: that they have no minds of their
own; that they can be teleguided by the Southwest. It is more cutting
to say they do not know their interest: Can the South-South see its
despoliation; its neglect; the carnage of Ogoni; of Odi; the curse of
Jesse? Or can they not?
The bottomline is: what is the interest the Adamu North is protecting
in Nigeria? Adamu's North has ruled for at least three- quarters of
Nigeria's independence. What has happened has been the heightening of
tribal tension; the ruination of the economy; the routine massacre of
Nigerians.
Let us admit, all of us, our mistakes and come down from our high
horses. We may still be able to salvage what is left.
What can we make of a people who are talking of avenging the so-
called wrongs of the last mayhem in Lagos? Who do they want to avenge
and on whose behalf - Nigerians or Northerners? What do you make of
people using religion for politics? Do they think that people have
forgotten Akaluka whose head was severed and paraded on the streets
of Kano? Do they think the people of Kogi, supposed to be in the
North, whose young priest was killed in the last Kaduna riots have
forgotten?
Surely Nigerians want to forget and move on but utterances such as
those of Adamu and the so-called Sharia body would not help matters.
Until we can agree on the need to come together and how to come
together it is going to be to "thy tents oh Israel". To loosen guard
is to be forced into a false sense of security. Until then, I shall
remain a Yoruba first, second and last in this unreliable contraption
called Federal Nigeria.
Abdullahi Adamu spoke of the Northern numerical strength, that
strength is suspect. That suspicion was what the Yoruba leader, Pa
Abraham Adesanya, codified as goats and sheep voting in elections.
Until a true and reliable census is conducted the numeral strength
will remain suspect.
The blackmailers of the North have tried to sow discord among the
people of the South-South and Southwest. The Southwest cannot
appropriate the resources of the South-South; the Southwest is an
implacable advocate of owners of resources controlling and enjoying
them.
Practically, every part of this country can survive on its own. It
takes just hard work, dedication, good faith and the will to do so.
Wealth creation has defied the conventional economic alchemy of land,
labour, capital. Land may still have something to do with it, but
technology and capital have more to do with it now.
Why must we share a consumption tax with those who do not consume? To
do so is what Adamu wants. But our amiable Vice-President, Abubakar
Atiku, did prescribe for the arrogant Northerner and I hope he took
the point at the same reception.
He said: "Selfishness, self-centredness and parochialism had never
led to progress and development, rather they dwarf societal vision,
stunt development and undermine the cohesive strength of societies,
leaving them stuck and vulnerable".
People are warned not to start what they cannot finish.

Original_Search_Title: Express Fisheries Comes Under New Management
Headline: Nigeria: Express Fisheries Comes Under New Management
Body_Text:
Lagos — As the Christmas season sets in, the culinary sector is
exploring all avenues to keep Nigerians busy with assorted dishes to
make the period truly memorable.
One such move was recorded recently following the opening of business
at the popular Express Fisheries on Ajao/Olufemi junction Surulere,
under a new management Ugoli's Restaurant and bar.
The managing director, Ugolis restaurant and bar, Mr. Jonice Ogonuwe
said while speaking at the opening ceremony in Lagos that the public
was certainly in for good times beyond the Christmas period.
He said "good things have happened in Surulere, at your door
particularly to those living around Ajao and Olufemi streets, who now
have the opportunity to savour our dishes."
He implored would-be customers to expect the best in African cuisine
and local dishes including pounded yam, fried rice, jollof rice,
salad, semovita, amala, fufu and eba, with assorted soups to go with
every choice.
Speaking further, he urged prospective customers to come in and have
a taste of their daily menu adding, the taste of the pudding is in
the eating."
Ogonuwe explained that he found succour in restaurant management in
order to demonstrate what he has learnt all these years and have
creditably done well in it since he ventured into the industry of
over a decade now.
The managing director who disclosed that Express Fisheries brought to
three, the fast food outlet managed by his company, said the fourth
would be an upbeat fast food shop located at Okonlawon which would
commence operations soon.
He spoke of plans to increase the staff strength but commended the 15
workers who had been responsible for packaging these fast foods at
what he described as moderate prices, saying their level of
productivity coupled with the hardwork of his wife over a period of
four years brought the company to its present enviable position.

Headline: Nigeria: The Price Mongers
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerians live at the mercy of price mongers who capitalise
on minor dislocation to arbitrarily hike prices Arbitrary price hike
on the pretext of slight crisis in the country is increasingly
becoming endemic.
Consumers are left to dance to the whims and caprices of importers,
manufacturers, retailers, service providers and transporters. Any
slight upset in the economy, no matter how marginal, immediately
attracts higher prices to goods and services.
However, it takes only one day to get pushed to the wall and you
start to fight back. One day you find out you have been kicked so
much and so hard that you cannot take any more.
Early in the week, I went to Oshodi market in Lagos to buy meat but
found out that a piece of meat which otherwise sold for N500 now sold
for N1,500. Reason? the butchers said fighting broke out between
members of the Oodua People's Congress (OPC) and Hausa butchers at
the Oko-Oba abattoir, Agege, Lagos. About one week after normalcy was
returned to the troubled Abattoir, meat sellers still using the fight
as an excuse to hike prices.
A survey at the various cold stores in Ikeja, Apapa and Balogun
market in Ogba, revealed that, a kilogram of chicken/turkey parts
which hitherto sold for N170, now attracts N300. "Most of the frozen
chicken/turkey parts are imported so, what has it got to do with the
mayhem at the Abattoir?" I asked the traders. "Madam, everything don
cost" replied one of the traders.
At the Ketu/Mile 12 market, Lagos, the prices of every item from
fresh tomatoes, yam and beans had all attracted 50 to 100 per cent
price increase few days after the OPC/Hausa skirmishes had subsided.
A tin of brown beans which sold for N500 about three weeks ago, now
sells for N700. Of course, the transporters had their own field day.
Transport fare went up as high as 200 per cent. Last week,
transporters going to Obalende/CMS bus stop in Lagos Island, charged
as high as N150 instead of the normal fare of N40. Mr. Johnson Jimoh,
a member of the Road Transport Union explained that, they bought a
four litre fuel for N500 instead of N110.
But then, does that justify the almost N200 increment? There are 29
Industrial Unions in the country, each one protecting the interest of
its members but none protecting consumers. According to Comrade Ola
Ojedokun, Deputy General-Secretary, National Union of Food, Beverage
and Tobacco Employees, "apart from the Nigerian Labour Congress
(NLC), no other body has been championing the cause of consumers."
Blaming government for the arbitrary price hike, he said "it is their
duty to control the prices of commodities and keep inflation in check
but they are handicapped as they are even too weak to control the
manufacturers."
Stressing the need for a consumer association, Comrade Ojedokun said
"regrettably, the level of our awareness may not make it possible
because, every one is after his pocket. We have had a situation where
Government tried to control house rent but selfish landlords tried to
jeopadise it."
Godwin Eluwa, the Managing Director of Godax Nigeria Ltd, Allen
Avenue Ikeja, also stressed the need for a consumer association and
called on the Federal Government to organise itself by working
towards a stable economy, higher naira value before the private
entrepreneurs can follow suit.
A consumer association, said Mr. Eluwa, "will mean a pressure group
that can reject sub-standard products, bring prices of products down,
draw Government's attention to the influx of sub-standard imported
food products flooding our markets just like you have in other
developed countries." In July, a seminar tagged: "National Workshop
on Consumer Protection" was organised by a Consumer Protection
Council in Abuja and the Nigerian Institute of Legal Studies Lagos.
The Consumer Protection Council went into hiding as soon as the
seminar ended. Consumers are yet to feel their impact while majority
do not even seem to be aware of its existence.
In Nigeria, there is no known democratic legislation regarding
consumer protection. The consumer protection laws available, can be
classified according to Federal and state laws. The Federal laws
include, the Hire-Purchase Act of 1990, the Food and Drugs Act 1990,
the Price Control Act 1990. The Merchandise Marks Act 1990, the
Standards Organisation of Nigeria Act 1990, the NAFDAC Decree 1993,
the Consumer Protection Council Decree 1992 and the Trade
Malpractices (Miscellaneous Offences) Decree 1992.
The state laws include the Sale of Goods, the Carriers, the Inn
Keepers and Hotel Proprietors Laws. But none of these has in the real
sense of it, offered any protection to the Nigerian consumers.
Publication date: November 6, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Leader of Nation's First Coup To be Exhumed and Reburied
Headline: Nigeria: Oshimili Confers Posthumous Title On Nzeogwu, May Exhume Corpse
Body_Text:
Okpanam, Delta — Thirty-four years after the death of the leader of
Nigeria's first military coup, the late Major Chukwuma Kaduna
Nzeogwu, his kinsmen are poised to exhume his body and rebury him at
Okpanam in Oshimili North local government of the state.
The late Nzeogwu had also been conferred with a posthumous
chieftaincy title of Dike Omu of Okpanam, meaning "the strong one,
the one who leads in time of war."
The Oza-Omu of Okpanam, Chief (Mrs.) Martha Dunkwu, announced this
weekend at Okpanam on the occasion of her first New Yam Festival,
first coronation anniversary and 45th birthday celebrations.
She appealed to the federal, state and local governments "to name
streets, institutions and monuments after Major (Chief) Chukwuma
Kaduna Nzeogwu (of blessed memory), a courageous and great son of
Africa.
"Government", she further suggested, "should also institute an
Nzeogwu memorial lecture, which will eventually reveal whether this
motives were selfish or for a genuine progressive national purpose.
"At the appropriate time, the people of Okpanam will demand for the
body of Chief Nzeogwu to be exhumed and reburied at Okpanam," she
said, explaining that the anti-corruption drive of the present
administration "has only cosmetic value at the moment as its proper
application has not been achieved", while the Niger Delta Development
Commission (NDDC) Act was yet to be fully set on course.

Headline: Zimbabwe: We Are Prepared to Die Rioters
Body_Text:
Harare — Eyes and nose itchy and streaming, I forced my way into the thickening
crowd just to catch what some of the agitated young men and women were
trying to say.
But suddenly the atmosphere was shattered once again by a volley of
teargas fired into houses and straight into the growing crowd. This scenario
of upheaval had been played over and over again early this Monday morning in
Harare's high-density suburbs of Mabvuku and Tafara.
Some of the teargas canisters were shot right into the schools in the
two suburbs where Grade Seven and Ordinary Level students sitting their
final exams were rudely interrupted. They had to contend with struggling
with the right answers as well as breathing.
Residents, citing unbearably high costs of living and the political
crisis in the country, said they had reached breaking point and could not
carry on anymore.
"We have reached a point where we can't carry this Robert Mugabe
government anymore. Mugabe must go now because we have had enough
problems. We have no jobs and don't know where our next meal will come
from. Those of us who still have jobs can't even afford to go to work
because of high transport costs," said one Mabvuku man.
He applied a wet pink towel to his eyes to relieve the sting of the
teargas and went on: "Instead of the government doing something to alleviate
our problems, they are sending their police force to intimidate us by
teargassing our families.
"We are prepared to die if it means Mugabe and his government will go as
they must go."
Around him, several young men and women chanted "Mugabe must go" and
vowed that some of them would have to die to make this possible.
One young woman, hair askew and eyes red and streaming, said she and
other poor residents in areas such as these were now used to teargas and
strong-arm treatment by the police.
"They can throw as many teargas canisters as possible but there's no
going back. We are hungry, unemployed and have absolutely no hope for a
better future as long as Mugabe is still in power," she said.
"We are not scared of dying because as long as Mugabe is in power, we
are actually dead."
One middle-aged woman sporting a tattered brown blouse scoffed at the
suggestion by the Consumer Council of Zimbabwe that consumers should boycott
bread and instead feed their families with sweet potatoes or yams.
"How many people in this country have fields of yams and sweet potatoes?
You still need money to buy enough to feed a family. These people are so far
removed from reality that they don't seem to realise that our major problem
is lack of money, not alternative food," the woman said.
Another woman, wiping mucus and tears from a toddler's face, pointed out
that the suggestion would only work when the sweet potatoes and yams were
still in season.
"What then do we do when they are no longer in season? But even then, we
have no disposable incomes to talk of so I think it's very unfair for well
fed and rich people to suggest alternatives for us when they are free to
make their own choices," she said.
The prices of basic food commodities such as mealie-meal, cooking oil,
sugar and bread have all become a luxury for most families.
A standard loaf of bread is now selling for anything between $22 and $27
and most urban families depend on bread for their breakfast and lunch.
Roads leading into both suburbs were barricaded with heavy rocks, car
wrecks and burning tyres.
The riot police, determined to put an end to the growing unrest managed
to round up some of the young men and women and forced them to remove the
barricades but no sooner had the police passed than another group of young
people came and put back the blockades in scenes reminiscent of South
Africa's 1980s struggle against apartheid.
Even in other parts of the suburbs where the streets were deserted,
truckloads of riot police willy-nilly fired teargas into houses as if to
draw out the people.
As if prepared to do war, some of the residents made their way onto the
streets armed with wet towels, a sure sign of seasoned teargas victims, and
threw stones and any other missile at the law enforcement agents.
As some of the rioters marched towards the police, they chanted that the
teargas was mild and would not kill them.
Business came to a standstill as shops were forced to close and market
stalls stood dusty and deserted. Those who had earlier in the morning dared
to buy bread were forced to crumble it and throw it away.
A Kukura Kurerwa bus that had tried to defy orders not to take workers
into town was burnt down and commuter omnibuses were forced to pick up and
drop off passengers on the out-skirts of the two suburbs.
By midday, the situation was growing more tense as school children
joined in the chanting of slogans denouncing the government and Mugabe.
The running battles between the people and the police intensified but
the residents refused to be cowed. As we continued on the teargas trail, one
of the three police trucks suddenly stopped.
About 15 police officers spilled out, moved menacingly towards the young
people, under orders from other policemen to remove yet another barricade
across the road separating Mabvuku and Tafara.
Wielding baton sticks, the police officers made for the youths and
started whipping them and telling them off for blaming the government for
their problems. By now several members of the foreign media were busy
filming the events as they unfolded.
The leader of the force who had led his officers in whipping the youths
suddenly turned onto the media and demanded their Press cards.
Forced to retreat, Chris Mazivanhanga, a cameraman with Associated Press
(AP), was manhandled and forced to retreat. Rob Cooper, also of AP, was told
rudely to leave before the officer turned on the Financial Gazette crew and
demanded that they too leave.
Only the state-controlled news agency ZIANA was allowed to go on with
its work.
"All of you should leave because you are disturbing our programme," said
the officer, adding to his fellow officers: "The ZIANA guys can go on
because they are our people."
As we drove out of Mabvuku, a soft drink delivery truck was being turned
away by the police who said if the driver valued his security and that of
his goods he should put as much distance as possible between himself and the
two suburbs.
I could not help but notice that the police could have had a tip- off
about the impending disturbances.
A few metres from the junction that joins the Mabvuku and Mutare roads
were the remains of a fire and police coats draped on a shrub and an
assortment of cups.
On the other side of the capital, in the high-density suburb of Mufakose
and in the nearby town of Chitungwiza, a few skirmishes pitting residents
and tuck-shop owners had been quickly quelled down by the police.
By Tuesday morning, the riots had spread like wild fire to the capital's
other high- density suburbs of Mbare, Kuwadzana, Highfield, where once again
police fought running battles with residents.
One was left with the feeling that neither side was willing to
compromise.
Alfred Nhema, a political scientist at the University of Zimbabwe, said
continuous hikes in the price of basic goods had reached unacceptably high
levels that were not matched by salary increases.
"In most countries, food riots are symptomatic of the underlying
political situation in the country and such demonstrations usually serve as
a wake-up call to the political leadership and the elite in the country," he
said.
"This problem has to be resolved because it does not help to simply
adopt the ostrich approach of burying your head in the sand. In most cases,
food riots are a precursor to political instability so they need to be
addressed or they will spread."

Original_Search_Title: Strike Will Persist Unless Government Disciplines Itself-Akinlaja
Headline: Nigeria: Strike Will Persist Unless Government Disciplines Itself-Akinlaja
Body_Text:
His predecessor was a firebrand and has remained so ever since. And
he has proved as daring since he succeeded Chief Frank Kokori, the
man that stood on the side of the masses during the June 12
imbroglio. Joseph Akinlaja, the new helmsman at NUPENG does not speak
with hot yam in his mouth. He calls a spade a spade. In this no holds
barred chat with Asst. Features Editor, Leonard Nzenwa the man had
warned that more shock treatment may come the way of government if it
does not discipline itself.
EARLY last week, Lagos the commercial nerve centre of the nation
almost witnessed another round of fuel scarcity following threats by
your members that they were going to go on strike. How was this
misunderstanding resolved.
Yes, when we heard that the tanker drivers and the Lagos State
Government were going to be on collision course, we took pains in
this office to contact the authorities, that is the director of State
Security Services (SSS) and the Lagos State Government. Everybody
played a wonderful role. The executive governor of Lagos State,
Senator Bola Tinubu, even invited me and officials of PTD in the zone
and resolved the matter. He asked that the vehicles be released and
also said in future if they have disagreement with any of his
functionaries, at least before they do anything they should give him
the opportunity to intervene which we like very much. Myself and
Senator Tinubu resolved many problems quietly. But the problem also
is that it is difficult also to see authorities without problems.
They say he is not around, which does not help issues at all. People
say that there are state matters, and really there are state matters,
but it seems to me too that there are some that are more urgent than
the others. If you hear that certain officers call and you are not
there I think it is better to return that called to know how serious
the matter is. Especially, we in NUPENG since we won't call on
anything frivolous. So we have managed to resolve it and there was
really no strike.
Sir, your members seem to be a heady lot. They park their vehicles
recklessly and indiscriminately on major highways obstructing
traffic. They also disregard traffic rules and regulations in the
country. Why is this so, and how is the association addressing this
issue?
Well, in some cases it is not their fault. When our members are at
fault we try to eliminate that through education of their leaders.
That they should first of all realise that the vehicles they are
driving are big, and so they are to give consideration first to other
road users and that they should not park indiscriminately. The other
one that is not their fault is if you go to Apapa area, you see a lot
of parking even on the highway, which is dangerous. That problem is
as a result of two things, one insufficiency of fuel in the northern
parts of the country, so everybody would have to come to the coastal
area to lift petroleum products for the hinterland. Unfortunately in
this country people talk about symptom without discussing about the
cause of those things. If it is in Europe, immediately they
anticipate this kind of thing, they will open up a place for them to
park. They link it up with computer gadget and then call people to
come and load. So during Adisa',s time, we had this kind of problem
that lead to strike. And they gave a place at Orile-Iganmu, an
expanse of land, sand filled place. To enter the place during rainy
season is difficult, because it is a difficult terrain and the people
having towed their vehicles many times abandoned the place and came
to the street. It is like when you say you are evacuating beggars to
a rehabilitation home and you don't give them food there, they will
come back to town. That is exactly what happens.
What is your opinion about the proposed plan by the government to
impose special tax on oil products considering that fears are being
expressed that it may be a grand ploy by government to hike the price
of fuel and petroleum products.
Well, I will say that the government should not make the mistake it
made the last time. You see from my own point of view it is not that
people cannot pay for fuel because if the price is not really
appropriate you cannot kill the black market. Black market will
thrive and people will sell at arbitrary price. The last quarter of
1998 and the first and second quarter of 1999, fuel was surplus in
this country. Black marketers were put out of business. Definitely,
they will not be happy about it and so they may be part of the
saboteurs, pipeline vandals and in the end you find out that it is
the same people who say they cannot pay certain amount that now pay
N300 per gallon for kerosine. It is painful really, but there's
nothing you can really do about it.
Also, your members have been accused of aiding and abetting smuggling
of petroleum products abroad. Is there anything your association has
done or has been doing to checkmate members from indulging in this
act.
Yes, I mentioned something which I want you to note. During the third
quarter of 1998 and the first and second quarters of 1999 fuel was
surplus in this country. So much so that even when government fixed
diesel price at N19, people were buying at N18 and N17.
It seems at the slightest excuse, your association threatens that you
are going on strike. Is this not a blackmail to perpetually impose
hardship on Nigerians. Are you giving any assurance that this will
not reoccur?
Authorities should be sensitive to issues. You see most of the things
you see in NUPENG, all those strike you see is to sensitise the
conscience of the authorities because our union is critical and if
you disband it by law you did not disband the human being any more.
So they already know what to do. So the question of disbanding it
does not arise, because it is going to be ineffective. We are using
some issues to send some signals that government should be sensitive.
Governments in this country are not sensitive. In Europe, if you go
on hunger strike people will panic. But here if you go on hunger
strike, you will die. That is a way of not punishing the public but
still sending a signal across to the government. So here, your right
is your right. Your right in Nigeria is a function of your influence
or your power. So there are certain things we do that look odd but
sensitising the conscience of the government of this country. So the
fact that somebody does not go on strike does not mean that he has no
genuine case, so listen to him. If you know that you are going to
listen to him when he goes on strike why don't you listen to him
before he goes on strike. Until we get there, we will continue to
have this problem. You know in the oil industry, we came to get
recognition through this shock treatment. Before, they will ask
workers to sign forms that never belong to unions. And workers will
sign. They need an organisation which will say, it is right to
associate, to collectively bargain. Look at the case of teachers and
the way they have been humiliated. So if we in NUPENG dont' send
shock wave till they ask question, they will not ask question. And if
they ask question, we then tell them that is not our intention to
punish the public. People came out enmasse in Belgrade last week to
sack Milosovic. If it is in Nigeria, they will roll out the tanks to
kill everybody. Everybody has a right of protest. So we must have an
organisation that must talk to the government.

Headline: Nigeria: Then Where Was Agbonma?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Perhaps she decided to take a stroll. But how can that be? It
is so unusual for her to think of taking a stroll in the morning
hours and without informing anybody?
Okunnu has just taken his seat among his friends not knowing what to
do when his in-law, Ajuluchukwu walked in. He got up and shook his
hands.
"Welcome", he said, trying to appear as cheerful as possible, but he
could hardly hide his anxiety. Without even waiting for him to sit
down Okunnu asked: "Did Agbonma meet you in the house?"
"Agbonma?" No! It was your neighbour who came to inform me that you
wanted to see me. I would have come earlier, but I had some visitors.
But come to think of it, tell me, why should Agbonma be at Umulumgbe
instead of here?"
Okunnu realizing that he had broached the subject without preparing
the ground for it, searched Okeleke Anadi's face for help as to how
to handle the the situation from that point onward.
Always equal to the task Anadi cleared his throat and began:
"In-law do not mind your in-law Okunnu, there is no problem at all.
Perhaps the kolanut he gave us earlier is the last piece in the house
and that may explain why he does not know that an in-law should be
received like a king.
Okunnu without saying a word, got up and went into his inner chamber
and then came out with two big kolanuts and presented them before
Ajuluchukwu, with these people: "my people and particularly, my
in-law forgive me because it is only the brainless man who does not
know that his brother is a guest and should be well treated."
Very curious, Ajuluchukwu did not want to concentrate on kolanut . He
thanked his in-laws for the kolanuts, took one of them and put into
his pocket saying "when the kolanut gets home it will tell its own
history". He then blessed the remaining one and while the youngest of
the men in the assembly was bearing the plate of broken nuts round
the room he returned the issue at hand.
Okunnu still worried but not willing to be careless anymore said
"there is no problem at all." It is just that I came in and I did not
see her and since she had been saying that she wanted to visit her
father's home, I just thought that must have been it." Of course,
Okunnu was telling a lie, but he really had no option.
Trying to be as relaxed as possible he continued, "after all someone
who has two ripe fruits on a tree is entitled to take them all. Your
sister may have decided to drink from the famous Umulumgbe Ngene
early this morning. In fact, you may have taken different routes and
may have missed yourselves.
"We were eating and drinking before your arrival. Here is your cup,
you may wish to join us. "
Ehe ! that is what you should have said before going ahead to worry
about a grown up woman.
Almost convinced now that nothing was amiss Ajuluchukwu joined the
party.
Before departing Ajuluchukwu went to see Mgbeoye and looking at her,
he said "I know that it is a matter of market weeks now. Your mother
should be here by now. With her and Agbonma helping you will be able
to cope. Mgbeoye nodded not wanting to say much and in the process
betray anything for she was not sure what the men discussed. Taking
that as a final conclusion that there was no cause for alarm,
Ajuluchukwu thanked her for the delicious onugbu soup and pounded yam
and then accompanied by Okunnu, and his friends, left for Umulumgbe.
Okunnu was still wondering whether to leave for Itulu to go fetch his
mother-in-law, but the thought that anything may go wrong if he left
kept him to his seat.
He brooded all day. It was almost dusk when he was roused from a
drowsy reverie by a woman's frail voice.
"Okunnu Melifonwu, Emeni Okwugo has arrived." The voice was very
distinct. It belonged to Mgbeoye's mother. She had away of speaking.
She pronounced her own name and announced her presence as though she
needed to inform those around her again and again about herself.
Someone once said it was because of her dimunitive stature which she
believed she needed to call attention to severally or otherwise
escape recognition.
Okunnu wiped his face with hands as if to ensure that he saw right
and heard right. He stood up and met Emeni at the door of the Obi.
His relief was unmistakable. At least that was one problem off his
shoulder so that he would now have the time to solve the riddle
concerning Agbonma's whereabouts.
These thoughts coursed through his mind as he watched Emeni pick up a
stool.
Shortly she sat beside him wanting to know all that had been
happening. Emeni stood out among women. She was a very peaceful
woman. She made peace, set forth suggestions to family problems
without appearing to intrude.
She did not seek to accord more respect than she deserved and for
that reason Okunnu went out of his way to please her. During
festivities he always took gifts to her.
"Has Mgbeoye been troubling you?" she inquired playfully.
"No!" he replied with mock consternation and then played along: "Even
if she meant to, she is no longer in a position to do so. But of
course you know that she is peace herself".
That was exactly what Emeni was angling to hear. She had been
wondering why Okunnu did not come to fetch her.
"Now Okunnu," she continued shortly, "I saw Mgbeoye's friend Ezinne
Okolocha of Itulu Akpa. She was the one who told me that she saw
Aduaka, you know that one that lives seven houses from you, who had
come to sell yam.
"She then inquired about Mgbeoye her childhood friend from him and he
then told her that she would soon put to bed.
"After what Mgbeoye has gone through in life, you know she deserves
all the care she can get. What am I suggesting in effect? I am saying
that I have been expecting your emissary or you to call on me. After
waiting for a long time, I decided to come. I know you cannot send me
away. Mgbeoye is my daughter and I also believe you cannot say that I
have come too early. From what I have seen of Mgbeoye since I came, I
should have come earlier."
Okunnu, now shamefaced managed to explain, "no, no Emeni Okwugo, all
is not as it seems. All is not as it seems. You are not another
person that I should lie to you or play games with you. Our gods
forbid. You see, we have been having problems with my second wife
Agbonma. In fact, she left this morning without saying a word to any
of us. I am only praying that she does not come to any harm. I have
been finding it difficult to do anything with a straight mind since
her strange ways began. Now she has stepped into a clear brook and
muddied it. Her brother, you know him now, Ajuluchukwu left here
around midday and he said that she had not been to Umulumgbe. It has
become a pain".
Emeni listened attentively and replied: " May the gods of the land
forbid that she fall into any danger."
"Ami Oh!"
It must have been midnight. The family of Okunnu was asleep and the
entire village was very quiet . Only the shrill sounds of night
insects broke the dead silence.
On her floor bed Emeni turned on her side and wondered what woke her
up. She could not immediately tell. Initially she thought it was her
newly found insomnia that was at work. But hardly had she readjusted
herself ready to sleep off again when she heard voices.
They sounded so close. The first voice, obviously that of a man
inquired of his companion, " why did I not see you at the appointed
time that day?"
"You mean the day before yesterday", the second voice wanted
clarification.
"Yes, did we not agree to meet here ?" the first voice asked,
sounding very impatient.
"You will never believe what happened that day. We had gone to fetch
water from the Ogbanku. We were having a good time, I mean all of us
who went to fetch water. There was no sign of trouble and in fact, we
had almost got back to the village when Achalaugo the sharp mouthed
one turned the discussion to child bearing."
Unimpressed, the first voice asked: "And what did that have to do
with our appointment?"
"Listen!" the other continued. "Achalaugo unknown to us had been
looking for an opportunity to tell Agbonma where her god burnt her
umbilical cord.
"Everybody was talking but immediately Agbonma said "it was a matter
for God to decide", Achalaugo retorted: "Go and sit down. What do you
know about it anyway. You may never be able to discuss this issue
with a voice of experience in your life. What I know is that it is
the atrocities that some families have committed that have made the
gods to seal up the wombs of their children from generation to
generation.
"Agbonma did not speak again. I did not know what to say. Achalaugo
having achieved her aim was enjoying herself. It was really a bad
day. It was such a callous thing to say and to say it so directly.
"When I got home, I just went to bed and was too restless just
imagining what Agbonma was going through.
"I kept wondering what if I got married and did not get my own
children, would I have to go through all that from my fellow women.
Perhaps I should never really get married. It is very scaring to
think of what horrors and evil the human mind and tongue are capable
of visiting on people!"
"God forbid! Don't be silly! You will get married and to me. People
will say what they feel but there is no one that agrees not to be
happy because of the wickedness of others. I mean, what perverted
logic are you getting carried away with. In fact, you are yet to tell
the whole truth."
Emeni lay still awhile. What? Agbonma? Which Agbonma? My daughter's
co-wife? No it cannot be?
To be continued next week.

Original_Search_Title: Why Igbos Are Not United - Prof Nwankwo
Headline: Nigeria: Why Igbos Are Not United - Prof Nwankwo
Body_Text:
Distinguished banker, author and former Dean, Faculty of Business
 Administration, University of Lagos (UNILAG), Professor Green .O. Nwankwo
 has admitted that Igbos are not united  in the pursuit of their legitimate
 demands from the Federal Government.
The reasons for the sad development, he said, is the preponderance of
youths in the Igbo population which according to him, "is very high".
Speaking in Surulere, Lagos during the Ndiakeme -Arondizuogu New Yam
Festival, Professor Nwankwo said "Igbo population is high just as the
youths are many. While elders sit behind watching and mapping out
ways of forging ahead, the young ones are easily agitated and
restive." Referring to the lukewarm attitude of the Igbos to the
Movement for the Actualisation of the Sovereign State of Biafra
(MASSOB), Prof. Nwankwo said "that does not mean elders are not
supporting them." In his remarks, the organiser of the festival,
Chief Innocent M. Okoro tasked Ohaneze Ndigbo on Igbo unity saying
"it has a role to play in the unity or disunity of Igbo people."
According to him, Igbo elders were not in the forefront of the
struggle by the Chief Ralph Uwazuruike-led MASSOB because "we do not
want to derail the new democracy in which we all have a stake." Chief
Okoro said this year's New Yam Festival was also organised in
remembrance of the great nationalist, politician and acolyte of the
"Great Zik of Africa," late Chief Mbonu Ojike "whom the government
has left unsung." Late Chief Ojike he said, in collaboration with
late Mbadiwe, established the only NITEL exchange in Arondizuogu
which has long collapsed. Chief Okoro enjoined state governors in the
former Easter Region to live in peace and harmony with their subjects
saying "in spite of the balkanisation of the former Eastern Region in
the name of state creation, we still remain one."

Original_Search_Title: Abidjan Residents Prepare for the Unknown
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Abidjan Residents Prepare for the Unknown
Body_Text:
Abidjan, CÃ´te d'Ivoire — Abidjan residents began to
restock their food supplies Wednesday, 48 hours ahead of the
publication by the Supreme Court of the list of candidates for
Cote d'Ivoire's 22 October presidential election.
"We have come to the supermarket to get our food supplies,
as tension escalates in the conflict between the ruling military
junta and the numerous supporters of political parties, to the
extent that this makes people afraid", said Marie-Andrea Kouame,
a housewife pushing a trolley full of food items at a supermarket
at the Plateau area of Abidjan.
She has not forgotten the 4-5 July mutiny by soldiers,
which prevented people in Abidjan from going about their day-to-
day chores.
"During the mutiny by soldiers last July, my children and
myself spent two days without showing our faces outside. We
had all sorts of difficulties finding what to eat", she recalls.
The mutiny officially led to the death of three people
and eight others who sustained injuries.
Ten commissioned and non-commissioned officers were
arrested and detained in the Abidjan military prison.
Another resident, Alfonso Gonzales, an Italian national who
has spent three years in Cote d'Ivoire said he needed to buy more
food as a precautionary measure ahead of the announcement of the
D-day.
He said people whose candidates fail to make the list could
raise hell.
At the Treichville market, several women said they would
buy food that would last for at least two weeks.
"You can see all these baskets filled with bananas, yams,
rice and 'couscous'. We have enough supplies for 10 or so
days. The precaution is worth taking, for with the military,
anything can happen", another resident said.
Banks too have also been busy with an influx of customers
coming to withdraw money, in the face of the uncertainty
that lies ahead in the coming days.
"Since eight o'clock, the number of clients keeps growing.
They rush in either to get the salary of last month or to
withdraw money in anticipation of possible social unrest",
Zacharie Aka, a bank employee, said.
"It is normal that we all find ourselves at the bank.
Nobody wants to run into trouble with nothing to eat during hot
days", Anatole Zago said.
Supporters of the Rally of Republicans (MDR) have
threatened to make Cote d'Ivoire ungovernable, if their leader,
Alassane Dramane Ouattara, is barred from the presidential
race.
To "avoid confusion", the ruling junta in Cote d'Ivoire,
has imposed a state of emergency and a curfew from Friday 6
October to Monday 9 October 2000.
"These measures are aimed at "preventing serious threats to
the security of people and goods in this pre-electoral period
likely to destabilise the normal operation of State
institutions", the government said in a statement announcing the
state of emergency.

Headline: Nigeria: Bash Ali's Phoney Cruiserweight Title
Body_Text:
Except for the discredited transition programme manipulated by self
acclaimed evil genius, General Ibrahim Badamosi Babangida, no other
programme has suffered postponement or cancellation than the
so-called world boxing title bout packaged by loquacious and burly
boxer, Bash Ali.
Like General Babangida, Bash Ali had the yam and the knife in his
boxing match against Terry Ray. In the publicity hype that preceded
the fight, the Edo-born boxer was the piper and was doing the paying
too.
He visited most media houses announcing that he wanted to do what no
other person on earth has done, winning a world title at an age (56)
even his late mother could not defend.
It got to the a point that the Journalists got fed up with his
assurances on the fight while the average man on the street waved off
Bash Ali with the left hand each time his name or the purported fight
was mentioned.
But last Saturday, the much postponed fight came to pass and Bash Ali
is shouting to high heavens that he has permanently shut his critics
up with not only staging the fight but becoming the "oldest' man ever
to win a world boxing title.
Surprisingly however, the media world seem to have been hoodwinked
into believing him and joined in the celebration of a victory that I
believe has no other name but a "fluke."
Even his ex-school mate, Tayo Balogun who knows all the truth about
Bash Ali is keeping mute, allowing Nigerians to be deceived or making
the international media to take Nigeria as an unserious country.
Kudos must be given to Emeka Odikpo, a former chairman of the Lagos
State chapter of the Sports Writers Association of Nigeria (SWAN) who
asked some pertinent question during his sports programme on Radio
Nigeria 2 in Lagos. If those questions could be answered by Bash Ali,
then his so-called victory could be seen in another light.
Odikpo wanted to know why a boxing contest of that magnitude was
nowhere in the Internet, not mentioned in any radio or television
network around the world except by Lagos-based newspaper, radios and
televisions. Even at that, the mention was made after unnecessary
pressure from the boxer himself who has friends all over the place.
Anyway such trash can never find its way into the pages of Sports
Vanguard.
Whether Bash Ali answers Odikpo's questions or not, authenticity can
never be given to the so-called fight in which the name of the
President, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo was smuggled in as the chairman of
the occasion. What an insult. Name dropping, you would say, all in
attempt to give the fight some credibility.
From the beginning of the boxer's hype to last Saturday, real bout,
the questions that bothered me were, why was Bash Ali the arranger,
the organiser as well as the promoter of the fight. When boxer's like
Mike Tyson, Frank Bruno or even our Heavy Akinwande had a fight, they
were not involved in the organising or promotion of their own fight
talkless of organising their own publicity.
Another dimension in the so-called cruiserweight fight was the
concern of Bash Ali in the fight that brings Terry Ray here, the
hotel he will stay and even the type of food he should eat. To worsen
the matter, the loudmouth turned master of ceremony of any occasion
he arranged for the fight.
But whether the fight is fraudulent or not, Bash Ali has beaten a
Terry Ray that is more flesh than muscle for a boxer.
It was obvious the former champion was not in shape or has been out
of the punching business for a very long time.
For those who believe in Bash Ali, he is now a world boxing
cruiserweight champion and the country can have some quiet.
The only area I would quarrel with Bash Ali is in the attempt to
sneak into the Guinness Book of Records as the oldest boxer to win a
world title. It is not honourable to inflate your age to claim a
credit that you don't deserve.
Though I am not the boxer's father to know his true age, but those
who are close to him have said Bash Ali cannot be 56 or 57 years old
that he is claiming. One such friend is Tayo Balogun whom I learnt
was the boxer's senior by one year in secondary school. Mr. Balogun
has said that he is senior to Bash and not yet 50, so how can the
boxer claim he is 57.
Bash Ali's elder sister was reported to have once said that the
boxer's claim could throw her into some kind of problem in getting a
suitor because men who hear that her younger brother is already above
50 years would do some calculations with their legs.
World records do not come by wishful thinking. You don't work towards
it, it happens while one is struggling to achieve one thing or the
other.
A good example is the story of Senator David Dafinone who qualified
as a chattered accountant and thought his children should follow his
footsteps. Today his family has made a name for itself and the
Guinness Book of Records has recognised it. Would anyone say the
former Senator planned towards this? It just happened as the family
worked along.
Bash Ali should contend himself with the so-called World title he has
won. Of course these are so many world title belts in the United
States that could rival the ones we have here at the great 'Oluwole'
in Lagos.
If you look closely at the Jankara market also in Lagos, you can get
such belts there because I'm told there is nothing you can't get
there.
But Mr. Balogun who has a popular Sunday phone-in programme on Star
FM radio should save some die-hard believers of Bash Ali's claim that
he is the oldest boxer to have won a world title, the trouble of
spreading false information to the public especially the growing
kids, by telling Nigerians the whole truth on Bash Ali's age.

Headline: Nigeria: Going Down Under
Body_Text:
Lagos — The problem with being on the color pages of TEMPO is that I
have to submit each article one clear week ahead of its publication
date so that it can meet the production. That usually is not a
problem when I am not commenting on a burning, juicy national issue
or when I am on my way to Australia!
Yeah baby! Yours truly is writing this on Thursday the 7th and by the
time you read it I shall Insha Allah be in the beautiful habour city
called Sydney. Yes right, envy me if you must, I do love the
attention.
Now, the down side is this one week gap between me writing and you
reading, yep, I will e-mail the stories but it will lack the
immediacy that sending the story two days before publication would
have.
So what do you think? Should I swallow my vanity and go back to my
good old black and white page? Well then you might as well cut off my
right hand and feed it to the dogs. I have come to like my "sisi"
picture on this page and the glamorous look of the color page so
going back to that mug shot and the guerilla journalist-like black
and white pages may just be asking a tad too much for a freshly vain
me.
So color pages and delayed articles it shall be then. Incidentally
the primary purpose of this trip to the bottom of the world is
ostensibly to go and experience and report the Olympic games which I
shall do on my sport column in the weekend edition of PM News but the
real reason baby is to have fun, period! I am going to have so much
fun I will be sick of it. I really do wish you could go with me but
hey? There is no plane big enough (oh the arrogance of Funmi) for all
the jisters so I ask you to come along on this page as I experience
for us all and write about this trip down under. I knew I had to be
at this Olympic games. First, it is the only major sporting
tournament in the world and the only one I had never been to. Then,
it is of course the first in this millennium (na you know if you are
waiting for your own millennium to start next year, free will and all
you know) and it is to be held in Australia! Australia had always
sounded to me like Siberia, one of those places you just do not take
off and go to. Now, mind you, I inherited my father's Ajala spirit I
intend to go around the entirety of the earth, all countries at least
once before the good Lord calls me. I have oft said it that if He
calls me before then, I shall vex and return as an airplane, at least
then I shall get to fulfill my dream. So, I knew I must see the games
in Sydney for if I miss this chance, how am I sure that I shall get
another opportunity to go there. We after all are not talking about
Europe or the Americas, which are a reasonable distance away, this is
another world entirely. I am told that I shall fly for two days and
almost twenty-two hours through Brussels and Zurich, which is the
route I have chosen.
I have no idea what Australia is like except what I have gathered
from the internet but of course any seasoned wakabout will tell you
that all those brochure pieces are never exactly as it seems also the
places that interest me most are hardly the cosmetic tourist
attractions of any city. For one, I intend to find a local Nigerian
food joint as soon as I can. What do you mean I can't? Do you not
realize that Nigerians can be found in such unbelievable places as
Afghanistan and unpronounceable, thus unspellable little cities in
impoverished Russian enclaves? Why would there not be an appreciable
Nigerian population in a big cosmopolitan albeit dead far city like
Sydney. I know there must be a joint were Nigerians hang out and were
on can get a decent plate of Ogbono, Edikanikong, Ewedu with pounded
yam. At the least, there will be a pepper soup joint, trust me on
that. However if against all expectations, there are no such places
in Sydney, I can bet my last Zuma (sorry, no name rock) that some
smart Nigerian business woman would open one whilst the game lasts.
That was my experience during the world cup in France two years ago.
Even with all the gastronomical delights the city of Paris had to
offer, we the chosen people of God from Nigeria began to hunger for
some real food, a few swallows here and there and a plate of soup
with some soul.
On frantic enquiries, we were told that there was in fact one place
in town where we could get "Nijer" food served without any
pretensions. I cannot remember anymore what part of town it was we
went, all I recall is that we took the tube and came out into this
nice prosperous looking neighborhood and walked a few blocks to a
little alley which turned out to be the living room of an apartment.
The apartment was crawling with carpenters, mason and such who were
making such a din as they knocked the place into shape.
At the end of the alley was a little backyard like place with chairs
and tables set in typical Nigerian food joint manner. There was
makossa music playing in the background above the cacophony of voices
speaking in various Nigerian languages as the aroma of food wafted
through the air. I knew I was at home. I recognized quite a few of
the other customers as officials and journalists from Nigeria who had
also come for the World Cup. How come they discovered this place so
quickly? I wondered. Then I found out that the woman was just setting
up the place. She came with the Nigerian contingent with a mobile
kitchen and rented the apartment just for the duration of the
championship! She realized there would be a great demand for Nigerian
food and decided to open a joint in Paris whilst the world cup was
on. I asked if the investment was a worthwhile one and was assure
that she would make enough money to cover her cost, make some profit
and get a holiday and shopping in Paris to booth. Her food was very
pricey but we were too glad to have it to care.
I never found out if she did come back home after the world cup. If
she did I wonder if she would be in Sydney to give us her pricey, not
very great but glad to have it anyway brand of home cooking.
So, it is that I leave you in peace for a working holiday for the
next three weeks. I will be free of the Lagos traffic and drivers. I
will be free of NITEL, NEP, the word nascent and the sight of burnt
bodies on the road. I will not be afraid to park or go out in the
evening for fear of armed robbers. I shall have a ball. However, I
shall miss the madness, the drama from Abuja and my TV show, which
has run me, ragged around the edges. I shall miss Nigeria for you
know what? Even in all of the madness, insecurity and want, I find
comfort and an identity. The most dreadful thing about being outside
Nigeria is that people look at you but they do not see you.
You are completely unimportant, just one of the crowd, a mostly
unwelcome part of the crowd, it is an unsettling eerie feeling.
I discovered how truly essentially Nigerian I am when once in Harare,
as we drove around town I wondered unconsciously but loudly why there
were no street hawker so I can buy a snack to munch on in the car.
The biggest "Nijer dey for blood" joke occurred in Chicago when one
morning I woke up and began to iron my clothes. I had ironed half of
the clothes in my box when my girlfriend asked in alarm if I was
going away, why was I ironing so many clothes at the same time. It
was then it dawned on me that NEPA (sweeter than NEP) was not about
to take the light so I had no cause to iron clothes in "advance"! May
God keep you too.

Original_Search_Title: New Anti Crime Outfit Underway in Delta
Headline: Nigeria: New Anti Crime Outfit Underway in Delta
Body_Text:
Asaba, Delta — A new anti-crime outfit coded "operation storm" is to
be launched soon in the Delta State. Vanguard gathered that the new
outfit will replace the existing one known as "Operation Wipe,"
launched by the former police commissioner in the state, Mr. Morris
Evanson, a few years ago.
Addressing top officers of the state police command at the end of a
two-week training programme in Asaba, the police commissioner, Mr.
John Ahmadu, said the training was meant to prepare the officers and
men for the execution of the new police offensive code-named
"Operation Storm."
He explained that officers and men that would execute the new
offensive needed to be groomed to prepare them for great challenges
ahead. He advised them to live above board to be able to restore the
confidence of the public on the police. Said he: "You have to be
polite to the people on the road. Greet them good morning sir, good
afternoon sir; where are you going, can you identify yourself, please
do you have an ID card for me to identify. If he brings it and after
checking whatever, you say "thank you sir, have it back sir, bye,
sir". And I can assure you that if people see you behaving like this,
you would not be surprised if somebody brings you one motor load of
yams.
Close sources hinted that the setting up of the new offensive was at
the instance of the governor, James Ibori, who had on several
occassions expressed concern over the rising crime wave in the state.
Already, no fewer than 25 pick-up vans have been procured by the
state government in readiness for the take-off of the new outfit in
the state.

Headline: Nigeria: War Against Militant Groups
Body_Text:
Lagos — More than the allegations of arbitrariness and miscarriage of
justice, there are other factors that many have informed the
directive from Federal Government to state Police commands to clamp
down on militant groups in their areas of operations.
The regular press briefing of the Lagos State Commissioner of Police,
last Tuesday, witnessed a radical departure from its usual course.
That day, Mr. Mike Okiro, the commissioner, was not in the mood for
the exchange of banters that usually preceded the business of the
day. Rather, the police boss was serious and furious. The opening
paragraph of his prepared statement said it all.
"The Lagos State Police Command has observed with dismay, the
unfortunate events and incidents of man's inhumanity to man, wanton
destruction of lives and property of innocent citizens within Lagos
metropolis, including the unprovoked killing of law enforcement
agents, police officers and well meaning people of Lagos State by a
group of hoodlums who have christened themselves Oodua People's
Congress (OPC)," Okiro remarked. Having set the stage for the day's
agenda, Okiro recounted series of recent embarrassing activities of
the OPC that are currently pitching it against the state. Among
others, Okiro accused the largely South-west Yoruba based
organisation of engaging in senseless murder of innocent citizens in
the guise of fighting robbery. He cited instances of victims of the
group's latest acts in Ajangbadi, a suburb in Ojo Local Government
Area of the state. The commissioner also alleged that some members of
the organisation are often used to settle scores by parties involved
in disputes.
On this instance, he recalled the case of one Mrs. Sibat Eyitayo, a
resident of Aguda, Surulere, who, earlier in the month, was attacked
by persons suspected to be OPC members. Her assailants were hired by
one Mr. Maurice Amonye of number 8 Nureni Street Ipaja, to unleash
violence on her because she was indebted to him. The police chief
equally cited cases in which the OPC had in the last couple of days,
made life meaningless to the residents of the state by holding
certain areas and individuals to ransom.
These, Okiro frowned at and remarked that the state police command
would not shirk in its statutory responsibility of protecting the
lives and property of residents as well as maintaining law and order.
The commissioner capped his address with a warning "that the
existence of democracy does not and will not imput a state of
lawlessness or disregard for the laws of the land or the fundamental
rights of law abiding citizens."
Not many, who had keenly followed the unfolding events in the state,
in the last couple of days, were surprised by Okiro's riot act.
In fact, if anything in their estimation, the reaction from the
police boss was rather belated. Two embarrassing activities
attributed to the OPC prepared the ground for the police sudden
response. There was for instance, a bloody clash between Okiro's men
and members of the ethnic organisation last Sunday. The clash, which
had the trappings of a battle, cut across Itire, Mushin, Mile 12,
Oshodi, Surulere and Agege areas of the state. Death toll in the
fracas was put at over 20, with the police loosing two of its men. 18
people were reportedly arrested in connection with the mayhem. The
actual cause of the clash remain sketchy. But a source told The Post
Express on Saturday that trouble started when police authorities
refused members of OPC in Bariga to hold an unauthorised meeting in
the area. This did not go down well with the organisers of the
meeting who, in trying to have their way, fell out with the police.
Before the Sunday episode, a clash, the previous Thursday between the
largely Igbo traders at Alaba International Market, Ojo, and members
of the OPC, had left about 15 persons dead. The crisis was triggered
off by the killing of an Igbo boy, Ikechukwu Obi, by members of OPC.
The late Ikechukwu was said to be having an outstanding rent dispute
with his Yoruba landlord.
The latter took the case to court where it was adjourned till July
27.. Before the date of the scheduled hearing however, the landlord
was said to have lodged complaints to the OPC, labelling the deceased
a robber. Without proper investigation, the members of the militant
organisation allegedly pounced on the trader, killing him and setting
his corpse ablaze. The news instantly filtered into the electronics
market. Promptly, his colleagues asked for justice and in the
ensuring skirmishes, a full blown riot erupted, which with the
passage of days almost pitched the Igbo traders and their Yoruba
hosts at different camps. The state police command laboured to
restore order. The state Governor, Bola Tinubu, condemned the wanton
destruction of lives and property, threatening to impose curfew on
any trouble zone.
On their own, the leaders of the OPC have always claimed that the
organisation is only being blackmailed by mischief makers. On several
occasions, Kayode Ogundamisi, the secretary-general of the outfit has
maintained that members of his group do not harm innocent people.
"What the OPC has done is to make sure that the neighbourhood is kept
safe," he insisted.
Giving the impression that his organisation is a benevolent type,
Ogundamisi held that they were forced into dealing with robbers
because of police copious inadequacies. He said specifically that the
Nigeria police "are underfunded and do not have enough fire power to
face these robbers."
Those that have favourable sentiments for the organisation easily
throw up this explanation as a strong reason for its activities.
Before it began being enmeshed in personal and group agenda, the
argument had worked for OPC. Today however, the genuiness of the
explanation appears to be a matter of conjecture and generally
depends on who is involved.
All the same, similar organisations that profess elimination of
crimes as their main objective, are springing up in different parts
of the country. Apart from crime detection and prevention, they are
also used in protecting group interests. Prominent in this class are
the South-east based Bakassi Boys, the Ebgesu boys of the South-south
and Arewa Peoples Congress (APC) of the North.
A common denomination, on the surface, appears to be running through
the organisations. They are essentially informed, on the one hand, by
the glaring inability of the police to provide the needed protection
and the desire to protect group interest. The Post Express on
Saturday, gathered that these dual functions, informed the invitation
of the Aba based Bakassi boys to Onitsha, Anambra State, by Governor
Chinwoke Mbadinuju, recently. Before the arrival of the dreaded gang,
the commercial city was virtually a no-go area. Robbers struck both
at day and night making insecurity the order of the day. On two
occasions within an interval of three days, Lagos-bound commercial
luxury buses were attacked, over 40 passengers killed and millions of
naira taken away. Within the streets, similar situations prevailed.
The citizens became helpless and began to flee the town. And the
police had no response. Some of their men were even accused of
conniving with the men of the underworld. Forced to the wall
therefore, Mbadinuju had no alternative than to reach out to the
Bakassi squad whose earlier exploits in Nnewi spoke volumes. The Boys
promptly responded to the invitation and in a matter of days, their
operation began to yield results.
Even the hardened robbers fled the town. Those that were not lucky
were apprehended, thoroughly screened, made to confess their sins and
matcheted to death. All over the city and beyond, people heaved a
sigh of relief.
But the Federal Government is no longer amused with the messianic
roles ascribed to the vigilante organisations. Accordingly, it has
mapped out strategies to clamp down on all of them, irrespective of
what the people think about their individual activities and
objectives. Acting through the Inspector- General of Police (IGP),
Musliu Smith, the authorities are said to have directed state police
commands to descend on all militant groups operating under the guise
of vigilante groups. Apart from the allegation of the groups being
used to settle personal scores and in the process, killing innocent
citizens, the IGP was said to have reminded his colleagues that it
amounted to a vote of no confidence on the police for the militant
groups to be seen as the real outfit maintaining law and order. Smith
specifically directed that militants who lynch suspected criminals
should be arrested and charged for murder.
Signs of the latest directive began to emerge last week, when Smith
was invited alongside the Minister for Police Affairs, General David
Jemibewon (rtd), to a Federal Executive Council (NEC) meeting. A
source at the meeting disclosed that President Olusegun Obasanjo
after assessing the security situation in the country, gave the
police a low performance rating. He, particularly was said to be
miffed that both Jemibewon and Smith had allowed their men to
abdicate their statutory responsibilities to the unconventionally
trained militant groups. Pointedly, he was said to have told the two
henchmen of the police to either sit up or be prepared to get the
boots.
The duo, The Post Express on Saturday learnt were not only ears as
Obasanjo read the riot act. They told him the police perceived
incompetence in executing its duties had nothing to do with inability
on the part of their men but had to do with a combination of
personnel and infrastructural inadequacies currently hitting the
force. They reminded the president of the problem of obsolete
equipment and shortage of personnel with which the police had had to
contend with over the years. The IGP specifically told the president
that his men had not abandoned their responsibilities to militant
groups but had all along seen these organisations as complementing
their efforts. However, he was said to have assured the president
that with the rate at which some of them were going, measures would
be taken to curtail their excesses. It was in carrying out this
pledge, our source learnt, that the directive from police high
command was issued to the state.
However, beyond the factor of drawing the attention of the police big
