wigs to the uncomfortable exploits of the militant groups in crime
eradication, Obasanjo is said to be concerned that the rate at which
events are unfolding in the country, some of these militant groups
could be turned into regional armies and in the process threaten the
corporate existence of the nation. Certain ÂÂ experiences in the
recent past were said to have combined to inform his fears. In the
East, West and North, there had been incidents that had made him lose
days of sleep.
There had been, for instance, the recurrent activities of the OPC in
his native South-west. Apart from the two most recent cases of
violence involving the group, it had in the past acted or been
portrayed in lights that made the government jittery. There was for
instance, the late November 1999 showdown between the group and a
segment of Hausa traders at the popular Mile 12 market in Kosofe
Local Government Area in Lagos.
A leadership tussle among some members of Sukura Yam Sellers
Association in the market had degenerated into an ethnic warfare
between the Yorubas and Hausas.
Before calm was restored after two days of fierce fighting, the death
toll fluctuated between 40 and 60. Visibly angered by the rate of
destruction, Obasanjo ordered that from then "anybody who calls
himself OPC should either be arrested or shot on sight if he resists
arrest."
Obasanjo's, warning to his kinsmen was as well intended to inform
other ethnic organisations that they were not above the laws of the
federation.
Barely two months later, militant Muslim youths in Kaduna State
engaged in acts that suggested that the warning was not heard or not
meant for them. Following the controversy generated by the state's
attempt to introduce the Islamic legal code, local youths unleashed a
regime of terror to the Christian adherents in the state.
Incidentally or by design, the Igbos were the hard hit. As the death
toll rose, the South easterners realised that as in the past, they
had fallen victims to an issue that was not of their making lorry
loads of decomposing corpses brought into Aba, the commercial nerve
centre of the east and incidentally, the base of the Bakassi boys,
brought out the ire in the group. Instantly, they went for revenge
killings. But peace was soon restored.
Interestingly, while the groups battled against themselves or went
for reprisals, the law enforcement operatives were caught napping.
For one thing, they appeared better organised and possessed superior
fire powers to the police. Thus, analysts with critical bents have
been quick to observe that whatever peace that may be prevailing in
the country, with the militant groups fully intact, may not last the
test of time.
"What we have, right now, could be likened to a balance of terror as
we had in the days of the cold war" observed Mr. Dick Uduma, a
lecturer in Political Science, Abia State University, Uturu.
According to the don, "with the massive arms build up by these
associations, the country could as well be said to be sitting on a
keg of gun power. In that case, any thing could happen at anytime."
His fears are not in vein. Rather recurrent incidents in different
parts of the country give bite to them. In the north, for instance,
the governors of the Sharia states are not shifting grounds from
their rigid position. As a counter force, the southerners are
throwing away their petty jealousies and suspicions of one another
and coming together to form a formidable force against the Sharia.
There is therefore the fear that when conditions entirely slip out of
control, individual blocs may fall back on the militant groups in
their fold to pursue their agenda. Analysts with a strong sense of
history and international relations easily agree that that was how
the crises that are presently consuming Somalia, Yugoslavia, Congo
Republic and Sierra Leone, began. Obasanjo, a major actor in the
search to bring out peace in these trouble spots, does not lose sight
of this. On Tuesday, he raised an alarm that the ultimate objects
behind the formation of these groups as well as the Sharia haze were
an attempt to embark on secession by the blocs involved. It was to
nip these suspicious moves in the bud that the president called on
the police to sit up to their constitutional roles.
Obasanjo's marching orders could sound well in ordinary times. "But
these are not ordinary times," retorted Haruna Ismail, a lawyer, at
his "Gaskiya" Chambers, Ikeja, Lagos. He told The Post Express on
Saturday, that "in situation as we now find ourselves, security
becomes a matter of personal responsibility. Whatever a particular
group puts up to guard itself may not be genuinely faulted."
His questions were, "if the OPC, The Ebesu, The Bakkassi, the APC, as
you call them are liquidated, who do the people turn to in periods of
crises? ? Is It the army who have been forced to the barracks or the
police who are busy collecting bribes on the roads?" These questions
are yet to be answered.

Headline: Nigeria: Ndigbo As Nigeria's Village Fools?
Body_Text:
Lagos — A lot of people were stunned at how quickly the Ohaneze and
the governors of the states in Igboland took to the rostrum to
dissociate themselves from Chief Ralph Uwazuruike and the Movement
for the Actualisation of the Sovereign State of Biafra (MASSOB) when
they took their non-violent campaign to the flag-hoisting level.
Some, if not all of us can vouch that all that they said so far is
not beyond the ordinary: That Ndigbo do not have a sense of belonging
in Nigeria. The Ohaneze itself, even some of the specific individuals
who constitute it have said the same thing themselves by calling it
the marginalisation of Ndigbo in Nigeria. In fact, across the Niger,
our neighbours have not hidden what can best be termed their own
resolve to opt out of Nigeria altogether if the existing political
arrangement is left un-addressed. Which explains why they founded the
O'dua People's Congress (OPC). And ever since the OPC stepped into
the mix of things, no right-thinking Yoruba has stepped out to
dissociate him or herself from it in any form or shape. Which is in
fact, the way it ought to be. As a bosom Yoruba friend of mine told
me here recently, the least that a group like the Ohaneze that calls
itself an apex body or any reasonable Igbo could have done about
MASSOB is to maintain an unusual silence while we watch to see how
the kite will swoop down to drag off the dwarf.
Not only that the Ohaneze's quick negative reaction gave me a lot of
cause for wonder, it equally worried me as well. And since the toad
is never known to embark on a race at noon time without a cause, I
was compelled to embark on my own investigation to discern why. Prior
to all that, I was privy to information from some privileged sources
that former dictator, General Abdulsalami Abubakar had made a
clandestine trip to Igboland. That was a little before the flag
hoisting by MASSOB. In fact, my findings indicate that in the course
of that trip, Igboland was not the only southern area that he
visited. The other leg of that same trip took him to northern
Yorubaland where he was sighted at the house of General Olanrewaju. I
found out that while he preferred to visit Igboland under the cover
of darkness, he was not concerned at all being seen in northern
Yorubaland in broad day light. But I'll return to that shortly. Also,
I found out that the former dictator's trips were conceived by
leaders of the Caliphate society. I must quickly mention that
Olanrewaju was one of those who was almost slaughtered by the Abacha
regime in which Abubakar himself held some crucial positions. In view
of that I may not have been the only one who wondered why the former
dictator visited a man who they almost killed in a phantom coup plot.
Well, some of the other things I found out about Abubakar's trip to
Igboland especially about the fact that some of those who he met with
in Igboland are prominent elements of Ohaneze are enough to convince
me that the witch that shrieked in the dead of the night has
something to do with that neighbourhood child who was found dead the
next morning. The other thing is that the present occupant of Aso
Rock is terribly distressed about what the former dictator said in
Igboland. What I tried but could not ascertain yet are these: Whether
the occupant of Aso Rock is intent on discerning who actually sent
the former dictator on those trips. The other thing is what he
intends to do about it. But if you ask me to speculate, I'll quickly
tell you that he probably may be aware of the faceless clique behind
the trip. And that he'll simply be unable to do a thing about it all.
He can only be distressed. My reason? He has refused to appreciate
the underlying dynamics of the goings-on around him. In fact, he has
always been. But back to my main story.
Please, don't fall off your seat because of what you'll hear next.
Abubakar went to Igboland specifically to canvass some of the
so-called Igbo leaders on what he termed the urgent need for Ndigbo
to close ranks with the Hausa-Fulani and gang up against the Yoruba
to save Nigeria from disintegration. That wasn't all. He tried to
take those he canvassed back to history about how Ndigbo had always
ganged up in the past with the Caliphate rulers against the Yoruba.
He referred specifically to the times in the late 1950s, in 1964 and
in 1979. He termed what took place at those times as indications of
age-old friendship between Ndigbo and the Hausa-Fulani. Indeed!
While my guess is that the same mission may have taken him to
northern Yorubaland as well, I can swear in the name of my ancestors
that he was not too concerned about being seen in broad day light in
the home of Olanrewaju. Which tallies with what I found out. Which
says a lot about the mindset of those who sent him out on that
mission. I can tell you that as far as northern Yorubaland is
concerned, they still regard the place as part of their legitimate
domain.
I haven't found out what he received as a response from those he
canvassed in Igboland. But some of those people who he visited with
are in the leadership of the Ohaneze which quickly went to the podium
to dissociate Ndigbo from the MASSOB. Which brings me to what I
consider the dreadful signal in the former dictator's foray into
Igboland and what followed on the heels of his trip. You see, in the
part of Igboland that I come from, the yam used to cook snake meat is
usually eaten in a free-for-all manner by every passer-by. And
because the village fool is without regard in the community, he's
always the one who is cajoled into bringing out his yams for a snake
meat feast. Which worries me. Does it imply that Ndigbo have gotten
to the point where the rulers of the Caliphate society now have the
confidence to approach us, to convince us to bring out our yam for
what can be similar to a thankless snake meat feast?
I know for sure that the leaders of the Caliphate society are in
distress over the stirring all over the South. I also know that they
lack the moral courage to press a case for the continuation of their
disastrous hegemony over the other peoples of Nigeria. Hence, they
have settled on a ploy to use tricks to sway Ndigbo and the
inhabitants of northern Yorubaland away from the rest of the South.
Their ultimate aim here is to isolate the Yoruba.
But while I will not waste my time to counsel the Yoruba on how to
check the forays into their backyard, I won't hesitate to alert the
youth of Igboland on why Ndigbo must not allow anyone to cajole us to
bring out our yam for a snake meat feast. The talk of a traditional
enmity between the Igbo and the Yoruba is all a myth. Let me ask you
this: How many times have Ndigbo been slaughtered in numbers in any
part of Yorubaland? The answer is nil! Refer to the history books if
you may, counting from 1945, there have been more than ten times when
Ndigbo were victims of successive acts of organised violence in the
North.
The former dictator or anyone at that has no business sneaking around
Igboland under the cover of darkness distorting the facts of history.
We, Ndigbo are hurting in Nigeria there is no doubt about that. The
generality of us understand that. It is cause for joy that our youth
are rising to reassert the dignity of Nd'Igbo in Nigeria. The yam for
the snake meat feast will not come from our barn. If it was so in the
past, it will not be so today. God and our ancestors will forbid.
E.C. Ejiogu is of the War, Peace, and Military Organisation Programme
at the Center for Research on Military Organisation, University of
Maryland, College Park, Maryland, U.S.A.

Original_Search_Title: AIDS Rampant Among Low Income Earners
Headline: Nigeria: AIDS Occur Among Low Income Earners, Says CFH Co-Ordinator In Calabar
Body_Text:
Calabar, C'river — The scourge of the dreaded HIV/AIDS has been found
to be rampant among people of low economic status compared with the
well-to-do.
The co-ordinator of society for family health, in charge of Cross
river and Akwa Ibom states, miss Victoria Inyang, told newsmen in
Calabar on Tuesday that spot studies in the two states had shown that
low-income earners contracted HIV/aids more than those of high
economic status.
Inyang described the victims as living under "compromised immunity
because they do not have access to better healthcare and are careless
about simple hygenic teachings".
She listed the victims to include cyclists, mechanics, petty traders,
hawkers, long-distance drivers and residents of border towns. She
attributed the high incidence of HIV/AIDS among the population
mentioned above, to certain traditional festivals, such as new yam,
age-grade ceremonies and vigil night during funeral rites of dead
persons in villages.
She explained that during such festivals, most persons in villages,
especially men, tended to exhibit their sexual prowess indulging in
indiscriminate sexual behaviours with multiple sex partners.
Others found to have high prevalence of HIV/AIDS were students in
tertiary institutions, military and para-military personnel and
commercial sex workers, she added.
On the activities of her organisation, Inyang said that the society
engaged in counselling, distribution of printed messages on the
hazards of HIV/AIDS, in addition to researches into the mode of HIV /
AIDS spread among people of different ethnic groups.
She counselled intending couples to always know their HIV status
before getting into marital union to avoid embarrassing consequences
in future.

Headline: Nigeria: BBC Management Opposes Cementia
Body_Text:
Lagos — The feud between the Benue State government and the Bureau
for Public Enterprises (BPE), following the choice of Dangote Group as a
core investor in the Benue Cement Company (BCC) has taken a dramatic
dimension as the management of BCC has petitioned the Federal Government
that it would resist the come back of Cementia/Lafarge to the company.
The Director-General of the BPE, Alhaji Nasir El-Rufai, made this
disclosure Saturday in a chat with the newsmen in Kaduna after the
Federal Radio Corporation of Nigeria (FRCN), hosted him as the Guest of
a popular Hausa programme, Hannu Dayawa (meaning too many hands).
Said he: "I have a nine-page letter from the management of Benue
Cement saying that they will never work with Cementia because Cemential
abandoned them and insulted them in 1985 and said that when they leave
they would not be able to produce cement but bake Gboko yams."
El-Rufai, therefore, stated that the brouhaha by the Benue State
people as a result of the sale of the 35 per cent Federal Government's
shares to Dangote remained sheer "hypocrisy and the reasons being given
by Benue State government is all a bundle of nonsense and chasing
shadows."
The director-general argued that the opponents of Dangote's core
investorship were doing so not out of genuine concern but because "he is
a Fulani Moslem, pure and simple," describing such people as
"ethnic/tribalists" who do not believe that "every Nigerian has an
opportunity to go and live anywhere in Nigeria and invest anywhere in
Nigeria."
He described Dangote as a whizkid who has the managerial capability
and expertise to turn around the moribund cement company.
"Dangote is qualified. He has come with sound technical partners. He
gave us highest price and in our view he is a young man that should be
encouraged to continue investing anywhere in Nigeria. He has more of his
investment and facilities in Lagos. He has done very well. He has
employed people there. He has created jobs. His activities in
commodities sector has brought down the prices of rice and sugar and we
hope he will use his acumen to bring down the prices of cement," he
enthused.
El-Rufai also declared that the entire transaction which favoured
Dangote Group was transparent.
"The people that oppose him are just being tribalistic. They are not
looking at the facts. What we have done was totally transparent and I
challenge anyone to tell us what we did wrong," adding, "the Federal
Government is free to sell its shares to anyone that it wants. We are
not going to sell shares to Cementia at N4.50 when we have a Nigerian
willing to buy it at N5.35. We are not mad," he stated tersely.

Headline: Nigeria: June 12 And Enemies Within Democracy
Body_Text:
Lagos — History is after all a very useful subject. No matter how
disguised, no matter the amount of secrecy, no matter the magnitude of
bribes at the point of committing an ignoble act, history unstoppably
catches up with agents of reprehensible deeds and passes its cruel
verdict.
That has been the lot of those who signed away in secret the June 12
Presidential victory and until each of the men {thank God there was no
woman except late Justice Ikpeme of doubtful memory} can produce
evidence to the contrary, they will remain in the Black Book of
Nigeria's history.
The scoop in The Guardian of June 11, 2000 was not entirely news to
many in the Nigerian media. Many of those who signed away Nigeria's
peace, progress and prosperity by signing away the expressed wishes of
over 14 million Nigerian citizens, were known. But now, the
comprehensive list of the makers of Abacha, the harbingers of woes for
millions, bringers of premature deaths for hundreds and false
imprisonments for a lucky few, have been named. Stand up for
identification please, Augustus Aikhomu Babangida's dupe 'civilian' No
2 and Shonekan of the illegal National Government (ING).
Alhaji Rimi, Dr. Olusola Saraki, Dr. Ahmed Kusamotu, Bushir Dalhatu,
Tom Ikimi, Joseph Toba, Joe Nwodo {a reluctant signatory}, Okey Uzoho,
Dr. Bawa Slka, Theo Nikire, Prof. Eyo Ita and Bola Afonja should also
join them. Also to stand up for identification are Y. Auka, Abba
Muntala Mohammed, Alhaji Ramalan, Alhaji Halilu Maina and Dr. Odunze.
The soldiers, Brig. Gen. Ukpo, Lt. Gen. Dongonyaro and Brig. Gen John
Sagaya are also members of the gang. May the good Lord forgive deceased
Musa Yar'Adua, Abdulrahman Okene and Amos Ikulade, whose names also
occur in the list. Also to stand for identification are Senators Jim
Nwobodo {make up box and all) and Gen. David Mark of former
Babangidaist. From Gen. Obasanjo's cabinet let the ministers Adamu
Chiroma {Finance}, Tony Anenih{Works & Housing} and Dapo Sarumi {Co-
operation in Africa or whatever} and Sule Lamido{Foreign Affairs} join
their colleagues. Patrick Dele Cole who advises the President on
Foreign Affairs and Babangida's National Security Adviser, Lt. Gen.
Aliyu Mohammed who is also President Obasanjo's National Security
Adviser should join his colleagues.
These men owe Nigerians lots and lots of explanations. We need to
know why each of them still living individually and severally put their
pens on paper to sign away June 12 and sign on corruption, death,
hunger, strife, what have you. What inducements were offered by the
self-confessed evil genius? Whose mandate were they exercising at the
point in time they took their actions, since there was no meeting of
any recognised organ of the parties at that time? To think of how many
innocent souls their signatures had sentenced to gruesome deaths, how
many homes have been destroyed, how much property damaged, how far back
we have been sent as a nation on the path to progress and prosperity,
is to confront the issue in rage. But we must give them room to explain
how the yam rotted and their knives still peeled them. Probably they
were blackmailed. Probably they were misled. Probably. Probably. The
world cannot wait to hear their explanations.
But what happens until those explanations come? For those who are
dead amongst the men, we can only leave them in the courts of the
Almighty God. But a question mark hangs on the suitability of those
living to continue, unrepentant, as public office holders. There is no
guarantee that given another opportunity they will not betray the
people again as they did in the closet of the past darkness. So, the
best they should do is to resign honourably and pray for the nation's
forgiveness. There are no two ways about it. They have repudiated
democracy and have no moral justification, whatsoever, for being key
players in the new democratic process.
Of course, being politicians they would likely hang on with such
traditional excuses like quota system and the marginalisation of the
constituencies they represent. Here, the Nigerian polity should become
wiser. The polity should ask itself whether hunger, disease and want
have ever disappeared because a particular son-of-the-soil has been
installed in a government job. Besides, and this is significant, there
is no part of Nigeria today which cannot boast of ten, if not better,
of least, equal replacements for any single office holder at any level.
It is a matter of conscience. May be those concerned have facts we
don't now know which will change their present public image. Let them
speak up and speak up very very loud. The nation is all ears.
Meanwhile, the press will be doing the nation a great service if it
spikes all reports on the enemies within democracy, unless the event
concerns their confessions and sincere apologies.

Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Price War
Body_Text:
Lagos — Emeka Alex Duru, staff writer takes a look at the factors that gave
rise to the fuel price hike, bringing to the fore, the attendant consequences
and reaction from Nigerians.
By the close of work on Wednesday, it had become apparent that the Federal
Government of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, had failed in its last ditch efforts to
halt a scheduled nationwide strike ordered by the apex leadership of the
Nigerian Labour Congress (NLC). The indefinite sit-at-home exercise was billed
to commerce on Thursday, in the event of government delegation and the NLC
failing to reach an acceptable agreement on the outrageous new fuel prices,
unilaterally imposed by the government. The government through its major oil
marketing agency, the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation, (NNPC), had, late
last week, without prior negotiations with relevant and concerned bodies, jerked
up the prices of petroleum products by over fifty per cent. By the new price
regime, a litre of Premium Motor Spirit (P.M.S), commonly referred to as petrol,
was raised from N20 to N30. A litre of diesel was hiked to N29 from the previous
price of N19. While kerosine was jerked from N17 to N27 a litre.
Initially, not many look the rumoured price increase seriously especially as
it was not backed by any official statement from the NNPC. By Friday, a day
after the embarrassing decision was taken however, Mr. Jackson Gaius Obaseki,
the Group Managing Director (GMD) of NNPC, laboured to explain the reasons
behind the measure. Throwing up a combination of emotions and what senior
government officials referred to as the facts of the matter, the oil chief held
that the hike remained the only option for his corporation to stay afloat. He
claimed that the price increase would not have been contemplated but for the
fact that over 75 per cent of fuel consumed in the country is imported. While
this, he said, was not in his original agenda, it had to be resorted to
following the failure of the NNPC machinery. A barrel of imported crude he said,
sells at $20 instead of $10.
Given this regime of "massive importation to meet up", Obaseki said it would
only amount to self deceit for the pump price to remain at N20 per litre. "It
was obvious that if we continue the way we are going, it becomes a suicidal bid,
he sated. Giving the impression that the government was even magnanimous in its
new price, he explained that "if we are to recover our cost today, you all know
we should be looking at a situation where you will be having between N35 and N40
per litre of petrol.
In a manner that apparently sought to replay previous governments' stated but
largely unkept pronouncements, Obaseki said the hike is temporary. According to
him, the old prices would be reverted to, sometime around August when the
nation's refineries would have been back on full stream. By the human face
approach, the NNPC obviously intended to mollify the visibly enraged Nigerians.
But this did not achieve his desired results. Shortly after the new prices
were unfolded, transports fares and costs of other basic needs hit the roof
tops. Within Lagos metropolis for instance, short distance journeys that
previously attracted fares of N5 and N10, were hiked to between N10 and N20
respectively. Between Oshodi and Apapa which used to cost N20, the fare was
jerked up to N40. Inter-city fares were also inflated. A journey from Lagos to
Ibadan that used to go for N80, was almost doubled, to between N130 and N150.
While the trunk journeys from Lagos to the East which in times of lull like now,
ordinarily cost between N600 and N1000 depending on the state of the buses,
attracted between N1000 and N1,500. As the transport fares jumped up, prices of
food stuffs and other household communities followed suit. At the popular mile
12 market in Kosofe Local Government Area, Lagos sates, tubers of yams that were
previously sold for about N50, went up to N70, while a small basket of tomatoes
that sold for N150, attracted N250. Mallam Ismaila Uman, a Hausa trader, who
attributed the price hike to the regime of fuel prices laboured in vain to hide
his frustration with the situation of things. "Oga", he referred to The Post
Express on Saturday, E no dey easy. You see this price, me, I no like am. But na
government (pronounced gwomant) e cause am. Before, we dey pay N15,000 to bring
tomato (lorry load) to Lagos, Now, the price (fare) e don go up. This one you
dey see, I bring am yesterday (Wednesday). I pay N23,000. Na him make me put
money. But (bat) I no like am, customer no dey buy", he lamented.
Elsewhere, the situation was the same. Tensions were visibly written on the
faces of Nigerians . Pockets of resistance and protests were reported in major
cities of the federation especially in the South-west. In Lagos, hoodlums and
street urchins commonly referred to as Area boys,had a field day. Apart from
joining genuine students in disrupting the sale of petroleum products, at
filling stations, they cashed in on the confusion brought by the situation, to
dispossessed innocent but stranded commuters of their belongings. All eyes
looked up to the government to take charge of the situation and rescind its
decision on the new fuel price. But nothing happened. Neither was anything
intended to happen.
So, the organised labour took up the gauntlet. An emergency meeting of the
Central Working Committee (C.W.C), of the NLC was hurriedly summoned in Lagos,
on Saturday. It was presided over by comrade Precious Kiri Kalio, the NLC vice-
president. At the meeting, the C.W.C reasoned that "the decision of the Federal
Government to increase the price of fuel was most insensitive, inconsiderate and
punitive to the citizens of the country especially the workers and the masses of
Nigerians". The increase, Labour observed was "inconsistent with the assurances
of the Federal Government that it will consult with the NLC on the very volatile
and sensitive issue of petroleum prices".
The C.W.C recalled the position of the organised labour that, should the
government effect any price increase unilaterally, the action should be resisted
with every conceivable means. It therefore regretted that the "government, by
its action has compelled organised labour to react accordingly." The union
consequently directed "all Nigerian workers to commence a sit at home strike by
Thursday, June 8 if the Federal Government (failed) to reverse the outrageous
increase".
The order was communicated to all the state branches of the congress, who, in
turn signified their intentions and resolve to abide by it. To give vent to the
directive, Adams Oshiomhole, the President of the NLC, hurriedly cut short his
visit to Geneva, Switzerland, where he was participating in the annual
International Labour Organisation (ILO) conference. By Monday, he had already
flown into the country to take charge of events. His arrival gave tremendous
impetus to the resolution of the C.W.C. True to expectation, he embarked on
fool-proof mobilisation campaign for the strike. His efforts instantly paid off.
By the close of work on Monday he had receive assurance from the NLC branches in
virtually all the states and Abuja that none was about to sabotage the strike.
Emboldened by the show of solidarity, Oshiomhole, fixed a rally at Abuja. The
Post Express on Saturday, learnt that the turnout was overwhelming. In a rare
demonstration of fraternity, workers at the Federal Government Secretariat,
abandoned their duty posts to take part in the exercise. They even asked for the
strike to commence a day earlier than scheduled.
With the tremendous support from Abuja, Oshiomhole scheduled a similar rally
for Lagos. One factor strongly worked in his favour. Majority of Nigerians were
emphatic in rejecting the new price hike. Even some of those that were thought
to be in the same corridors of power with Obasanjo, disowned him on the issue.
While the new price regime made the rounds, the National Assembly washed its
hands off the debacle. Both Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, President of the Senate and
Alhaji Ghali Na'Abba, Speaker of the House of Representatives, blamed the
Presidency for the unwarranted increase. At a deferent forum, they expressed
surprise that despite a provision of N104 billion made by the Assembly in the
year 2000 budget to subsidise fuel the Presidency went ahead to effect a price
hike.
Na'Abba was more emphatic on the topic. He said, in the 2000 budget, we
provided for N104 billion to ensure that government does not withdraw the so-
called subsidy. This, we note, was accepted by the executive.
Even during the harmonisation of the grey areas in the budget, the executive
did not raise any objection to it. We are therefore at a loss to understand the
need for the current price increase. This is nothing but a breach of trust".
Other commentators toed this line of thought. Even the Alliance for Democracy
(AD), which, of late, appeared to have assumed the ignoble role of defending
every of Obasanjo's actions, frowned at the increase. Through its national
Publicity Secretary, Valentine Ohams, the party descried the increase as a
devastating economic assault on the already impoverished Nigerians. The Academic
Staff Union of Universities (ASUU), added its informed voice to the floodgate of
criticisms. Its National President, Dr. Dipo Fashina, said that price hike
"contradicts everything that a government ought to do if it is serious about
combating poverty". Chief Gani Fawehinmi, the effervescent Lagos lawyer, simply
saw the act as Obasanjo's efforts at poverty elongation". Many also spoke in
this direction.
Thus, faced with robust opposition on all fronts, Obasanjo was literally left
in the colds. His tiny and insignificant band of supports included Chiefs Bola
Ige, Arttoney-General and Minister for Justice, Philip Asiodu, Chief Economic
Adviser and other top government functionaries who support him were informed
more by the sycophantic efforts at retaining their jobs than facing the
realities of the time. His party, the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), made
feeble attempt to defend him but its voice was drowned in a sea of opposition.
Even the governors deserted him. They were embarrassed that the President could
take such a fundamental decision without taking them into confidence. More so,
they were ill prepared for the likely fall-outs if the action. So they left him
on his own.
As a face-saving measure therefore, the President made a complete U-turn to
embrace dialogue that he had previously ignored. He called for a meeting with
the NLC on Tuesday, at his Abuja residence. At the meeting, The Post Express on
Saturday learnt, Obasanjo adopted various strategies to sway the NLC leadership
to his side of argument in raising the fuel price. When this did not yield the
desired results, he injected subtle threats of cutting down the size of Federal
Government work force if he was forced to slash the fuel price. Even this did
not work as the NLC emphatically told him that by contemplating this action, he
would be courting the anger of all workers in the country. At this juncture, our
source revealed, the government delegation asked to be allowed to reduce the
price regime to N25 per litre of petrol. Two strong considerations were said to
have informed this posture. The government asked for the new price to enable it
minimise the cost of subsidising the products. There was also the ego factor. In
this regard, the government reasoned that a total reversal to the status quo
ante would portray it as being lily livered. To its opponents, it assumed it
would appear as being unserious and inarticulate in formulating its policies.
This would therefore be used as an instrument of blackmail against it. To keep
its head therefore, it asked for the N25 price both as a concession on both
sides and as a factor of prestige in politics. However, this argument hit the
brick walls as the Oshiomhole - led NLC executive told the President point
blank, that the union would not accept anything other than a total return to the
old selling price of the petroleum products. At this stage, our reporter stated,
there was a stalemate and the meeting was adjourned, till the next day,
Wednesday.
At the resumed parley, nothing of substance was achieved, Obasanjo, goaded by
a military antecedent that brooked no opposition, stuck to his gun. On its part
the NLC, carrying the mandate of Nigerians, did not blink. By the close of the
day, no agreement was therefore reached. Thus the final stage was set for the
showdown.
And as programmed and anticipated by labour, the face-off was total. Even
non-government employees took part in the sit-at-home strike. Apparently for
fear of losing their wares to hoodlums, traders and shop owners did not turn up
for business. Private and commercial vehicles kept off the roads. Even the
nation's air spaces were closed to both in-coming and out-going flights.
Even with these tough measures, the Presidency, by Thursday afternoon had not
rescinded its decision on the fuel price hike. those that claim deep knowledge
of Obasanjo swore that given his stubborn background and the prodding of the
International Monetary Fund (IMF) officials, he might not be in a hurry to
reverse the price. Rather, if given the chance, he would seek a further hike.
Indications to this line of thought began to manifest themselves at the early
stage of the administration shortly after being installed last year, the
government floated the kite of the fuel price increase . However, it then, said
the appropriate pricing" or deregulation of the products would be handled by the
major oil marketing companies, in line with the market forces of demand and
supply. Soon after the government unfolded the agenda, public outcry ensued.
NLC, with benefit of hind sight suspected that the administration, like its
counterparts in the past, was up to a mischief. It promptly reacted. Oshiomhole
led a workers delegation to the National Assembly complex Abuja where they
registered their protest with the law makers. While the delegation sought to
carry its message to major streets in the metropolis, it was teargassed by anti-
riot police. The delegation was dispersed, but it had achieved its mission. The
government beat a temporary retreat from the policy.
Some months later, the fuel serpent began to emerge from its hibernation.
Chief Asiodu lifted the lid for its dangerous re-appearance shortly after the
yet- to-be implemented wage increase pronouncement last month, the presidential
adviser, gave hints of possible job cuts in the federal civil service unless
something drastic was done. Analysts then saw him groping towards fuel price
hike as the only route at shoring up the federally generated revenue. In
presenting his recipe, Asiodu, who in the past had presided over NEPA then known
as the Electricity Corporation of Nigeria (ECN), for over ten visionless years,
pretended to be guided by national sentiments.
However, unknown to many, he and the President were out to play the scripts
authored by the IMF and the Paris Club. These are made up of a group of leading
Western countries to which Nigeria is heavily indebted. The two bodies were said
to have provided a lot of inputs into the government's economic policies. IMF
specially insists that complete withdrawal of petroleum subsidies remains the
only viable route for the country to recover from its prostrate economy. Given
its fine text book postulations, Nigerians would not have been in a hurry to
doubt the IMF elixir. But its antecedents in the country had been catastrophic.
And so its presence in the nation's economic decisions is always seen as a bad
omen.
It made its ignoble incursion into the country's economy in 1981, when it
goaded the civilian regime of Alhaji Shehu Shagari to adopt austerity measures.
The hardship occasioned by the policy, provided the face-value reasons for
Major-General Muhammadu Buhari and his band of coupists to sack the regime in
1983. At inception, Buhari did not give the Fund much room to operate. So, he
threw open a nation-wide debate for its loan to be accepted or rejected. A
preponderance of opinions tended towards its rejection. But the government was
yet to call its bluff when Gen. Ibrahim Babangida struck and displaced Buhari.
Through the back door, Babangida embraced IMF and a Structural Adjustment
Programme (SAP), was imposed on the country. With the programme began a massive
inflation occasioned by persistent devaluation of the national currency, the
naira. The late Gen. Sanni Abacha's tenure 1993-1998, completed the circle of
IMF induced economic suicide for the country.
Based on these unsavory backgrounds, those that oppose the fuel price hike
may have a point. ASUU president believes that by the measure, " the government
appears to be more concerned with pleasing the IMF and the Paris Club and other
clubs of money lenders than with the welfare of Nigerians". Obasanjo and his
advisers are yet to debunk this allegation. He appears bent on his programme of
"poverty elongation". If he succeeds, he would after all be toeing the line
paved by his predecessors in office. Babangida did it in 1986 when he hiked the
price of petrol from 20k to 39.5k Two years later, the argument of "appropriate"
pricing and the need to check smuggling", was again used to increase the price
to 42k. Twice in 1989, it went up to 60k and 70k. Ernest Shonekan's Interim
National Government, raised it up to N5 with diesel going for N4.75k. Abacha
after his coup, temporarily brought it down to N3.25 only to hike it to N11.00
later in his regime. The Gen. Abdulsalami Abubakar regime which handed over to
Obasanjo, initially pushed up the prices of petrol to N25 per litre with diesel
and kerosine going for N23 each. However, following widespread demonstrations,
the government brought down the prices of petrol to N20, diesel N19 and kerosine
N17 a litre . Even at these prices, Nigerians have not been finding it funny.
But Obasanjo seems unperturbed. Close watchers of his tactics predict that in
raising the price of petrol to N30, he was only trying to act out what his
predecessors had done. The thinking here is that what he had in mind is N25 per
litre. So, by dangling the N30 option, the workers would, themselves ask for a
N25 per litre Abacha and Abubakar succeeded in the gamble, perhaps, due largely
to the environment under which they operated. Both were military dictators.
Obasanjo on his own, has a military background, In fact, he once called the
shots as a military Head of State. However, whether these credentials would work
for him in the current dispensation remains a question which only time can
answer.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger, Tension Increase In Besieged Kisangani
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Hunger, Tension Increase In Besieged Kisangani
Body_Text:
Kampala — Tension caused by renewed reinforcements, deployments and
arrival of big arms in Kisangani has created fears here of a fresh
outbreak of clashes between Ugandan and Rwandan armies, reports Emmy
Allio from Kisangani.
The civilian population trapped by the war is now unable to afford
basic necessities like salt which now costs US$2 (sh.3,200) per packet
of 500 grammes. A piece of pineapple now costs about a dollar (sh1600).
Around Kapalata, 14kms on the Kisangani-Buta road, civilians refused
to sell their fresh cassava tubers and yams over the weekend.
Ugandan soldiers said the Congolese now want to barter salt for
foodstuffs.
"I think we have no option but to fly in more salt to barter for
food," a UPDF soldier said.
The local population now blames "foreign armies" for the suffering
and destruction of property. They are demanding that the United Nations
plays a more active role in forcing the former allies-turned
adversaries out of the city.
Besides two UN flights last week, there have been no other flights
into Kisangani since May 4. Salt, sugar, petroleum products and
medicine are scarce as prices sky-rocket daily.
The rate of a dollar to Congolese Francs has dropped from 36 to 25
in the past fortnight.
Authorities in Kigali and Kampala have agreed to demilitarise the
ruined city of over a million people.
They have also agreed to withdraw their troops to a distance of over
200kms out of the city, and leave only companies of 200 soldiers.
In the agreement, the Rwanda-backed RCD-Goma rebels are also to
withdraw. But RCD-Goma's military commander, Jean Pierre Ondekane, on
Friday said his forces would not withdraw.
Col. Sula Semakula, the UPDF commander in Kisangani, said "What is
known as the RCD-Goma the army is in fact RPA. The Rwandans have never
recruited Congolese into army.
"The agreement to demilitarise is null and void if the Goma group
does not pull out."
Uganda and Rwanda army chiefs say the situation in Kisangani is
unavoidably hostile.
Attempts by Col. James Baxter, the chief of staff of the UN Observer
Mission in Congo (MONUC) to bring Col. Semakula and his Rwandan
counterpart, Lt. Col.
Steven Kalyango, to a round table flopped twice last week.
UPDF-controlled Bangoka International airport, about 4kms from La
Forestiere, is being besieged by RPA who have dug-in along the southern
and eastern sides of the runway. The rival armies are separated by a
runway and its apron measuring about 100metres. Sources said the RPA
has brought in two fresh battalions to Bangoka since both countries
agreed to cease fire. UPDF soldiers say RPA's control of the Kisangani
city centre is in violation of the agreements to end the clashes.
But the RPA are trapped in the city as they cannot get supplies.
The main road to their supply route is being held by the UPDF.
Ugandan soldiers said RPA now uses the River Congo to ferry supplies
from the Bafwasende-Lubutu junction.
Lt. Col. Patrick Kiyingi, commander of UPDF's 9th battalion whose
forces man the bridge, said the situation is tense and fighting may re-
erupt anytime.

Original_Search_Title: Mr. President, You Can Do More For The Judiciary (Editorial)
Headline: Ghana: Mr. President, You Can Do More For The Judiciary (Editorial)
Body_Text:
Accra — President Jerry Rawlings last Thursday expressed grave concern about
the quality of some aspects of justice being administered in the country. He
also spoke about the inadequacies in the judicial system. We are glad about the
President's frankness but The Dispatch thinks the President can do far more for
the Judicial Service than currently pertains.
Is he aware that quite a number of the newly-promoted judges do not have
court rooms? Is he also aware that some of the judges lack certain basic office
equipment? Has he had a look at their salary scales?
Has the President bothered to find out why many lawyers are not attracted to
the bench? Apart from the poor and unattractive salary structure, the lack of
proper accommodation is also a deterrent.
In addition to improving the lot of the Judiciary, the President must upgrade
the working conditions of the Police, since an efficient Police Service makes
the work of the Judiciary easier.
Regarding instances of what may appear to be lenient bail conditions for
certain crimes, the Attorney-General's Office must ensure the reform of some
laws which will make such things difficult to occur. Someone who has stolen
three tubers of yam may be denied bail and in the end, sentenced to a minimum
jail term of six months. The trial would be over within a month. People who may
have been in fraud totaling Â¢1 billion could be bailed for Â¢2 million. The trial
may even drag on for months.
Mr. President, we appreciate your frustration but you have the power to
improve the situation. The ball is in your court!

Headline: Nigeria: HIV And The Abala Cure
Body_Text:
Lagos — Joseph Abalaka, M.D. has been in the news in recent times.
is much-trailed therapy for the cure of persons ailing from the Human
Immuno-deficiency Virus (HIV)/ Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome
(AIDS) has split public opinion down the middle.
In the event, very little about the debate is clarified by the
advocacy of the contending parties.
The Health minister's eagerness to resign if anything of substance
comes off Abalaka's claim is as unhelpful as the latter's professed
disposition to inject himself with the dreaded virus in a bid to prove
the value of his cure. In as far as Dr.
Abalaka claims for his therapy, the ability to reverse an HIV
sufferer's sero-prevalence, it ranks alongside the combination drug
therapies, based on protease inhibitors, that are currently popular in
the west. Thus far, it scarcely extends the frontiers of scientific
knowledge.
But it promises a great deal at another level: it challenges all
known therapies on price. Whereas current anti-HIV remedies cost
upwards of $12000 (about N1.2million) per annum, Dr. Abalaka claims for
his therapy the humble cost of N25,000 These obviously are the details
that matter.
For if this claim were true, it bespeaks some advance in the
compounding of drugs. Lamentably, the contrasting declarations made for
and against the likelihood of Dr. Abalaka having chanced on a nostrum
of such powerful import overshadow the central issues.
More than any other, both the ministry of Health and the claimant
should know that procedures exist for substantiating the veracity of
such claims. Indeed, a couple of years back, the countries of the OECD
agreed on common standards for conducting the clinical trials on which
these procedures are based.
Doubtless, such procedures have been expedited in the past
Â­especially for some of the present anti-HIV treatments. But it is
essential that Dr.
Abalaka's claim demonstrates fidelity to this process in order that
it be taken seriously. For starters, he ought to file a patent
application (describing both the idea behind his treatment and the
method for working it). It is then expected that Âpre-clinicalsÂ -
tests on laboratory animalsÂ­to ascertain the drug's toxicity (how much
collateral harm it does to the recipient) and bioavailability (how well
it is absorbed and delivered to the point of need) are conducted and
recorded. ÂPhase twoÂ and Âphase threeÂ trials (testing on humans)
follow from this.
The basic requirements for these Â­large numbers of HIV-infected
people, and a fair network of relatively sophisticated teaching
hospitals and related medical facilitiesÂ­are already in place.
Fundamentally, the task is to determine the category of patients to
administer the drug to, when and how, while all the time ensuring that
a statistically reliable result will be obtained therefrom.
Conducting these tests and arranging the result in a way that could
convince regulatory authorities to give the necessary approval for the
commercial development of the drug demands that it be given to a great
number of people who suffer from the disease the medication is designed
to cure. Similarly, some or any of the existing HIV intervention
therapies must be administered on an equal number of persons.
This latter set must not be aware of the fact that they are not
benefiting from the new drugs. It is for this reason that the search
for a commercial development of a new drug is a very costly process.
And about a third of this cost, which is put at around $500 million by
the leading pharmaceutical companies, is accounted for by Âclinical
trialsÂ.
Which is where the government comes in. It is improbable that Dr.
Abalaka can muster anything like the sums needed to go through this
process unaided. Thus once he demonstrates himself amenable to the
idea, it behoves the federal authorities to help speed things up, to
lend financial assistance and to guard the patent on this jealously.
Sadly, this prospect is soured by two traits local to the Nigerian.
The most off-putting is the penchant for not taking ourselves
seriously. On the other hand, those in charge of our healthcare system
have not handled the matter with the urgency it deserves. No one is
saying that Abalaka should be given a carte blanche.
But for crying out loud, a desperate disease calls for a desperate
cure. The danger ultimately is not that Abalaka might turn out to be a
quack.
Actually, this would give a rather desirable outcome. It does not
only settle the matter one way, it would be of immense relief to those
who argue that the euphoria over Dr. Abalaka's claim will deflect from
the larger message of the anti-AIDS campaign.
The possibility instead, if at all there is anything to Dr.
Abalaka's claim is that as it happened with the yam-pounder concept,
this idea might yet see better days outside this shores.
Publication Date: May 11, 2000

Headline: Africa: HIV And The Abalaka Cure
Body_Text:
Lagos — Joseph Abalaka, M.D. has been in the news in recent times. His much-
trailed therapy for the cure of persons ailing from the Human Immuno-deficiency

ourselves seriously. On the other hand, those in charge of our healthcare

will deflect from the larger message of the anti-AIDS campaign. The possibility

Headline: Africa: End The HIV/AIDS Cure Controversy
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is about time that the claims by Dr. Abalaka that he has
developed an effective vaccine for reversing HIV infection be scientifically
ascertained and the raging controversy laid to rest in the overall interest of
the nation.
Claims on the discovery of cures for HIV and full blown AIDS are not strange
in Nigeria. Unconstrained by the rigid rules of scientific proof required of
orthodox western trained doctors and unaffected by their code of ethics that
punishes advertising with deregistration and expulsion by the Nigerian Medical
authorities, every roadside herbalist claims a cure for not only HIV but full
blown AIDS. Unfortunately the media, driven by the need to generate revenue,
have been less circumspect in airing such unverifiable and unverified claims.
Thus when Dr. Abalaka came on the scene his claims could not but evoke doubts.
But Dr. Abalaka is not another roadside herbalist opportunistically
exploiting the AIDS scourge to fleece off the public. He is a trained medical
doctor registered to practice in Nigeria. Thus, his claims to have developed an
effective vaccine against HIV infection does not fall into the hocus pocus
category of the herbalists. Besides, the corroboration of the effectiveness of
the ABALAKA VACCINE by very senior military officers who had sent soldiers
certified to have tested positive to HIV during their peace-keeping operations
for treatment which they claim had been successful, raises the level of
seriousness of the claims. In contrast, however, the Federal Minister of
Health, another trained medical doctor has emotionally refuted Abalaka's claims
and the mandarins in the Health Ministry have been generally sticking to the
official script that Dr. Abalaka's claims are frivolous.
It should be recalled that this is not the first time a Nigerian makes a
break-through in science or technology on the home turf only to be denied and
defamed by his colleagues and ignored by the government. It is on record that
in the mid-70s one student Fagoyinbo of The University of Ife (now Obafemi
Awolowo University) invented a yam pounding machine which was used to produce
good quality pounded yam for visiting General Obasanjo the then military Head
of State. There were encomiums and songs of praise. But there it all ended
until the Japanese smuggled out the technology and produced an improved version
of the machine which were massively imported into Nigeria and paid for in
foreign exchange. The same ineptitude and prodigal neglect were exhibited over
the first successful Nigerian test tube births and the invention of an
effective cholera vaccine by Nigerians in the private sector. This raises the
suspicion that professional rivalry borne out of the fact that those who
receive official funding only hold seminars and talk shows while those without
such motivations produce results. It could be surmised therefore that the
policy of one's rival never killing a full-grown game might be at work in these
matters.
A continuation of this trend is undesirable for the nation. It weakens the
intellectual development of the country, deepens the nations embarrassing
dependence on foreign scientific products and technological breakthroughs,
devalues the African image in the world and contributes largely to our poverty
in the midst of plenty. An effective made-in-Nigeria HIV vaccine, for instance,
could off set our national debt in a few years, beyond making Dr. Abalaka rich.
It is said that so far, no arm of government has shown special interest in this
matter. We therefore, call on Mr. President to first facilitate the patenting
of Abalaka's vaccine and second set up a high powered panel of neutral experts
to evaluate the claims on HIV vaccine. The on-going controversy must end
soonest and it must not become another yam-pounding machine experience.

Headline: Africa: Workers Nationwide Mark May Day
Body_Text:
Lagos — Workers nationwide will mark today's May Day amid expectation that
the Federal Government will announce a new minimum wage.
President Olusegun Obasanjo is expected to address workers at a rally by the
Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) at the Tafawa Balewa Square in Lagos. Also
expected at the event are the Senate President, Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, the Speaker
of the House of Representatives, Alhaji Ghali Na'Abba and Lagos State Governor,
Senator Ahmed Bola Tinubu.
It is expected that Obasanjo may use the occasion to announce a new enhanced
salary package for civil servants nationwide.
Speaking on a new salary package for workers yesterday, an NLC official
called for a review of the 1981 legislation pegging worker's minimum wage at
N250.
Mr. Chidi Nnorom, who is the Chairman of the National Union of Air Transport
Employees (NUATE) said in Lagos, shortly before a meeting of the NLC's Central
Working Committee (CWC) that the new minimum wage would serve as a benchmark
for future hikes.
Amid speculations of a raise of the minimum wage to N7,500 and allegations
of a sell-out by labour, Nnorom said whatever was agreed upon now would not
debar further negotiations. He said that the new rates, which would be backed
by laws, would serve only as benchmarks for unionized workers.
"When you talk of minimum wage, it is meant to protect only unionized people
and serve as a guide to individual unions in their negotiations with their
employers," he added.
On the N7,500 minimum wage being touted, Nnorom said he was not aware that
any amount had been fixed, saying that the NLC's CWC was meeting to get briefs
from its negotiating committee on the matter ahead of today's May Day
celebrations.
He urged workers to exercise restraint on their clamour for jumbo wages and
enjoined them not to allow their ranks to be broken.
The labour leader also urged extreme tact "in dealing with the person who
has the knife and the yam", noting that apart from increased wages, workers
needed an improved transportation system, housing and social services. On his
expectations today, Nnorom said" "it would be a day for sober reflections and a
day to remember our colleagues in the struggle."
In his message, former presidential aspirant, Alhaji M. D. Yusuf warned the
political class that unless the pervading poverty among the citizenry is
urgently stemmed, more Nigerians will be disillusioned with civilian rule - a
situation which, he warned, may trigger violent ethnic and religious conflicts.
Yusuf also berated the civilian administration for the inability of the
legislative arm of government in some states and at the Federal level to work
for the overall good of the populace one year after being elected into office.
He said though today is "historically a workers' day, May Day is also for
national reflection.
"There is no doubt that the last twelve months have been a trying period for
our political leaders. In this context, people can understand that many
mistakes will be made both from the side of the executive and the legislature
in the first few months of civilian rule.
"But a situation where for one year, the executive and legislative arms of
government, in some states at the Federal level have not developed the capacity
and the political will to work together for the well-being of the people of
Nigeria, is to say the least, disappointing. The situation becomes even
shameful when we consider the fact that both the executive and the legislative
arms of government are largely under the control of a single party, the Peoples
Democratic Party, PDP", he stated.
Absolving the PDP of the blame, he noted that the party has entered into an
alliance with the two "opposition" parties - the All Peoples' Party, APP and
the Alliance for Democracy, AD to facilitate the smooth working relationship
between the executive and legislative arms of government.
Yusufu however regretted that the two arms have failed to agree to work
together. He said the government has, for instance launched a Poverty
Alleviation Programme but that there was nothing on the ground to indicate that
it is taking off several months after.
"As the poverty of the majority of the people get worse, there is an attempt
to divert attention from this shameful paradox of a very rich country with very
poor citizens, whose rulers are receiving larger and larger amounts of revenue
which can be used to improve the living conditions.
"The three registered political parties in control of the executive and
legislature are jointly responsible for the situation we are in. The party
leaders and their elected and appointed public officials do not show any
serious concern about these terrible conditions of the citizens of this
country", Yusufu observed.

Original_Search_Title: KCC Wants City Of Floods, Cholera? (Opinion)
Headline: Uganda: KCC Wants City Of Floods, Cholera? (Opinion)
Body_Text:
Kampala — Although I am not in the hotel business, I agree with the two
central statements made by our Town Clerk Gordon Mwesigye in his article
"Kampala Not City Of Yams, Monkeys" The Monitor, April 10.
On the one hand, he argues, we need favourably priced hotels. On the other
hand, he asserts a little bit further, we need the environment to work for the
people.
I fully agree with the latter, and all our efforts in the National Wetlands
Programme are especially aimed at that. I am also prepared to agree with the
former because I do not doubt the ability of Golf Course Holdings to make some
sensible economic projections on their investments, in this case a hotel and
shopping centre on the edge of Kampala's most famous wetland.
Why, then, do I react and run the risk of being accused of issuing
"instinctive," "populist" and other unnameable utterances on this topic?
Because our Town Clerk fails to link the two and bring them to a logical
conclusion. During World Wetlands Day, the Town Clerk put up a passionate case
for Kampala's swamps. He explained to 200 participants and a discussion panel
that in the 1900 Agreement, the swamps were purposely left out of the mailo land
because everybody well understood that they should be left alone.
They were, and are today, draining the city's storm water and discharging it
steadily into Lake Victoria. In the process the silt and dirt is filtered, and
the water entering Murchison Bay is relatively clean. Is this important? Yes,
because it is from the same bay that we pump our drinking water.
The more dirt and filth we discharge into the bay, the more effort National
Water and Sewerage Corporation has to put into its waterworks to give us safe
and clean drinking water.
In addition, 90 percent of Kampalans are not connected to any sewage
treatment system, and if it were not for the few wetlands that sit in between us
and the lake, we would by now be drinking our own sewage. The Town Clerk knows
this very well, as he so aptly demonstrated during his lecture in the Sheraton
Victoria Room.
What then does he mean by "developing our swamps without destroying them"?
Does he have in mind the Bwaise and Kamwokya suburbs that are literally built
in the swamps, and which were hit by disastrous floods in August and September
last year?
Has he forgotten the 46,000 cholera patients at the height of the flooding?
Or does he have in mind the UMA showground, conveniently built in the middle
of a swamp and being flooded during every serious rainstorm?
Or does he picture the mindless in-filling currently going on at the railway
yard and the sports ground near the Clock Tower?
Mr. Town Clerk, where do think the water will go when you "develop" all the
low lying areas in the city?
It won't disappear. On the contrary, it will flood your roads, factories and
residential areas. It will float the contents of pit-latrines and sceptic tanks
into your gutters, streams and protected springs, in which our children play,
our women do their laundry, and from which our families fetch their drinking
water.
We know that in these modern days, arguments are won on financial grounds,
not on soft issues as sick children, and a dirty Murchison Bay. Well, according
to figures from the Ministry of Health, last year's cholera outbreak cost the
government over $ 2m (over Shs 3bn) in medicines alone.
The value of Nakivubo Wetland's tertiary water treatment is conservatively
estimated at Shs 2.3bn per year. We could also calculate the costs of road
repairs, damages to houses and industries and lost investment opportunities
because of flooding, and the Town Clerk's $ 15m suddenly become peanuts.
Oh, yes, what about the badly needed hotels?
We would strongly advise the Town Clerk to attract as many hoteliers to build
as many hotels as possible. But why the heck build one in an area that is one of
the last beautiful stretches of green in the city?
And, more importantly, why build it on a spot that absorbs large quantities
of storm water that, when it finds its original home filled-in, for sure will
find its way into Jinja Road and the Wampewo roundabout.
Mr. Town Clerk, the bitter truth is: water cannot be simply wished away.
* Reint J. Bakema is Chief Technical Advisor of the National Wetlands
Programme Support Project.

Original_Search_Title: Bank of Ghana cheques bounce 10 times
Headline: Ghana: Bank of Ghana cheques bounce 10 times
Body_Text:
Accra — China State Construction Engineering Corporation Ghana Ltd has
presented a cheque valued at over Â¢240 million ten times for payment at the
Bank of Ghana. In as many times the cheque bounced.
The Chinese Government owned company has undertaken major construction
projects in Accra and is presently renovating State House, popularly referred
to as Job 600. It has also been awarded a contract to construct the Alajo
drainage works around Accra's Kwame Nkrumah Circle area.
Further Chronicle investigations at CAL Merchant Bank revealed that
ENERGOPROJECT, another European construction company based in Accra is facing
financial embarrassment following a similar experience of dishonoured cheques
drawn on Bank of Ghana cheque. A cheque for Â¢318 million it presented for
payment has had problems with Bank of Ghana tellers for more than a month
having been rejected on a number of occasions. The cheque, issued on the 24th
of February, 2000 and credited to the company's account at CAL Merchant Bank
has bounced and as at April 5, 2000 the cheque had been so defaced that there
was no space on it for the 'Return to drawer' stamps usually administered on
cheques by banks.
Incidentally, that particular Cheque is part of the payment of a multi-
billion cedi new office for the Ministry of Finance. Energoproject has also
undertaken major projects for government including the Accra International
Conference Centre, Cedi House and the ongoing high rise building opposite SSNIT
Accra headquarters offices.
Another company similarly hit by these recurrent incidents of bounced
cheques is Razel, a French Construction Company. Currently battling serious
cash flow problems which is threatening jobs of construction workers. There
were fears expressed by workers visited by Chronicle at some of their sites
that they will be laid off soon, and some of the expatriate staff confirmed
their precarious condition.
Perhaps the company that may be hardest hit is the one that has had a
chequered history of run-ins and dalliance with the Government, the originally
German owned Construction Pioneers, previously called Carl Ploetner.
Chronicle learn that they are owed in excess of Â¢40 billion and were
recently forced to lay off workers to stave off collapse. A management staff
confirmed this information to The Chronicle at their Korle Lagoon project site
in Accra last Friday.
The Director of Budget at The Ministry of Finance, Major Tara said his
Ministry does not issue cheques but only approves of Government expenditure
after approval by the Controller and Accountant General.
His Public Relations Office however said that Government revenue for the
first quarter of this year was so low that it was exhausted before March and so
the Ministry if now having to wait for Parliament to approve their estimates
before their budget could be approved, adding that it was their hope that the
problem would be solved.
He explained that the Governor of The Bank of Ghana was so bent on asserting
his independence to curb excessive Government spending.
Dr. Kwabena Duffour has established a reputation of doggedness since he
assumed office, and has a very tight monetary control policy.
The Controller and Accountant General Mr. Tuffuor refused to answer the
question put to him directly in a note slipped to him by The Chronicle through
his secretary last Friday. He asked that this reporter should go and write a
proper letter.
Bank of Ghana official Secretary Mr. Odei said he is not authorised to speak
on issues even though traditionally BoG Secretaries had acted as Spokesmen. He
said he was not going to deny that their cheques have been bouncing. He also
asked for formal letter.
This is not the first time that cheques issued by the State have been
dishonoured by Bank of Ghana.
Last year, cheques hurriedly issued to a horde of angry contractors who had
besieged the Ministry of Finance because of liquidity problems.
Dr. Duffour at the time was quoted to have told the contractors that 'There
is no money here. Go and ask The Minister of Finance whether one send people to
go and collect yams from a yam barn into which no yams have been sent'.

Original_Search_Title: Goatherdism And the Rise of Village Tyrants
Headline: Kenya: Goatherdism And the Rise of Village Tyrants
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Definitions: Goatherdism is an ideology characterised by
two main features. First, it is a system of appointing and
disappointing in defiance of merit, and on the basis of tribe,
sycophancy, illiteracy and inability.
Secondly, it is the application of rudimentary, subjective and
irrational principles of goat husbandry to the complexities of
bureaucracy, government and society in general.
Village tyrant: One who is guided by this reasoning. All hens belong
to the cockerel. I am the cockerel. Therefore, all hens belong to me.
One particularly goatherdistic cockerel, called Mobutu Sese Kuku wa
Sabanga, or something in that general category, had a long name that
meant: the cockerel that never pauses to catch its breath so long as
there is a hen still on its feet.
Having dispensed with that, let us get to specifics. There is a bunch
of hooligans, thugs, layabouts and idlers that calls itself Mungiki.
This thuggish Mungiki thinks that every woman on earth should follow
its wishes - the majority of which are illogical, harmful, atavistic,
others plain stupid.
For example, it thinks taking snuff is sexy and we should all be
pushing our forefingers up our noses. They also believe that all
women should be subjected to FGM. Equally, it entertains the stupid
notion that trousers are immoral and should not be worn. Well, if the
Mungiki are men enough (and women enough), why don't they take me on
rather than assaulting innocent women? I wear trousers, too.
These are goatherds, defying the law, attempting to impose their
contemptible wills on society and deserve nothing but the most
vitriolic vilification.
If the Mungiki can answer this question, then their dictates can be
put in the law and we shall henceforth conduct ourselves in
accordance with its wishes: What do they think qualifies them (a)
judge what is, and what is not, moral? and (b) what makes their
peripheral definition of morality generalisable on the rest of
society?
There you have it my snuff stuffing good people.
Here's another. Cabinet minister Isaac Ruto has accused the Press of
malice and distortion. He has denied, very strongly, that certain
things earlier reported and has protested that they were a figment of
the media's fevered imagination and have no relationship with the
truth whatever.
It had been reported that he had boasted of not only causing certain
civil servants to be transferred, but was also in the process of
transferring others who were not either lining his toe, or toeing his
line - I forget which.
His attitude, as reported in the Press, was not only ungentlemanly
and tribal, but it showed a person with little or no concept of the
objective workings of a government. One steeped in the belief that,
for example, because he is a minister, all civil servants in his
political principality of Bomet are his to command, fire, transfer
and brag about.
I cannot but wonder how a man of these qualities would end up in the
Cabinet and what possible contribution there is for him to make to
the nation when his world-view is so insular, so jingoistic.
Like I said, the minister has denied saying these things and the
argument is, therefore, academic.
All the same, I trust that Mr. Zakayo Cheruiyot, the Permanent
Secretary in the President's Office in charge of security and
provincial administration, as well as the Civil Service boss, Dr
Richard Leakey, understand the need to shield junior civil servants
from goatherdism.
Equally, were I a civil servant and were forced to choose between
committing allegiance to the whims to a man who will humiliate me and
brag about it in public, I'd choose to go and grow yams in Meru.
Here's yet a third goat driver, a fellow in Cote d'Ivoire who has
been refusing to grow up. He overthrew the legitimate government and
has been banning opponents from running for office. The Pretext? They
were not Ivorian. Then when this ka-man lost the election, he
declared himself winner and dissolved the electoral commission.
"Ivorian strongman steals election" was the headline in one news
service.
This man was behaving in accordance with an old script. He doesn't
seem to have heard of another goatherd called Slobodan Milosevic who
attempted similar tricks only a few days earlier. They didn't work.
Happily, the Ivorians have done something similar to this buffoon.
Wednesday was a great day for Africa.
Let's return home for another case. A member of Parliament, in a fit
of populistic exhuberance, tells a baraza (as a general rule,
politicians open their mouths without engaging their brains at
barazas) that he would take a Bill to the House seeking to make
lynching legal.
Then he denies he ever said anything like that and (a) accuses the
reporter of writing fiction and (b) me of maligning and impeaching
the dignity of Parliament.
Before starting an inadvisable press war, this gentleman, whom I
insist on not naming, needs to know two things. First, reporters
don't ever write fiction. Ever. They can misunderstand, they might
add salt (for which they lose their jobs) and they may mishandle, but
they never put words in politician's mouths. It is politicians who
eat their words of their own volition.
Number two, saying the quality of parliamentary debate is bogus and
sophomoric is not to affront the dignity of the House (if such a
thing exists) but to state a fact. To deny this fact is goatherdism.
We ran out of space, we'll pick up the theme later.

Headline: Ghana: The Worries Of Exporters
Body_Text:
Accra — The Ghana Export Soiree, which the Federation of Associations
of Ghanaian Exporters held at the Labadi Beach Hotel last Thursday
succeeded in bringing to light the headache exporters in the country
are having over the unprecedented free-fall of the cedi.
The function which brought together exporters and various
stakeholders in the business of exports especially the
non-traditional was intended to refocus attention on the long-term
role of the NTE sector in stemming the recurring foreign exchange
problem in the country. No wonder government officials, diplomats and
bankers were all present to grace the occasion.
The function was the first to be organised by FAGE since a new Chief
Executive in the person of Augustine Adongo was appointed to manage
the affairs of the federation which is the umbrella organization for
the various product associations like the Yam Exporters Association,
Horticultural Association of Ghana and a dozen of others. The
federation has been supported by the USAID in recognition of the
potential of the private sector in enhancing the export sector of the
economy.
The federation's president, Nana Yeboa Kodie Asare II, who delivered
the key note address at the function did not mince words when he
painted a rather gloomy picture about the nation's ability to attain
the goals of the Vision 2020. The vision envisages that the country's
total export earnings by that year would hit some $16 million with
the non-traditional export sector raking in as much as $12 million.
To attain this, the president said something radical and innovative
would have to be done. Nana Yeboa was basing his position on the high
interest rate currently prevailing in the country and the free-fall
of the cedi. The unacceptable situation, he said, was subjecting
exporters to a high degree of stress.
Apart from the general constraints facing exporters in the country he
mentioned that our exports are unable to develop internationally
competitive capacities that are consistent with the requirements of
the market.
Another problem facing exporters in the country, according to him, is
the lack of access to information on international standards and
market opportunities. Even though much has been done to remove
bottlenecks to exports, he said there still are some regulatory
requirements that frustrate exporters and investors alike.
The federation on its own intends to embark on a programme to help
members surmount some of the aforementioned problems, he disclosed.
In the coming months, for instanc, he announced that the federation
would embark on a series of programmes to delve into the issues which
are militating against the smooth operations of exporting our non
traditional items. The ultimate aim of this, he said, is to
kick-start the development of a comprehensive and integrated export
development programme and strategy of implementation.
The sponsors of the soiree, Stanchart, also briefed exporters on the
advantages in doing business with their bank. The Executive Secretary
of the Ghana Export Promotion Council, Mr. Tawiah Akyea, who
represented the Trade Minister talked about government making the
business of exports easier and the achievements so far.

Headline: Nigeria: Scorched To Death
Body_Text:
Lagos — Not a few people perish as a petrol tanker loses control and crashes
into the ever-busy Obodoukwu Road Market, Okpoko. Everybody has his palm
clasped to his nose as flies keep hovering around, attracted, of course, by the
foul smell emitting from the spot where the charred remains of the petrol
tanker still lies.
Scattered around the vicinity are what remain of yam tubers, tomatoes,
pepper, cassava and other assorted food items which belonged to the roadside
traders.
And close by, a white coffin lies in wait, with the attendants
intermittently wiping tears from their blood-shot eyes. Evidently, they have
wept to no end.
That was the poignant picture last Thursday, at the ever-busy Obodoukwu
Road, Okpoko, where traders have found a market. Indeed, the market is a
typical case of illegal street-trading. Nobody, however, seems to have ever
raised an eye-brow. So, the people must have taken the situation as normal,
hence nobody anticipated the event of Monday, 20 March. Eyewitness accounts
gave the time as "between 5.30 and 6pm." A petrol tanker whose driver obviously
lost control swept everything on its way as it descended the road. Exactly
opposite the NEPA service station, it rammed into the gutter and lay still.
In no time, sympathisers and rescuers alike were said to have gathered to
salvage those trapped therein. Then, the unexpected happened.
A deafening explosion occurred pushing a great quantity of petrol outwards
in different directions. The accompanying inferno "spread with rocket speed"
according to Rufus Maduekwe, a beans-shop keeper who told TheNEWS he saw it
all. No fewer than 50 persons were said to have been trapped.
Fire-fighters were said to have come from Asaba, the Delta State capital,
but before then, the damage had been done. The ailing landlord in front of
whose house the tanker crashed could not muster enough strength to vacate his
house which had gone up in flames. Consequently, he perished with it. The white
coffin was for the remains of a woman trader who could only be identified by
the colour of her wrapper which was not completely burnt.
Similarly, a six-month-old baby was discovered in the gutter the following
day having been washed there by the torrential rain that fell later that Monday
night. Its mother, as at last week, was still lying under critical condition at
Pieter Hospital, Owerri Road oblivious of what had become of her baby.
Some traders were lucky. Those whose "God did not sleep" on that day as
Onyeka Onyeanwuna put it were taken to Chibike Hospital and Maternity Home, on
