Obodoukwu Road, Pieter Hospital, Njidechukwu Hospital and Graceland Hospital,
Okpoko. Onyeanwuna himself was one of those lucky few. The young, barrow-
pusher, told this magazine that he had gone to help rescue those initially
trapped under the tanker when the explosion occurred and entrapped him. But he
miraculously escaped death.
Nevertheless, he said he had already rescued one man, who though had one of
his legs broken, escaped the fatal explosion.
The same fate also befell Friday Ani and Joseph Eze, both of them barrow-
pushers too. Ani regretted that the man he was about rescuing went with the
fire. At Chibike Hospital, a nurse said a pregnant woman amongst others whose
condition were not very critical had been discharged.
As at last Thursday, nothing had been done to evacuate the trailer and those
trapped underneath it. Distended entrails were quite visible suggesting that a
whole lot of corpses were not washed away by the rain of that fateful Monday
night. Maduekwe said it was, in fact, the rain which finally put out the fire,
and "carried away many dead bodies."
Since the tragedy, Obodoukwu has become a Mecca of sorts, albeit an agony-
inducing one, for those whose relations and friends are no more. A NEPA staff
who pleaded anonymity told TheNEWS that a similar incident had occurred in the
same area in 1985, claiming over a hundred lives.
To avert more of such incidents in the future, he suggested that Ogbanu
Local Government Area where Okpoko belongs, should ban road-side trading. Until
the government hearkens to this advice, there may yet be more of such
unpleasant occurrences, since the traders are back to the roadside, displaying
their wares!
Publication date: April 3, 2000

Headline: Nigeria: We Are Battle Ready
Body_Text:
Lagos — Joe Igbokwe, an Igbo, a writer and a social critic, tackled
the Igbo question in the Nigerian polity. He spoke with ABIMBOLA
OGUNNAIKE.
Q: What is your view on the recent attack on Igbo in Kaduna and the
reprisal attacks in the East?
A: The attack on Igbo is not new in this nation. It started in Jos
in 1945, Kano in 1953, the entire North in 1966 to 1970 and that
culminated in the civil war in which millions of Igbo lives were lost.
Maitatsine religious riots in Kano which occurred in 1980 with very
dire consequences on the Igbo. In 1982, the riot came to Maiduguri. In
1984, it was in Jimeta, Yola. It was in Gombe in 1985. There, the Igbo
were killed, their property looted. The killing spree came to Kaduna
and Kafancha in 1987. In 1991, it was in Bauchi, Katsina and Kano. In
1992, in Zango-Kataf, Igbo were the primary targets because any time
you have a problem in the North, the Igboman is the primary target.
This is because of the type of business they do. They display their
spare parts, they display their building materials, they display the
electronics and so on. In 1993, the riot spread to Funtua. In 1994, you
would recall, Gideon Akaluka, an Igbo citizen, was murdered in Kano. He
was beheaded, his head was paraded in the streets of Kano. All these
attacks have very dire consequences for us, the Igbo. So if you push
somebody to the wall, he would have to react. It is becoming a
tradition that any time you have a crisis in the North, the Igbo bear
the brunt.
I think what happened in the East was pent up emotions of the sordid
past. Already, we have accepted, for 55 years, the senseless killings
of Igbo from 1945 to year 2000. But now, we will fight back. The
attacks will not be allowed again. It will not be condoned by Igbo
people. We are battle-ready. This is because no one section of the
country has a monopoly of violence. No one religion has a monopoly of
violence. I think what happened in the East, as a result of the recent
Kaduna riot, is a warning signal to anybody that Igbo nation of year
2000 is not going to accept this killing, this looting, this
destruction of lives and property. It is no longer acceptable. And if
any Igboman, I am telling you now, is attacked anywhere in this
country, we will respond as quickly as possible within 12 hours from
now on. What happened recently was just a signal. If it happens again,
it is going to be very devastating. We are not going to take it again.
Q: Don't you think this can lead to another civil war just like that
of 1967?
A: Yes. Why should people continue to repeat history. What happened
has shown that men have learnt nothing from history. Despite the
history of tragic dictators all over the world, Adolf Hitler and co.,
Idi Amin of Uganda, General Zia Ul-Haq of Pakistan, Nicolae Ceausescu
of Romania and even General Abacha too, men have learnt nothing from
history. We don't want to go along that line again because no one
nation fights civil a war and lives together. But the meaning of
Nigerian nation, just like my brother, Nkemba of Nnewi, has said,
should not be that of blood-letting. It should not be that of killing.
If the meaning of Nigerian nation is to kill one ethnic group any time
there is crisis, then the nation has no meaning. We had better return
to our respective areas.
Q: What is then going to be Igbo's gains if Nigeria breaks up?
A: We will survive.
Q: In what ways?
A: We have all the capacity to survive. We survived three years of
blockade during the civil war. We were able to manufacture our own
petroleum products. We were able to build our own rockets hitting their
targets with precision. We had our means of getting things done. We
refined petrol and brake fluids from coconut.
Q: (Cuts in). But you don't have access to the sea.
A: What happens to River Niger? Don't you know we have River Niger
at Onitsha? It can be dredged and it would link the Atlantic Ocean
through Calabar. President Shehu Shagari attempted to dredge the river
in 1983 as a gimmick for the election he won and he later abandoned the
project. Even if we don't have a standing river, Igboman can do two
things. We run the engine of this nation, without being immodest. I
want to tell you that Igbo run the engines of this nation. Most of our
people are scientists. Most of our people are engineers. Most of our
people are doctors. So, we have the wherewithal. We have the capacity,
we have the human resources, we have the material resources to give us
an edge. Eighty per cent of nations which have come from the former
Eastern Region are going by the grace of God. We are going with our
immediate brothers.
Q: Who are your immediate brothers?
A: The Akwa Ibom, the Cross River, the Rivers.
Q: But they believe that you have been marginalising them in the
scheme of things.
A: No. It is not true. We have allowed them. They thought we were
their enemies. If a woman feels that she is being cheated, she should
be allowed to marry another man. So, they thought we were cheating them
and we were their problems. So they tried to marry the other side. They
tried the other husband, that is their Northern brothers. They will go
with their immediate neighbours. If I have problem at 7, Igbokwe Street
here, people at No. 4, No. 9 will gather before you begin to look for
my brothers who are not even around here. Don't you know that recently,
South-South governors were to meet with the Eastern governors but that
was aborted. We have a cordial relationship with them. And I can tell
you, when we go to Igbo fora, we will tell a good number of them from
Calabar, from Rivers and others that we are brothers. In fact, the
emphasis will no longer be on Igbo nation but on the former Eastern
Region. That is the way it should be. All the time, we should not be
talking about three major ethnic groups. There are more ethnic groups
in this country that are not up to 100,000 people. If we break away, if
Igbo nation breaks away, if Yoruba and Hausa nations break away, what
happens to these small ethnic groups? Where will the Ogoni be? Where
will these small, small tribes that are not up to 50,000 people be? You
think they can be a nation? That is the tragedy.
Q: How do we relate with one another if Nigeria breaks?
A: I don't want to consider that possibility. I don't want to look
at that option. Nigeria will not break up because we have so many
things to gain by staying together.
Q: What are the gains?
A: Very many. When you go to Ketu in Lagos, people from the North
bringing tomatoes, bringing beans, bringing yams, bringing goats,
bringing cows and so on. That is something. And the East, they go with
their industries, they move to the North, they move to the West with
their spare parts and all that. And the Yoruba with their
intellectuals, have control over many of the financial institutions and
insurance companies. They are professionals. That is the benefit from
the Yoruba nation. There are so many things Nigeria will get from the
Igbo nation. There are so many things Nigeria will get from the North.
We have so many things to gain by remaining together.
Q: What is the Igbo agenda in the nation called Nigeria?
A: I can tell you, Igboman has the greatest stake in Nigeria. A
Yorubaman can move from here to Sao Tome, to Brazil, to Liberia to
Sierra-Leone. They have their kinsmen there. Even Togo there, they have
over three million people there. I have made this point clear in
several fora. The Hausaman has his kinsmen from here to Saudi Arabia to
Niger, so they can afford to go there any time there is crisis. Igboman
has no outlets anywhere in the world. There is no place in the world
where Igbo is spoken. That is why you see them they have accepted
Nigeria as their own. They reside in every nook and cranny of Nigeria.
Recently, they have invested about N8.5 billion in Lagos here, moving
out of Idumota auto spare parts market to another place near
International Trade Complex along Oshodi-Apapa expressway They are
investing N8.5 billion in that project.
Q: What are the problems of the North?
A: They don't want other people to succeed where they have failed.
President Olusegun Obasanjo has been able to correct all that in very a
short period and they are not happy about it. Look at how much money
Abacha looted. In fact, it is described as one of the biggest search
for stolen money in the history of the world. We are not happy that a
leader from the North is being portrayed as a looter, the worst leader
any nation has ever produced in the whole world. It is no longer
possible for them to beckon to the military any time they lose
elections. With their grip of the military, most of them were so blind
that they were pursuing career in the military that they forgot that
they have to send their children to school. They thought the military
would live forever, and they would control the machinery of power
forever. Since Nigeria gained independence in 1960, they have been at
the helm of the affairs. They have been there for so many years that
they don't even remember that they have other Nigerians to share power
with. They don't believe that one day, this leadership will swing to
other people. So, some people cannot manage life after power.
Publication date: March 27, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Census Not For Tax Purposes - DCE
Headline: Ghana: Census Not For Tax Purposes - DCE
Body_Text:
Accra — The BirIm North District Chief Executive, Prince Yaw Danso,
has dispelled the misconception prevailing in the district that census
figures will be used as a basis for the imposition of more property
rates and other taxes.
He said this when he addressed the chiefs and people of Akokoaso at
their annual celebration of Yam Festival at Akokoaso, recently.
Yaw Danso said since the formation of the Birim North district (BND)
in 1987, this is going to be their maiden census exercise, which will
help the district assembly to know their actual population figures,
adding that this in turn will help accelerate development in the
district.
He said with the expansion of Akokoaso township, the assembly is to
embark on a pipe-borne water project, which will take off during the
middle of this year, alongside their self-help electrification project.
In his welcome address, the chief of Akakoaso, Nana Danso Abeam II,
appealed to the Ministry of Health (MOH) through the District Chief
Executive to post a midwife to their clinic to help in the health
delivery system in the town.

Headline: Africa: Midlands Tourney Set For Tomorrow
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The Midlands Pool Organisation (MPO) in conjunction with
Sams Sports Cafe have lined-up a pool championship tournament as part
of Youth Week celebrations.
The events organiser, Kabungo Kamanga yesterday said the tournament
which starts tomorrow at 16:00 hours will attract eight players in the
first round.
"On Saturday at 12:00hrs another eight players clash and these will
qualify to the semi-finals. The 16 will then battle it out until two
reach the finals the following day.
The champion will walk away with a trophy, tape recorder and
K200,000 cash while the runner-up will bag a gift voucher worth
K100,000, Kamanga said.
He said the competition was open to both male and female players as
long as they paid the K 15,000 participation fee at Sam's Sports Cafe.
He paid tribute to Yams Motors, Bolder, Axis Centre, Dunns, Dulys
Motors, Compudoc and Bonel Holdings for sponsoring the tournament.

Headline: Africa: The African film making experience
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Gaston Kabore is an award winning film maker from
Burkina Faso. He completed his studies at the Sorbonne University,
France, and has been working in the film industry ever since. He has
worked as technical cinema advisor for the Ministry of Information and
Culture, as well as Director of the National Centre of Cinema.
From 1985-1997 Gaston Kabore served as General Secretary of the Pan-
African Film Directors Federation. He has also been a member of the
judging panel at the Cannel Film Festival.
His film repertoire include Wend Kuuni (the gift of God), 1982, which
won a Cesar award for Best French Speaking Film, Zan Boko 1988, winner
of the best Screenplay prize at Belgium's Namur Festival; Rabi, 1992;
and Buud Yam (Stallion of Yennenga) 1997, which won five awards at the
15th Fespaco Film Festival, 1997, including grand prize. Since 1979
Gaston Kabore has been working on the Executive Committee of the
Ouagadougou Film Festival.
Gaston Kabore was among the facilitators at M-Net's New Directions
Africa workshop that ended on the 22nd January, 2000. The workshop, held
off the coast of Senegal on the historic island of Goree, brought
together established and emerging script writers, directors and
producers from seven African countries for a series of workshops on
various aspects of film making, which ended with the production of a
short film-seven takes on the same script.
Gaston Kabore has been working with script writers from each country,
including Manyazewal Endashaw of Ethiopia, in an effort to identity and
promote film making talent throughout the continent. Indrias Getachew
from Ethiopia had the chance to interview the award winning film maker.
Excerpts follow:
Indrias Getachew: How did you get started in film?
Gaston Kabore: It happened in 1974. I was studying history in Paris
at the Sorbonne University and eventually for my Masters of Science I
did a study about the representation of Africa within the illustrated
press in France at the end of the 19th century after the Berlin
Conference in 1885, which was about the sharing of Africa among the
colonial powers.
By watching the pictures that they were drawing about Africa: savage
continent and the French being on a mission to civilize Africa, I saw
that the image of Africa has been disfigured since that time. They
established a kind of exotic vision of Africa, and I realized how the
analysis was false and how they are hiding their secret desire of
dominating under the [guise] of bringing civilization to Africa.
That was my first contact with images, pictures. When I finished my
4th year of university I was about to start my Ph.
D in history, instead I decided to go to film school to learn the
language of images. I was realizing that all the cliches about Africa,
the lies about Africa were so consistent that already we can see the
continuation of those things in the television pieces.
I wanted to learn the language of moving pictures to be able to use
cinema in history but also to reportray, to repossess my continent
through images. I was not aiming to become a permanent filmmaker, but it
seemed to me that cinema was a major tool of expression where Africans
can fight to recover their own image.
After two years I understood that in fact I was more interested than
I had expected, not only to record history with cinema, but to tell
stories with cinema, because telling stories is also something that
Africans need, because they need to reflect their own reality-also to
invest the imagination in moving pictures. Of course, the study that I
made before in history, a little bit of sociology and anthropology, it
enabled me to understand what cinema could bring to my people and what
kind of films I should try to do.
- The term African Renaissance has been used a lot at this workshop.
How do you see it, what is driving this renaissance?
I think the term renaissance, to be precise it came from South Africa
and I can understand why.
They were living under Apartheid, and with the end of apartheid they
were a new nation. I think for them it is a renaissance because the
anti-apartheid people-they might be black or white-did not really have
access to the media, particularly the medium of cinema and television.
Now for South Africans it is a real renaissance because for the first
time they have access to present their imagination, their visions into
film. For the rest of Africa, at the Pan African Federation of Film
Makers we have been fighting to have a rooted cinema in Africa.
Today we are saying we have already done a lot with more or less
success; but we still need to rethink film making in Africa. We need to
face the challenge of television today, the satellites and everything.
In the past we were fighting to have access to the screen in the
cinema hall; but today people don't need to go to a theater. In their
homes they are watching 15-30 (if you have satellite) different
channels.
So I think that the new historical situation in South Africa is one
thing; but also in the rest of Africa we are trying to address new
issues. We have the need to think differently for the survival of Africa
in terms of images.
- What are your predictions for the next four years-will we be able
to meet this challenge?
Of course it will not be easy because of the concerns that you know-
the lack of means, of infrastructure, the absence of skills in some of
the countries-but I still think that each country cannot go further
separately. We need to cooperate.
We need to bridge and consider the market of two, three countries so
we can hope that the tremendous things can go ahead. In the Pan African
Federation of film Makes for three decades we tried and have succeeded
to establish dialogue with the Organization of African Unity based in
Addis Ababa.
We have succeeded to make them vote a lot of resolutions and
recommendations concerning cinema. I remember this conference in '86 at
Fort Louis in Mauritius when one of the leaders was saying that cinema
is a vital need for African people.
The problem is that this political awareness and consciousness about
cinema is not transferred into acts in the countries. So, I do see
reason to hope but I don't forget that it is a struggle not yet
finished.
- In your perspective, what is the role of film in the development
agenda of the continent. Why is it a vital need for Africans?
I think Africa is suffering more from a lack of memory than from a
lack of resources.
Africa is suffering more from the political blindness of politicians
than from a lack of resources. I do believe that cinema helps to wake
up, to open the eyes of the people.
They will recover the possibility to see through their own eyes the
reality. They will become actors in their own lives instead of being
just passive in front of all the disasters taking place in the country,
and also in front of the politicians completely preoccupied with staying
in power instead of trying to see to the long term development of their
countries.
If we are able to give our people confidence, to let them know more
about their history because when you know who you are, you have a better
chance to know where you are going. But if you are completely hopeless,
if you give up, it seems that you are useless to the entire world.
To me cinema is not alone-it is also up to people working in
communication-what are you bringing as a new vision? What kind of
consistent hope are you giving to the people to say well it is difficult
today but we do know that we are building our country for the coming
generations. To me this is really what cinema could bring.
To me it is as important as health, food, education, roads, and
transportation. But we need people to have vision; and this is the real
drama of Africa.
Most of the time our decision makers, our politicians, the ruling
class, is completely hopeless in front of those big questions. A nation
is not built in ten years time-the time that they are in power only,
stealing the nation's money and everything.
And they forget that these people already have a very long history.
We need just to give them the possibility to stand up and play their
role, and be the workers of their destiny.
- When you look back on your career, have you been able to achieve
your vision?
To me the times I have been most happy in my film life were when I
see my people watching one of my films and they even think that the
story written by me belongs to them. It is better than any other
congratulations.
- As you think back to the things that drew you to this profession,
what are your feelings on this workshop?
For most of the script writers it is the first time they are
confronted with script writing, but it is good because we need to
develop new skills within Africa. Some of them have a very good
potential-they are very motivated.
I am not saying that the seven writers here will become scriptwriters
for the rest of their lives. But at least I have seen them all ready to
go as far as possible in that experience, learning quickly and
understanding that it is a real serious business.
To write a story is not a joke-it is something that hurts you a lot;
it is painful because you are in the process of excavating in your own
life, giving your vision. To write a story is to expose yourself, and
each of us is trying to hide ourselves.
That is the first thing that you discover when you are writing
novels. You are penetrating the fibers of the human being and sometimes
you are discovering yourself.
We need this kind of thing to be enlarged. M-Net cannot be the only
one to do that.
We need the television in Ethiopia to say well I also have my role to
play. I need to give the professionals in my country the possibility to
make filMs. It is possible; it's not just a question of money, it is
also a question of commitment, a question of historical consciousness,
question of responsibility.
When you are informed you no longer act like before.

Original_Search_Title: The sights and sounds of Ho
Headline: Ghana: The sights and sounds of Ho
Body_Text:
Accra — Ho is the capital of the Volta Region. It is blessed with a
countless number of tourist attractions. They are both natural and cultural,
varied and spatially distributed.
MOUNTAIN RANGES/HILLS
The Ghana-Togo Mountain Range and the Ewe Mountains form the most dominant
relief features. The mountainous areas are places of outstanding scenic beauty.
The most classical being the AVATIME mountains, most especially Mt. GEMI at
AMDzofe. It stands well over 2,800 feet above sea level.
The immediate environs have a near temperate micro-climate characterized by
cool mornings, evenings and nights and a lot of foggy weather.
Condensation often takes place on the ground level making the Amedzofe area
an extraordinarily unique destination.
The fact that mosquitoes are not found at Amedzofe-Gbadzeme areas make the
area the number one choice in the country for siting a health resort
(sanatorium).
The Ewe Mountains, WETO for short, lies between Tsito and Peki. It is also a
very beautiful mountain. In Tsito and Abutia Agore can be found spots where
water oozes out of an aquifer.
The Kpere Hill is also one area that commands out standing panoramic view.
One can see an arm of the great Volta Lake in the distance.
The KABAKABA/GALENKUI Hills, the most dominant hill at Ho is one area which
is beyond description. Currently, half of the mountain is re-forested with teak
trees, which make the mountain green, especially during the rainy season.
WATERFALLS
Ho District has at least two waterfalls, the better known being the falls
between Amedzofe and Gbadzeme. It is a lovely fall that cascades down slope
thrice before forming a big pool. The water from the fall is pure, colourless,
odourless and extremely soft.
The other fall is said to be located near Ashanti Kpoeta. Incidentally, I am
yet to visit the site described by a trusted friend as being very lovely.
KALAKPA GAME RESERVE
This reserve comprises both Adaklu and Abatia Traditional Areas and covers
an estimated 125 square miles of gentle gradient which drains into various
streams.
The Kalakpa stream is the only kind in this reserve, but dries up in the dry
season. The main vegetation is savannah grassland interspersed with a few fire-
resistant trees.
ANIMALS
The main animals found in the reserve include, Kob (Kobus-Kob), Baboon
(Papio Anubis), and Green Monkey (Cercopithecus Aethiops).
These three animals are mostly seen in the dry season, especially after the
bushfires.
ZITOE (KOB HILLS) is an area where one can see the Kobs in herds running, at
times into several hundreds. Other animals found on the reserve include,
buffalo, bush buck, bush pig, maxwell duiker, water buck, patas monkey, genet
cat, and grass cutters.
BIRDS
The Kalakpa Game Production Reserve is said to be among the richest in the
West African sub-region. There are countless numbers of sparrows, swallows,
egrets, franocolins, weaver birds, etc.
ANCIENT GERMAN BUILDING/RELICS
Hidden among the mango trees on the side of a hill at Abutia Agore, can be
found disused old buildings made entirely of locally-found good quality clay
and initially roofed with thatch.
It used to house at one-time, Capt. Von Doulis, a German officer in charge
of the then Trans-Volta Togoland, who served under Alexander Foxe (Galenkui)
At the foot of the hill which housed Capt. Doulis' bungalow is a beautiful
aquifer. The water is said to have been used by all the people living far and
wide in the olden days.
All other German buildings were destroyed. The old church building at Ho
Kpodzi and the EP Schools are some of the relics. There are some old buildings
too at Biakpa and Amedzofe now used as Guest Houses.
At Ho and Adaklu can be found graves of some early Missionaries who lived in
there. On top of Mt. Gemi is a huge metal cross erected by the Germans.
HISTORICAL RELICS/MUSEUMS
A huge bell which was captured by the Ashantis during one of their wars with
the people of Ho is on display at the E.P. Church Headquarters at Ho. The
church bell was said to be taken to Kumasi and placed on a fetish grove and
later returned.
ARTS AND CRAFTS
Kente weaving is very important at Kpetoe and Afegame. The natives of these
two towns are Ga-Adangbes who migrated there during the great exodus. Other
craft items include basketry, pottery, etc.
Festivals
The main festival celebrated in the Ho District is the Yam Festival. It is
celebrated in September. The Rice Festival is also celebrated in the Amadezofe
areas.
HOTELS
Ho alone has over 17 hotels and Rest houses. The star-rated ones are Chances
Hotel 3-Star, Freedom Hotel 2-Star, Woezor Hotel 1-Star and Tauras Hotel 1-
Star.
The latest sensation in the hotel industry is Caravan Sarai, which is yet to
be commissioned.
SUGGESTIONS
There is the need for the many villages in the Kalakpa Game Reserve, whose
inhabitants are persistently poaching to be relocated.
Unless this is done, this very important tourist asset will be completely
destroyed.

Headline: Uganda: ADF ambush UPDF convoy
Body_Text:
Kampala — 3 rebels killed By Souza Justus In Bundibugyo Rebels of the Allied
Democratic Forces (ADF) Jan. 31 ambushed a contingent of UPDF soldiers at Ngite
village Harugale sub county in Bundibugyo district.
The incident occurred at 7:30 a.m when the UPDF was on a search-and-destroy-
rebels operation.
The UPDF overpowered the rebels and killed three of them, and recovered from
them one SMG with 17 rounds of live ammunition.
Three UPDF soldiers suffered minor injuries. They are admitted at Bundibugyo
Hospital.
All the dead ADF rebels wore long beards, two of them looked healthy, but
the third was sickly.
The bodies which were clothed in torn civilian clothes had on them roasted
yams, ripe bananas, pieces of sugar-cane, three small saucepans, and pieces of
torn blankets.
The incident was confirmed by security sources.

Headline: Libyans Observe Ramadan
Body_Text:
Libyans Sunday started the holy
month of Ramadan, when Muslims fast from dawn to dusk with most
social activities suspended.
During the fast period Muslims are also obliged to abstain
from drinking, smoking and having sexual relations.
But most Arab and Muslim countries began their own fasting
Monday, while others will even wait to start Tuesday.
The fact that all countries do not start the fasting month
unanimously depends on the lunar calendar.
The beginning and the end of the month are fixed by the
appearance of the new moon, which might be seen in one place and
not in another at the same time.
In Libya, after the beginning of Ramadan was announced
Saturday night, the pace of life abruptly changed.
The 42-hour working week is also drastically reduced.
Shopping for foodstuffs has stepped up as crowds Sunday
thronged vegetable and fruit markets, triggering a marginal
increase in prices.
By Monday, a six-kg box of sweet potatoes had risen to 1.5
Libyan dinars (about three US dollars) compared to 1.2 dinars the
previous day. But the prices of imported fruits such as banana,
apples and pears, remained unchanged.
This is partly because, consumers are rather attracted to
the country's seasonal fruits, particularly oranges, which are
among the best and most juicy in the Mediterranean basin.
Meanwhile, Libyan television has started broadcasting a
series of special programmes for Ramadan, when most activities
are shifted to night time in the Arab and Muslim world.
In Libya, these includes shows, shopping in supermarkets,
football matches and even family visits.
But by the second week of the holy month, the emphasis
would shift from foodstuffs to the purchasing of clothes for the
Aid Al-Fitr, the end-of-Ramadan feast.
In line with the religious mood, fewer women are seen on
the streets of Tripoli during the day, while the working women
attend to their duties wearing long traditional attire.

Headline: Zimbabwe: And Now to the Notebook
Body_Text:
Harare — Let's get ready for a big win Cde In his interview with the
BBC last week, RGM carefully sought to do two things: tell senior
party members that he intends to run again as the party's candidate
in the 2002 state presidential ballot and that eventually, when he
decides to go, both his deputies would also have to clear the decks.
I think he succeeded in getting the message across, but whether he
will indeed be allowed to stand as ZANU PF's presidential candidate
is another thing altogether. But then, we won't have to wait much
longer because his party is scheduling what could turn out to be a
particularly defining internal power struggle in the December
congress. It's clear that all the unhappy voices which have hitherto
only privately complained about his policies in whispers will now
either have to come out into the open or shut up for a long time
indeed.
There are no two ways about it - the hour of decision has finally
arrived. FIM's attention has been drawn to an obituary on black
nationalist Michael Mawema written by ZANU PF's legal chief Eddison
Zvobgo in the Mirror two weeks ago. "It is a shame that he (Mawema)
was denied a Heroes' Acre funeral," Zvobgo wrote, referring to the
Politburo's decision to reject an application by Masvingo province
for hero status for a man whose contribution to Zimbabwe's 1970s
independence struggle has already been well-chronicled for anyone who
cares. "There is no doubt that his passion was the people and that
his life's purpose was also the people," Zvobgo said. He added: "It
is however comforting to know that many deserving heroes did not make
it to Heroes' Acre. Dr Mawema joins Sheba Tavarwisa, Lookout Masuku,
Jairos Jiri and numerous more who lie humbly elsewhere." Readers will
recall equally critical comments which Zvobgo, who is clearly upping
his criticism of the government, made at Mawema's private funeral in
Masvingo a few weeks ago. He told the mourners, most of them drawn
from his powerbase of Masvingo, that they should not worry too much
about the Politburo's decision because, after all, it had been made
by men and women who themselves "are not perfect". The Politburo had
merely voted by a show of hands which he said could never guarantee
anyone a place either in hell or heaven.
Mawema's deeds while on this our beautiful earth, he said, clearly
made him a people's hero. Looks like Zimbabwe's usual dull political
landscape is headed for some torrid times. Pity that it took so many
men and women of conscience this long to speak out against obvious
injustices, but then it is perhaps better late than never. Some of
the reasons given by government sympathisers for the seizure of
Capital Radio's broadcasting equipment are really outrageous,
notwithstanding the fact that these people have every right to say
what they are saying. Last week in Harare, there was Chenjerai
Hunzvi, leader of the lawless section of the war veterans, telling a
public meeting that the government's clampdown on the radio would
deny imperialist forces a voice to announce a coup which he said they
are plotting against the government. Really? What evidence does he
have and why has he not bothered to report it to the police, as he
should as a concerned and patriotic Zimbabwean? As for Jonathan Moyo,
the government's chief spin doctor, I have yet to hear a denial from
him that Capital Radio will only get a broadcasting licence over "my
dead body" - as he has been widely reported as saying. Surely how can
a government minister who is supposed to be fair to all (I can hear
you laughing at my irresponsible statement!) - and in this case the
final arbiter on who gets such a licence - speak out in this manner?
Capital Radio, owned and run by mostly Zimbabweans, has every right
to have its application for a broadcasting licence heard by an
impartial panel and not to be pre-judged by those who appear to have
already condemned it for whatever reasons, however valid. Certainly
such statements already disqualify Moyo from being an impartial judge
in this instance and could weigh heavily against him in any court
case that the radio station could bring against him.
Still on Moyo, he seemed to be at pains to tell state television at
the weekend that the presidential amnesty granted to people guilty of
offences related to the February referendum and the June ballot had
been a result of representations made by various groups which felt
that the action would help the cause of reconciliation. Really? It
would be interesting to know the groups that made the representations
and which political party will benefit most from the amnesty. What is
clear though is that the pardons, to benefit outright criminals who
abducted and even tortured opponents, will accentuate lawlessness in
Zimbabwe and increasingly push ordinary people into taking the law
into their own hands. When a government fails to protect its own
citizens and even pardons the guilty, no one is safe anymore and
there is no law and order, which Moyo says still exists in Zimbabwe.
As thousands of poor residents of Harare's Mabvuku and Tafara took to
the streets in protest against skyrocketing living costs earlier this
week, none other than the head of the Consumer Council of Zimbabwe
(CCZ) Elizabeth Nerwande denounced them. Nerwande, who is
increasingly alienating herself from hard-pressed consumers by her
frequent pro-establishment pronouncements, suddenly saw a hidden
political hand in the spontaneous riots. She then urged consumers to
boycott bread, whose price went up again last week, and to eat sweet
potatoes and madhumbe, forgetting that not only are these foodstuffs
seasonal but actually cost much more than bread these days. Just in
case the CCZ boss was unaware, a kg of both madhumbe and sweet
potatoes now costs no less than $30, higher than the $20 that one
spends to buy a small loaf of broad. And this, of course, assumes
that urban dwellers in cities such as Bulawayo and Harare can still
find madhumbe which I understand are only grown in far-away
Manicaland.
During my travels into rural areas in the past few weeks, I found an
interesting point worth sharing with readers: while urban areas
suffocate from constant fuel shortages, rural folks have never had it
better. In fact, fuel shortages are precisely the sort of things
which the rural folk only hear about on the radio.
I am sure this has nothing to do with the policies of ZANU PF
officials such as Ignatius Chombo and Obert Mpofu of punishing urban
dwellers for voting for the opposition during the June election.

Headline: Zambia: Vitamin A winners were great
Body_Text:
Lusaka — When we started out on the second phase of the Better Health
campaign a month ago, our main aim was not only to try and reach as many
people as possible. It was also to get them to involve them in radio and
television programmes and indeed this column.
With messages in local languages aired everyday, in English four times a
week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday and Saturday) and on television twice a week
(Monday and Friday) we had reason to believe we were reaching a reasonable
number of our people. We also hoped that people were getting the messages
that we were relaying to them. Put simply, that by taking simple to follow
actions everyday, you could control up to 80 per cent of your health
problems.
Prevention is of course the key to all this. Prevention is far much
cheaper and comes with little pain compared to cure.
But you must get into the habit of practicing all the things that enable
you to prevent disease before it takes root. Where prevention has failed,
then you must make every effort to detect the disease early. This makes the
management and cure of the disease that much easier.
And finally when the disease has been detected, you must take the
treatment correctly and completely according to your health care providers
instructions. The first topic we tackled as promised was child health,
specifically the importance of vitamin A for children. We looked at why this
critical vitamin was important for our children, where and when you could get
vitamin A supplementation and which foods were rich in vitamin A.
Just to ensure that you took another look at the messages, we asked two
easy to answer questions at the end of each topic. We then sat back and held
our breath, hoping you would participate in the programme by writing to us
with your answers and comments.
Being the ambitious people that we are, we were hoping that perhaps we
might get 10 letters per topic, maybe 20 if all went well. So we were more
than a little surprised.
No, absolutely bowled over when the letters averaged some 250 per
programme. At the time of the draw, we had more than 700 letters from you.
Now if someone had suggested just 50 letters per topic per week at the
beginning, I have a feeling that most of us would have prescribed an urgent
meeting with the shrink. The response has been terrific and each and every
one of you, not just those who wrote to us, even though they do get our very
special "thank you", has been marvelous. Initial reports are that the turn-
out at clinics and other medical centres during the vitamin A campaign was
impressive. That makes every parent or guardian who took their child to
receive the vitamin A supplement a winner! Let us just remind you what the
main messages in the three topics were: * Vitamin A is important to children
because it ensures normal growth, development and protects against disease.
* Vitamin A supplements ensure that children get enough of this critical
chemical. Ensure that all children from birth up to six years-old receive
vitamin A supplements every six months.
* That most foods which contain vitamin A such as chibwabwa (pumpkin
leaves) and other dark green vegetables, yellow fruits such as mangoes and
pawpaws and oil sources such as groundnuts are affordable. Most of these can
be grown in our backyards and need not always be bought from other people.
And for remembering these messages and writing to us about them, you stand
the chance to win a fabulous Your Health Matters and To Your Health T-shirt
and qualified for the grand prize of ONE YEAR'S SUBSCRIPTION TO A GOVERNMENT
HOSPITAL NEAR YOU FOR A FAMILY OF SIX. And now, the winners are: QUIZ ONE:
Question One - Why is vitamin A important for our children? Answer - Vitamin
A is important for our children because it ensures they have good eyesight
and protects them from disease.
Name one serious condition which can be caused by lack of vitamin A? Night
blindness, poor growth and development and in extremecases, death. QUIZ TWO:
Question One - At what ages and how often should children receive vitamin A?
From birth to six years. Where can you get vitamin A supplements for
children? From any clinic. QUIZ THREE Name five foods that are rich in
vitamin A? Dark green leafy vegetables (chibwabwa, ibondwe et cetera) liver,
kidneys, kapenta, yellow sweet potatoes, fortified sugar, milk, mangoes and
eggs.
How can you tell that sugar is fortified with vitamin A? The packet is
clearly labelled with the vitamin A logo and the words "fortified with
vitamin A." T-Shirt winners 1. Situmbeko Nalishebo Lusaka 2.
Beatrice Mutale - Kalulushi 3. Maggie Manda - Kitwe 4.
Sam A. K.
Llungu - Ndola 5. Pardon Mmukulusa - Kitwe 6.
Richard Chellah- Lusaka. Grand prize winner - Subscription to a
Government hospital near you for a family of six: Sam A K. Lungu.
Hearty congratulations to all. Please write to us at the address below
clearly indicating whether you want us to post your prize or whether you can
collect it from our offices.
To Your Health is brought to you by the Central Board of Health. In
addition, please tune in to ZNBC's radio Two (Monday, Wednesday, Friday and
Saturday) and One (daily) and television's news hour.
If you want more information on this and any health topic, please write
to: The Producer To Your Health Central Board of Health P.O. Box 32588
Lusaka.

Headline: Africa: The science of the queue
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — You're standing in the queue at the post office waiting for a
stamp, or at the Department of Home Affairs because you've lost your ID book.
And you know how long you can wait! Or even at the Department of Justice, and
that's world record-breaking in waiting. There's a TV set above the queue
running ads, infomercials and music videos. It comes from Twilight Advertising
and Entertainment Channel.
It's a new concept in narrow casting. It harnesses the three main qualities
of an audience standing in a queue:
It's captive, which means the advertisers are assured of attentive
viewership,
It's bored, which means it will be satisfied with anything that is
entertaining, especially the ads, and
It tends to get agitated - the video content reduces the agitation which
suits the host of the venue.
In short, the concept and its execution is magic.
A queue audience standing on its legs is far more proactive than the audience
buried in the comfort of the sofa at home, says Twilight's MD Harm Fourie. They
are heavily receptive to marketing messages, and are in a position to act on the
messages as soon as they get out of the queue. You can contrast this to the
couch potato who can only react the day after, by which time the motivation has
wound down.
Four legs
The Twilight formula has four legs: franchising, technology, content and
marketing of advertising. All four make use of glaring gaps in the traditional
broadcast audiences.
Twilight has divided the country and the outlets into various regions defined
by the movement of people around their contracted outlets. These areas are sold
as franchises, and for a remarkably small investment, the franchisee simply has
to sell local advertising, and has to ensure that the tapes are played and the
equipment remains in good order.
The KISS principle
The technology is simple. VHS tapes are compiled in the studio at Twilight's
head office in Sandton. There is a separate master for each region as this
venture harnesses the regional advertising pool that the advertising agencies
and the Independent Broadcasting Authority say does not exist. That is why
regional TV is so long in coming. Fourie should be very grateful for these
perceptions. The tapes are then dispatched by courier, and the franchisee
ensures the tapes are played in VHS machines that are set to a loop function.
The technology is no more than a VHS player, a TV monitor and a set of
speakers - one monitor for each area of the queue. That explains why queues are
now made to snake around in straight lines: it's not to squeeze more people in,
it's to give them more efficient viewing comfort.
The content follows a format and pattern that has been found by experience to
optimally suit the queue audience. The fundamental rule is that the content has
to be as entertaining as possible, says Fourie. We recommend that advertisers
make amusing ads, play down the hard sell and play up the content. But it's not
wall-to-wall ads. They are interspersed with sports highlights, short comedy
sequences, sports funnies, cartoons, public service announcements and
infomercials.
Short and sweet
No one sequence of content runs longer than a minute. You're trying to
alleviate stress and boredom. There's only one way to do that: with
entertainment and variety.
The compilation of the tape is customised to each region. It is a mixture of
national and local ads, and some queues move faster than others. So the design
takes into account that the tape must be longer than the longest time a person
will spend in the queue.
Regardless of that, Fourie will not allow advertisers to buy more than one
spot every fifteen minutes. We can't afford to irritate viewers.
Marketing the advertising is highly sophisticated and extensive research has
determined the number of people who will make use of the queue over any given
period. Ad rates are decided on the simple and conventional permutations of
frequency and reach.
Regions and franchises
There are about 80 regions, and 66 have been sold off as franchises. The
minimum return on investment (ROI) for a franchisee is 35% for doing absolute
nothing except to guarantee the tapes roll, as we guarantee turnover due to the
national advertising, says Fourie. With hard work, an ROI of 1000% is very
achievable.
Twilight will be making big changes within the next three months. Part of the
UAM group, Twilight has access to the technologies of AirMedia which satellite
content to the Hyperama Checkout Channel. Delivering the content to the 80
regions by satellite is very attractive. Currently mastering, duplicating and
delivery by courier costs some R100 000 a month.
This can be drastically reduced by playing out directly to the satellite from
the editing system all the 80 different versions by means of a playlist. The
signal is delivered direct by satellite to servers in the outlets, and played
out through AirMedia's tried and tested store and forward technology.
Can the local advertisers afford to make their own ads? Sure. The local
franchisee takes brief, sends it to the professionals in the Twilight studio,
and at the high rate of volume at which they make ads, we can turn out an
effective ad for about R3000.
The Twilight formula has proven successful by the expansion from where it
started in South African post offices, to similar queues in Zimbabwe and
Namibia. It's not going to stop there, but Fourie is understandably mum about
his future plans.
However, Twilight has just moved to even grander offices and studios, so it's
not hot air.

Headline: Uganda: Traders Irked By Arrests
Body_Text:
Kampala — Anger and frustration amongst traders against the Uganda Revenue
Authority (URA), eased slightly over the weekend following the release of
several colleagues held at Jinja Road Police Station.
This followed a stormy meeting between traders and top government officials
at the City Hall on Friday. The visibly annoyed traders, complained of their
goods being kept at various unofficial places like the Police Training School
and CID headquarters without receipts.
About 300 traders, who overflowed City Hall, also threatened to boycott the
import trade. The reason being the unexplained arrests of some members of the
Uganda Import and Export Traders Association (UGIETA) over allegations of
smuggling.
Manzi Tumubweine the Minister of State in the Finance ministry, told them
that the Ministry of Internal Affairs should check up on how people are
arrested as the law assumes they are innocent until proved guilty.
He also told them to tell Parliament to decrease taxes if they thought they
were too high.
"It is Parliament, which sets the tax levels," he said.
Tumubweine had been told by the irate traders that "home boys" allegedly
driving UC registered vehicles, were harassing them despite paying the relevant
taxes.
"These arrogant boys do not seem to care what is written on the papers.
They are so arrogant and do not appear to follow the law of Uganda," said
Godfrey Mukasa who had only lately left prison himself.
"I had paid taxes of sh45m, but immediately after leaving Customs, I was
arrested by a boy called Musinguzi, allegedly for not paying taxes! "I was put
in a cell with 70 others, about 25 of whom were also supposedly there for
smuggling offences and had not made statements. I was told I had been using
forged documents when those were computed and stamped by URA."
Mukasa said.
The traders ridiculed Government for being a 'circus', because it had set up
a another agency (Special Revenue Protection Services) to watch over URA's own
Revenue Protection Service (RPS).
The Commissioner General of URA Elly Rwakakooko told the traders they were
not there to blame other agencies.
"That may be done after the formation of a tripartite committee composed of
Representatives of the President's Office, Internal Affairs and Trade, which
will look into this matter.
"You have a right to complain, if you are harassed or humiliated by even
this other agency you mention. However you should be policing yourselves to
report the smugglers thereby removing move the distortion they cause in the
market, " he said.
One trader claimed that he was slapped by people from the SPRS in Iganga the
previous week, when it turned out that he had only sweet potatoes in his car
boot.
Tumubweine told the traders to approach the relevant authorities. "I expect
anybody with similar problems to tell us, otherwise we might not know.
Human beings behave differently. So now we think there was a mistake made,
how do we ensure that it does not happen again?" He however caused some
laughter when he told the traders never to give URA officials any money, but he
said that if they really have too, they should inform him confidentially
Kampala Mayor, Ssebaana Kizito also encouraged the traders to stand up for what
is right after hearing how they had been mistreated by some security people.

Original_Search_Title: National Sorghum Breweries Reports Profit For The First Time In Years
Headline: South Africa: National Sorghum Breweries Reports Profit For The First Time In Years
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — National Sorghum Breweries (NSB) has registered a
positive cashflow, reporting a profit of R504m for the first time in
years.
It expects to cut its long-term liabilities, according to the
company's latest annual report.
The company, which reports its results for the 12 months to December
31 1999 a year late because of past reporting problems, says
long-term liabilities will be cut by another R17,5m during the
current year.
The group had reduced its long- and short-term liabilities to R94m by
December 31 last year from R171m in 1998. The resultant reduction of
finance charges was expected to be about R4m.
The company's largest shareholder, India's United Breweries, has
committed itself to investing an additional sum of up to $5m in NSB
by way of share capital as and when required.
However, NSB is making efforts to raise capital from empowerment
groups in SA before accepting the investment from United. The
directors are confident that its profit will increase.
The sorghum-beer business is commercially and financially viable.
Last year's performance was significantly better than the year
before. Operating profit rose R13,6m to R27,6m from a previous R14m.
The improved performance of the industry together with the profit
generated from the Traditional Breweries Investments transaction and
the intended share issues, should increase shareholder funds.
Meanwhile, NSB shareholders have approved a change to the name of the
company to United National Breweries (SA) Ltd.

Original_Search_Title: United Breweries Buys Into Cape Hotel
Headline: South Africa: United Breweries Buys Into Cape Hotel
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — United Breweries Resorts South Africa, a subsidiary of
India's UB Group that holds a 30% stake in National Sorghum
Breweries, has added a Cape Town hotel to its portfolio of tourist
attractions.
UB Resorts CEO Jaishankar Ramchandran said yesterday the group had
joined with a prominent hotel management company to launch The Cape
Milner previously known as The Mijlof Manor which will be valued at
several million rands once the current upgrading exercise is
complete.
The Cape Milner, scheduled to open in March next year, is 65% owned
by UB Resorts. An empowerment partner, Sechaba, has acquired a 10%
stake with an option to buy the balance of 25% currently being held
by the Industrial Development Corporation.
Ramchandran said UB Resorts had formed a strategic alliance with the
Three Cities group, which has a portfolio of 21 tourist products. He
said UB's vision was to become a "sizeable multiproduct player" in
the tourism industry.

Headline: Zimbabwe: Ngwenya: Medical Genius Or Charlatan?
Body_Text:
Harare — Forty-Year-Old marketing executive Sue Gorett swears by his
name. Were it not for Dr Richard Ngwenya, she said this week, her
life would still be miserable and characterised by bouts of
excruciating pain from an inflamed ulcer and severe stress.
Gorett says three months of Ngwenya's unconventional treatment of
wheatbran porridge, vegetables and deep stomach cleansing have done
the trick. The treatment has even improved her eyesight.
Thirty-nine-year-old Zimbabwe National Army soldier Muntu Josamu -
this is not his real name - has been confined to a wheel chair by
what started as a mild bout of athletes' foot.
Josamu says the small sores on his feet could not respond to
treatment and gradually multiplied until they spread to the groin.
His feet started swelling and developed scabs that emitted a watery
fluid.
Soon his head became swollen and he started experiencing difficulties
even to go to the toilet, said Josamu this week at Ngwenya's surgery
in the leafy Avenues area of Harare.
Doctors at Murewa Hospital told him he was suffering from Karposi
Sarcoma, a debilitating form of cancer that is said to attack mostly
people with AIDS. He was referred to Parirenyatwa Hospital for
radiotherapy.
It was while he was at Parirenyatwa that some people told him to try
Ngwenya's treatment.
"I have been under Dr Ngwenya's treatment and you can see my head is
now almost back to its normal size and the scars on my legs are
starting to heal," Josamu told the Financial Gazette.
Josamu now only feeds on traditional Zimbabwean foods such as sadza
prepared from sorghum, okra, roundnuts and pumpkin leaves. He says
his condition has greatly improved since taking Ngwenya's medication.
So is Protacius Richard Ngwenya a medical genius or a charlatan
making money out of the misery of Zimbabweans desperate to find a
cure for AIDS and other opportunistic illnesses? Hundreds of
Zimbabweans, both black and white, are flocking to Ngwenya's
controversial James Mobb Immune Enhancement Centre in the suburbs and
vouch that the retired former high-ranking Zimbabwe National Army
soldier is their saviour.
Ngwenya claims that most of the chronic diseases affecting
Zimbabweans today, including HIV/AIDS, diabetes, arthritis, cancers,
constipation, hepatitis, stress, meningitis and pneumonia are caused
by fungal infections and that people are consuming too much yeast and
sugars.
He says many people have been mistakenly diagnosed as positive of the
virus that causes AIDS and have died because even the new anti-
retroviral drugs will not help.
Anti-retrovirals such as Combivir are now being celebrated as the new
generation of drugs that can prolong the lives of HIV-positive people
but their costs have been found to be too prohibitive for patients in
developing countries.
"The medical community was looking for the so-called HIV and started
to blame other viruses which unfortunately were not there, saying
there was now Tuberculosis 1 and Tuberculosis 2. I am not seeing
HIV," Ngwenya said this week from his James Chinamano Avenue surgery.
Ngwenya, who admits that he has clashed with most of the local
medical fraternity and Health Minister Timothy Stamps over his views
on AIDS, this week trashed the HIV virus as a "hoax virus".
He blamed the emergence of many different diseases in Zimbabwe after
1980 to poverty and the biological warfare unleashed by retreating
Rhodesian forces at the height of the independence war which he said
had created the fungi.
Ngwenya, a member of the late Joshua Nkomo's ZIPRA in Zambia, claimed
that he was trained by ZAPU during the war to find ways to counter
the Rhodesians' biological weapons.
Some local AIDS experts this week however called Ngwenya a fake out
to make money from AIDS sufferers.
"We are talking of someone who just wants to make money from HIV,"
said Professor Norman Nyazema, director of the University of Zimbabwe
medical school's continuing health education department.
Nyazema reminded Zimbabweans that Ngwe-nya surfaced about three years
ago with claims that were supported by then Deputy Health Minister
Tsungirirai Hungwe that he had discovered a drug called Mocrea that
could treat AIDS.
"If three years ago this man was saying he was treating AIDS and now
he says there is no AIDS, then what was he treating?" Nyazema asked.
Nyazema said the fungal infections that Ngwenya talked about were the
same that caused opportunistic infections such as skin cancers but
that these infections had worsened among Zimbabweans because of the
AIDS pandemic.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Link in Lethal Brew Deaths
Headline: Kenya: KENYA: Poverty Link in Lethal Brew Deaths
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In a remarkable about-turn, Kenyan
political leaders and a clergyman are now calling on the government
to take a lead from its East African neighbours and legalise the
production and distribution of traditional brews as part of its
poverty eradication policy.
Their call comes in the wake of a highly publicised incident in
Nairobi's sprawling Mukuru slums last week in which an adulterated
concoction left 140 people dead, tens blinded and more than 500
hospitalised with complications.
Parliament on Wednesday said the lives of poor Kenyans could be saved
and alcoholism reduced if traditional brews were legalised, as is the
case in Uganda and Tanzania. Most African countries have legalised
traditional brews such as chang'aa.
Bishop Otieno Wasonga of the Anglican Church of Kenya argued that two
traditional brews, busaa and chang'aa, were the only ones affordable
by poor Kenyans as well as a source of income for some of them.
Noting that the church does not condone alcoholism, the Bishop
however argued that if the government believed that alcohol was good
for the people, then it should not be selective but should legalise
these traditional brews.
The legislature concurred with Bishop Wasonga, saying that if the
drinks were legalised, hygienic standards could be assured, while the
government could at the same time earn revenue.
Teetotaller President Daniel arap Moi, however, disagreed. Speaking
during a visit to the city's extensive Korogocho slums where chang'aa
brewing is commonplace, he reiterated that "factories manufacturing
illicit brews will be closed down" to rid the country of "doomsday"
drinks. He blamed the brew deaths on opposition MPs and the clergy
who campaigned for the repeal of the notorious 'Chiefs Act', which
empowered local chiefs to raid chang'aa and busaa distilleries and
drinking houses.
The victims of adulterated alcohol are almost always the poor because
there is no cheap alternative beer on the market that compares with
"kumi kumi" (literally 10-10 in Swahili), which is sold at 10 Kenya
shillings (roughly $US 0.12) a glass. Typically, the victims in last
week's incident comprised the poor, single parents, the unemployed
and the under-employed whose pastime is drinking chang'aa, a potent
brew made from fermented maize or sorghum flour which is distilled.
The killer brew was laced with ingredients such as methanol and
formaldehyde, an embalming fluid. For most of these people, chang'aa
is the only affordable drink.
The basic national minimum salary of Kshs.5,000 a month is a drop in
the ocean in so far as goods and services are concerned. At most,
such an amount lasts two weeks for a family of two.
"We are in the business, not because we like it," one civil servant
said. "We have to supplement our lean pay packets." Luckily this
dealer did not get his supplies from the Mukuru "distiller".
The legislators argued much in the same vein, saying that the
legislation against traditional brews in 1998, ostensibly to rid
society of drunks and to save lives, was a futile exercise because
"our people are poor and have to eke a decent or indecent living at
whatever cost".
At that time, Parliament blamed several cheap locally distilled
liquors following the death of 80 people believed to have drunk an
illicit brew laced with methylated spirit. The following year, 19
people were killed after taking an adulterated brew. The alcohol
content of some of the brews has been found to be as high as 90
percent. There are more chang'aa dens in the country's poor
neighbourhoods than officially acknowledged.
Even the cheaper brews by Kenya Breweries Ltd and other large
distillers, which retail at about Kshs.36 a bottle (about US $0.45)
are still way beyond the means of the vast majority of Kenyans. The
country's per capita income is estimated at US $280, with about half
the population living in absolute poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Women Step Up War Against Illegal Brews
Headline: Kenya: Women Step Up War Against Illegal Brews
Body_Text:
Nairobi, Kenya — Still grieving the loss of over 140
people who died after consuming a contaminated illegal
brew, a group of Kenyan women has launched a strong crusade
against the social menace.
Leaders of various women's organisations Tuesday mobilised
their lot in Kangemi, a low-income estate on the western
outskirts of Nairobi, and flushed out those suspected to be
behind the sale of the deadly "chang'aa" and "busaa" drinks.
Chang'aa is unrefined gin, while busaa is brewed from
millet or sorghum.
The 300 protesters, all members of the Kangemi Women's
Group, destroyed the shebeens and impounded the brewing and
distilling equipment, which they ferried to the local provincial
administration office.
At one of the busaa dens, the women were shocked to find
dead rats and women's underpants and sanitary cotton wool in
busaa and raw chang'aa containers.
During the march, the women arrested a man with 10 litres
of chang'aa and handed him over to police.
Efforts by the provincial administration officials to calm
the women and plead with them to let police do their work, were
fruitless.
The group's chairperson, Grace Njoroge, said they had
been forced to take this action because their husbands had been
"married" to the illicit drinks and abandoned their homes.
"Most of them (men) have been made impotent by the drinks
and cannot perform their night duties," she complained.
The move follows an incident at the Nairobi slum
residential area of Mukuru, on the southern suburbs of the
capital, in which over 140 consumers of the illegal drink have
died and 400 others hospitalised in the past 10 days.
Scores of other drinkers have gone blind.
The spirit, dubbed "kumu kumi," or "ten ten" on account of
its price of 10 Kenyan Shillings (about 12 US cents) per measure,
is suspected to have been laced with methanol, a highly poisonous
chemical.
To enhance its potency, the dealers usually mix the gin
with ethanol, which is less toxic. But, out of greed and lack of
concern for human life, some dealers occasionally lace the drink
with small doses of methanol.
Occasionally the plan goes awry, resulting in the death of
the consumers who cannot afford the legal drinks because of
endemic poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Is Behind the Increase in Illicit Brews
Headline: Kenya: Poverty Is Behind the Increase in Illicit Brews
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Death and injury from consumption of illicit brews is an
almost daily occurrence in the poorer sections of the Kenyan
community.
Unable to afford conventional drinks, the number of poor Kenyans
submitting to the vice has grown with the worsening state of the
country's economy. Half-hearted and haphazard attempts by the police
and the provincial administration to curb the practice have largely
failed due to growing poverty.
The fact that many of the poorly-paid police officers and chiefs
comprise a good portion of patrons who frequent these illicit
outlets, speaks volumes for the failure of the crackdown.
It is also common practice for law enforcers to operate protection
rings that ensure dealers in illicit brews are not arrested.
Save for the occasional raid by police officers out to punish traders
for failing to pay the "retainer", the effort has largely been
abandoned to women's groups who, out of concern for their menfolk,
have attacked dealers.
The practice has of late gained currency in Kirinyaga, Nyeri,
Murang'a, Kiambu districts and parts of Nairobi among them Kangemi
and Kawangware.
Nyeri MP, Mr. Wanyiri Kihoro has also mounted a one-man campaign
against the trade, and like the women, has had to contend with a
hostile police and Provincial Administration.
A highly-publicised government ban of some of the better known brews
and spirits in 1998, was largely ignored by the manufacturers who
abound in Nairobi and most of the country's urban centres.
The brews included Kulta, Sorghum-Sake, Kibuku Ram, International
Beer, Rumulika, Medusa and Simba Wine, Cantata, Kamata, Kulta,
Sorghum, Medusa and Teachers.
These, and many new brands can be readily obtained in many of the
dingy outlets dotted in Nairobi slums and trading centres in Central
and Rift Valley provinces.
The ban followed a parliamentary motion by Kangema MP, Mr. John
Michuki whose area has been badly afflicted by the vice. In his
constituency alone, at least 120 people had died after consuming the
brews, he told Parliament.
Mathira MP Matu Wamae said 200 people had died in his constituency
from the brews. Kabete MP, Mr. Paul Muite said 205 of his
constituents had also died.
Faced with a plethora of life-sapping problems, the Kenyan society
has generally relegated to the periphery the vice which only comes to
public attention when people die.
Yesterday's is a case in point.
The catalogue of deaths arising from consumption of illicit brews and
spirits is frightening.
The worst single incident, and perhaps not the last, occurred in
August, 1998 when nearly 100 people died, and many others went blind
after consuming a brew laced with power alcohol.
The Maai Mahiu incident was - Kenyan style - quickly forgotten as
many similar tragedies have followed, the difference being the brands
of brew consumed.
Among those who died in Narok in 1998 was a policeman and a public
health officer said to have been a regular consumer of the brew.
The brew was siphoned from tankers destined for neighbouring
countries and sold cheaply in Nakuru and Narok districts.
The 33 suspects who were subsequently arrested and charged with
causing the death of 36 of the victims, were later released as most
of the witnesses either died, were too ill to testify or could not be
traced altogether.
In 1996, 24 people died while six others ran blind in 1996 after
taking an illicit brew in Murang'a.

Original_Search_Title: Township Dwellers Weigh Up Poll Options
Headline: South Africa: Township Dwellers Weigh Up Poll Options
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Driving down the Soweto highway past Diepkloof and
Orlando East, pictures of a smiling Democratic Alliance (DA) leader
Tony Leon proclaim in Sotho: "Ya batho ka moka" (for all people).
Ironically, the African National Congress (ANC) poster's message is
in English: "Speeding up change and fighting poverty."
The absence of posters of other traditionally black parties like the
Pan Africanist Congress, the Azanian People's Organisation (Azapo)
and the Socialist Party of Azania is glaring. If the posters are
anything to go by, next month's local government elections in the
area will be a contest between the DA and the ANC.
Where Noordgesicht, the coloured township next to Orlando East, meets
Soweto there is a busy shopping centre.
Ephraim Khuzwayo, who works in the bottlestore for R100 a week, says:
"I did not register to vote because I cannot think of any party to
support. I have been let down by the ANC."
Raymond Norman and Henry Koerratwo, from Noordgesicht, who had
crossed the "artificial" racial divide of a busy highway to venture
into the township of Orlando to buy liquor, join the conversation and
say: "Tony Leon, he is our man."
Outside the building, taxi owner and driver Vusi Manana says he lives
in Meadowlands township and up to now there has been no delivery of
services promised by local councillors, who he describes as
"useless". "Let us not even blame them for unemployment, but let us
look at what our townships look like. The water and electricity gets
cut, there are no proper streets, no sewerage. What do we have to
vote for?" he asks.
Orlando East, one of Soweto's oldest townships, has a politicised
population made up largely of older people, as the younger generation
has moved out. Residents still speak of James "Sofasonke" Mpanza, who
led the homeless to build shacks in Orlando West. This led to
government having to build houses for the thousands living on the
edge of Orlando township.
Further down the road, two women say they not only voted for the ANC
in the past, but also campaigned in the township for it. Yet they
also say the party has let them down.
"We applied for houses in 1991 and again in 1995 and nothing has come
out of our applications. Instead foreigners, because they have the
money, have been allocated houses near New Canada. That area was
ours. It was earmarked for Orlando residents. We have electricity and
water cut off, there are no jobs and we are suffering. Why should we
support the ANC? Let those who have benefited from them vote for
them," the women say.
Orlando West in Soweto has always been an ANC stronghold. This is
where veteran ANC leaders such as Nelson Mandela and Walter Sisulu
lived before being imprisoned on Robben Island.
It is also the township which experienced bloody turf battles between
ANC-affiliated Soweto youth organisations and those backing Azapo. It
is where the Mandela United Football Club, which terrorised Soweto
townships, was born and destroyed.
In the 1980s, Xorile Street in Orlando West was known to be the turf
of ANC youth. Today, where the ANC youth once carried AK-47 rifles in
broad daylight, it is now a forlorn area. Except for the unemployed
who loiter aimlessly, there is nothing extraordinary about the
street.
It is midday and pensioner Bobby Zwane, who recently built a one-
roomed structure, sells sorghum beer cartons and is enjoying a drink
with his friends, whose ages range between 25 and 60. They share many
things. They are unemployed and bitter. They also voted for the ANC
in the past elections.
"We gave the ANC two chances and they failed us. For now, we are
going to give Tony Leon a chance. If he also fails us we will give it
to someone else who will live up to his promises," says Zwane. His
friends join in the conversation; a chorus of damnation of the ANC.
They are not moved by criticism that by voting for the DA they will
be bringing back a white government. "What has a black government
done for us? Our electricity and water is being cut, pensioners have
to queue for long hours to get their pensions. Who cares for us and
our children who died to get the ANC into power?"
One of the men says: "During the apartheid government, life was
difficult, but I always had money in my back pocket."
Their anger, it seems, has been fuelled by the recent actions of
municipal officials who removed containers where people had set up
telephone services. They are of the opinion the ANC government should
have known better than to remove the containers, because as a black
government they know black people's problems. They say they feel
betrayed.
ANC provincial spokesman Mike Ramagoma says his party is confident
many people will vote for the ANC because they have seen what the
party has been able to achieve in only six years.
He says it was not an easy six years, as government had to turn many
things around to make towns and cities work better, to include black
townships left out under apartheid. "That process has taken time and
plans have now been put in place. We are on track towards achieving a
better life for all the people, not just a few," he says.
Ramagoma says those planning on abstaining from voting need to be
reminded that the vote was achieved through blood and the loss of
loved ones. He says those planning on abstaining from voting need to
be reminded that the vote was achieved through blood and the loss of
loved ones.

Original_Search_Title: Researchers Develop High-yield Potato, Sorghum Varieties
Headline: Ethiopia: Researchers Develop High-yield Potato, Sorghum Varieties
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa, Ethiopia — Researchers at Alemeya
University in eastern Ethiopia have developed two high-yield and
disease-resistant varieties of potato and sorghum.
The government-owned Ethiopian Herald newspaper reported
Thursday that the two potato varieties yielded up to 40 tons per
hectare while those of sorghum produced 8.3 tons per hectare.
The potato varieties were discovered by a team headed by
Tackling Tsega, head of the potato research project, while Temam
Hussein headed the team on sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Awassa And the Road to Moyale Part II
Headline: Ethiopia: Awassa And the Road to Moyale Part II
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Last week we went from Awassa to the Shabe Rock
Carvings at Dilla. This week we continue on Tuesday, further south.
Tuttufalla and Tootitti:
Past Dilla there are two large monument fields. The first one is
Tuttufalla, 25 km south of Dilla on the left hand side down a gravel
road. The locals tell you where it is. You go down the gravel road
about 2 km, then take a dirt road about 1 km up a hill to the
monuments.
Tuffufalla is a nice field. It has been worked on by the same French
archaeological team, which reconstructed the Tiya field south of
Addis. Around 1994, they set up the 70 or so stelae in a loose rock
foundation.
The stelae are really interesting - with a greater variety of designs
than either the Tiya or the Tootitti fields. They have a spider-like
carving, which is the only carving they have at Tootitti, and a
pillow stand design like in Tiya. Tuttufalla is therefore a link
between the other two sites. The stelae are smaller and shorter than
in the other two fields.
The unique feature of Tuttufalla is the faces carved on some of the
stones. These are fascinating. There is one stone with a long face
with an aquiline nose, very nicely done.
The stories of the locals about the stones are interesting. They
associate them with Gran Mohammed, the Muslim invader from Harar who
conquered the area in the early 1500s. One local informant said he
didn't know if Gran had them carved, but he had taken the pillars
with his powerful left hand and driven them into the ground. He'd
then used them to tether his horses.
Although the locals dated the stelae at around' 3000 years old or
so', they seem to be associated with Tiya which has been dated to
around 700 years ago. Who did them is a mystery. They are called the
' Sidamo monuments in the Southern People s' Region, but the local
Sidamo people have very little knowledge about them. With the number
of invasions which have gone though there over the centuries, it's
very had to know who was there back then.
Another 10 km south of the turnoff to Tuttufalla is the twin stelae
field of Tootitti. The turnoff to the left is at a little place
called Chelba. It is easy to miss the little dirt road up to the
monoliths - my first time I drove straight past. On the second try I
found it, and we drove the rough 3 kilometers up to the top of the
hill to Tootitti.
The stones appear suddenly on the left. Some are standing but most
have fallen over. The tallest ones tower over the sorghum in the
field. They stand 2- 4 meters high. At first we counted 56 in the
bunch in front of the local administration office - I thought that
was all of them. But the field went on and on, and I lost track of
the number after almost 200. The monoliths vary in size and shape,
but most are cylindrical. Most of the ones lying on he ground are
broken. The ones standing seem to indicate regular shapes and walls.
A-tell tale ridge worn in the top of one of the stallest standing
monoliths seems to indicate that it was a pillar with a roof and/or
walls attached to it.
All of this is speculation of course. Again, I wasn't able to find
out from anyone local the age or reason or builders of the monoliths.
The only marking on the Tootitti monoliths I could see were carved
spiders with long legs - which don't resemble the swords, trees and
circles of the Tiya monuments, but were also in evidence a
Tuttufalla.
Borena and the Road to Kenya:
About 150 km south of Awassa you begin the long descent from the lush
highlands to the barren lowlands of Borena. One of the first signs is
the appearance of camels. Camels simply cannot survive forlon in
cooler climates, they get sick and die, so they area a very good
indicator of lowlands.
Banana trees are replaced by low acacias, the dryland tree of Africa.
The landscape turns to classical Africa Savannah, with the flat
topped accia standing out above a vast grassland. The soil is iron
red, and formed into phallic ant hills in an astonishing variety of
shapes.
The people change too. The highland Sidamo give way to the lowland
Borena Oromo. While highland people have generally adopted casual
western dress, the Borena women retain a different style. They wear
colorful shawls and dresses, falling fetchingly off their shoulders.
The men wear western-style clothes, but they carry spears. My clever
