brother- in law-Dennis, who visited us, observed that men in the
north seemed to be born with sticks in their hands, and men in the
south with plastic bags. These guys must have been genetically
disposed to spears.
There is an interesting tale about the Borena and their spears. It is
said that a Borena woman, even a married one, can have a roll in the
hay with any man she chooses. The man drives his spear into the
ground in front of the hut before he goes in, and even the husband is
not allowed to intrude so long as the spear is there.
The Borena are also famous for their strong caste system based on age
groups. Groups move though stages of life and responsibility
together, moving from one stage to another as they age. The Borna and
Guji Oromo are the least Westernized and have the least contact with
Amhara people, so they retain traditions which may have been general
with Oromos but have been discontinued with most.
There are very few towns on the road. For miles there is nothing but
trees and antihills. Occasionally you see livestock and people, but
must of the time is seems like empty wilderness.
Next week the series concludes with the trip to Kenya.
Send your comments or questions to John Graham at
jgraham@telecom.net.et

Headline: South Africa: National Sorghum Breweries Turns Profit
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — National Sorghum Breweries (NSB), South Africa's first
black-owned company, has turned a profit for the first time in many
years.
The company is on track for a brighter future and plans to reveal the
details at its annual meeting in September, said Jaishankar
Ramchandran, a representative of Indian industrial group United
Breweries (UB), which is NSB's largest shareholder and has management
control.
Ramchandran said the UB management team had succeeded in reducing
overheads and increasing productivity and capacity utilisation at
NSB's breweries.
The company's prospects have also improved after the competition
commission recently ruled in favour of NSB buying SA Breweries'
Traditional Beer Investments. The commission gave the takeover the
goahead in April and the deal was finally concluded in June.
UB bought a 30% interest in NSB for R70m in November 1996, with an
option to increase its shareholding to 50% in due course.
Ramchandran said UB was sticking to its agreement with NSB to rope in
an empowerment partner.
He said there were still plans to list NSB once it has established a
sound profit record. NSB commands a market share of approximately 70%
in the sorghum beer segment in SA.
Ramchandran said the deal between NSB and UB represented the largest
Indian investment commitment in SA and a close co-operation between
Indians and black SA.
He said: "UB is committed to build shareholder value so that the
value of NSB's black ownership grows. The UB group believes that this
investment will be seen as a test case of how serious the SA
government is in efforts to make SA more attractive for international
investment." Ramchandran said NSB was UB's largest investment outside
of India and certainly the biggest Indian investment in SA.
With worldwide revenues of $1bn UB is one of India's fastest- growing
industrial houses with more than 60 companies around the world.
UB's core competencies are in brewing and distilling its own brand of
rum, brandy, spirits and beer such as Kingfisher. New lines of
business are trading, media, ecotourism and resorts, including the
Mabula Game Lodge. Ramchandran said UB's main focus in the future was
to strengthen the group's position in existing profitable operations.

Original_Search_Title: South Africa's Awethu Breweries Poised For A Turnaround'
Headline: South Africa's Awethu Breweries Poised For A Turnaround'
Body_Text:
Durban — Traditional beverage and food products group Awethu
Breweries says it expects to stage a turnaround in its current
financial year, on the back of greater market penetration and cost-
cutting initiatives.
Newly appointed MD Tony Ford said last week the group expects to post
only a marginal loss (1999: R8,9m loss) for its full year to end-
June and is on track for a profitable interim period.
Ford, already the major shareholder in Awethu, subscribed earlier
last week for 21,67-million shares in the company at the current
price of 6c. The move will raise R1,3m for Awethu.
The issue, necessitated by "a severe cash flow shortage", means Ford
will own 42,4-million shares, or 50,09% of the increased issued share
capital.
He was appointed MD several weeks ago with the previous MD Mike
Kennedy assuming the position of strategic and commercial director.
Ford said the historic cashflow problems stemmed from Awethu
acquiring several "overpriced assets". Awethu, although trading
profitably, has been forced to commit resources to paying for these
assets. Ford says the capital injection will ensure the company
overcomes these problems.
He says Awethu is trading profitably and expects to either break even
or report no more than a R500000 loss for the year to end-June.
Ford forecasts an interim profit of between R2m and R2,5m, as cost-
cutting exercises of R150000 a month pay further dividends.
Awethu distributes traditional beverage and food products to the mass
informal market, mainly through its own retail network of more than
700 franchised outlets in the Free State, Gauteng and North West
Province.
The company also manufactures sorghum beer, mageu (an alcohol-free
maize drink), milled maize products, milled wheat products and bread.
The business is highly integrated with full control of raw material
inputs, manufacture and distribution.
Ford said the group has four years left of its contract with National
Sorghum Breweries. Increased market penetration should push sales up
20% to 25% over that time. The contract can be renewed.

Headline: Nigeria: Christian Group Faults Sharia Adoption
Body_Text:
Lagos — The present clamour for the implementation of Sharia legal
system by some Northern state governors has been described as a mere
deception to actualise the hidden agenda of the Muslims in the country.
At a press conference in Kaduna, last Thursday, a non-governmental
organisation (NGO), Voice of the Christian Martyr, a member of
International Christian Association, (ICA) described the agitation for
the Islamic legal system as a ploy to destabilize the country because a
Christian is the president of the country.
According to the organisation's Director, Rev. Isaac Oluwole Newton-
Wusu, I think it's absolute deception for anyone to come out and say
that a system of law can only govern a section of the citizens and that
others will be left free. I think this is a deception.
Newton-Wusu enjoined the Muslims to come out and tell the whole world
the truth behind the Sharia legal system, which has shaken the country
to the very foundation.
These governors have actually sworn to uphold the constitution, why
are they now bringing their religious books to substitute the
constitution of Nigeria? I think, this is unfair, he added.
Furthermore, Rev. Newton-Wusu was of the opinion that the arguments
advanced by proponents of Sharia that it would not affect the Christians
were untrue.
In Saudi Arabia, where Sharia is being practised, he said, if you
monitor media reports very well, many Christians hands are being cut
off. In fact, from our knowledge, we know of underground churches in
Saudi Arabia and Egypt, which comprise people who have lost their two
hands, and any time you are in the church, this is the way they clap
(demonstrates). They have lost their two hands to Sharia.
He then warned it Christians would continue to resist the imposition
of the Islamic legal system on the citizens because the more they
continue to push Christians to the wall, Christians cannot just sit down
and watch them. According to him, the worldwide NGO was in Kaduna to
donate relief materials, which comprise rice, maize, sorghum, beans and
toilet soap worth over N1million to the victims of the religious crisis
in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Drops To 13.5 Percent In Sudan
Headline: Sudan: Inflation Drops To 13.5 Percent In Sudan
Body_Text:
Khartoum, Sudan(PANA) — Inflation dropped to 13.5 percent in February from
14.7 percent in January, the central organ for statistics announced in Khartoum
Saturday.
The organ's general manager, Hassan Abdulsalam Suleiman, said a drop in the
prices of wheat, millet and other food stuffs as well as those of vegetables
had helped to bring inflation down.
However, he noted "a slight increase " in sorghum prices during the period.
"Prices of beef, lamb, electricity, kerosene and butane have recorded relative
increases also," he added.

Headline: Botswana: Beer Sales Affected By Floods
Body_Text:
Gaborone — The giant beer monopolists which are significantly owned by
Sechaba Brewery Holdings Limited, a publicly listed company, had their sales
affected by the recent floods as distribution networks were paralysed.
The two breweries, Botswana Breweries (Pty) Ltd (BBL) and Kgalagadi
Breweries (Pty) Ltd control sorghum and clear beer production in the country
respectively.
KBL controls the production of beer products such as St Louis, Castle, lion
and so forth in addition to soft drinks like Coca Cola while BBL produces
Chibuku.
The distribution and delivery of these products especially Chibuku that has
a short shelf life, demands an efficient infrastructure to avoid damages and
expiry periods. Consumption too besides the low production was adversely
affected.
According to one of the local stock brokers, Investec Securities, the sale
of beverages while being highly skewed to the Gross Domestic Product and
disposable income is also correlated to the weather conditions.
Less people drink less beer or soft drinks when the weather conditions are
not conducive. The low demand for these products in the last few weeks is
attributed to recent rains which affected the ability of breweries to deliver
products.
The stock broker says it has downgraded its annualised turnover assumption
for year 2000 as well as marginally revised its EBITDA margin.
Stock levels are assumed to be marginally higher than historical averages.
This has resulted in Investect's forecast falling by 6 % with a prediction that
earnings per share will rise by 61% to 74.4 % or an annualised rise of 61.1
thebe. Despite the downgrading of Sechaba its return on capital is estimated at
28.6 % and a clear beer operating profit per hectolitre of about P140, well in
exceed of its regional competitors. Investec maintains that it continues to
recommend a buy for Sechaba stock in view of potential profitability of the
company.

Headline: South Africa: Manuel reforms SA taxes
Body_Text:
Cape Town — Finance Minister Trevor Manuel has proposed the most far-
reaching tax reforms in recent years, including a new capital gains tax and a
R9,9bn tax relief package.
In a budget designed to stimulate growth, create jobs and reduce
inequalities, he also set government's long-awaited inflation target at between
3% and 6%. Inflation, based on the target definition, is currently running at
7,7%, and the aim is to bring it within the new band by the fourth quarter of
2002.
The Reserve Bank has introduced a new consumer price index for inflation
targeting, which mirrors the existing CPI, excluding interest costs.
Other features of the budget include a further easing of foreign exchange
controls and the taxation of individuals' income worldwide.
Small businesses will benefit from a tax break under which they will pay a
rate of only 15% on their first R100000 of income, and 30% on larger amounts.
Financial markets took the budget in their stride. Share prices on the
Johannesburg Stock Exchange rose as Manuel began his speech, but gave up some
gains after the announcement of the capital gains tax. The all share index
ended 80 points higher at 8270.
Nico Czypionka, chief economist at Societe Generale SA, predicted equities
would benefit from improved foreign perceptions resulting from the budget.
Economists and analysts are divided on whether the budget measures are
sufficient for credit rating agencies to upgrade SA's rating. The rand firmed
2,4c yesterday to R6,285 to the dollar.
Manuel said "this budget makes a strong contribution to enhancing the growth
and development potential of our economy". his budget speech in Parliament. He
predicted an average annual growth rate of 3,4% over the next three years.
Government forecasts growth in gross domestic product (GDP) of 3,5% this year.
The capital gains tax will kick in on April 1 next year. It is aimed at
achieving greater tax equity by preventing the conversion of taxable income
into tax-free capital gains. Family homes, private vehicles and household goods
will be exempt.
Individuals in lower income groups will absorb most of the R9,9bn in forgone
revenue in tax concessions, but those in higher income brackets will reap
substantial benefits.
Just more than 40% of the forgone revenue is accounted for by those with
incomes below R70000, and 38% by those with incomes of between R70000 and
R150000. Taxpayers with incomes between R50000 and R200000 will pay 10% less
tax, while those earning more than R200000 can look forward to an 8% reduction.
The top marginal rate for individuals, which kicks in at R200001 rather than
the previous R120001, falls from 45% to 42% while the income tax thresholds
rises from R19526 to R21111.
Patrick McGurk, tax partner at KPMG, said: "There are more tax issues in
this budget than in any of the last five or six." He predicted that the closing
of a number of tax loopholes would be "potentially disastrous" for structured
finance deals.
The budget included a new international airport departure tax of R100, which
takes effect on August 1. Manuel also raised "sin" taxes on tobacco products
and liquor, as well as fuel levies. The sins of smoking and drinking are once
again hit, with excise duties on a packet of 20 cigarettes rising by a real 10%
or 38c, while excise duties on alcohol, excluding sorghum beer, rise by 5,5%.
The duty on soft drinks and mineral waters is cut by a third from 12c to 8c per
litre.
He said fiscal prudence remained a high priority and committed government to
balancing current expenditure with revenues by 2002.
Spending is projected to rise by about 8% in the fiscal year to March 2001
to R233,5bn. It will be financed by revenues of R210,4bn, giving a deficit of
R23bn or 2,6% of GDP, up from 2,4% in the present fiscal year.
The net borrowing requirement in the coming year is expected to be R20,3bn,
of which R13,6bn will be financed on the domestic market and $1bn abroad.
Privatisation revenue is conservatively projected at R5bn, increasing to R10bn
in 2002/03.
On the spending side, Manuel said stronger economic growth, improved tax
collection and lower debt service costs made it possible to set aside an extra
R8,3bn for social services in the coming year. However, social spending as a
percentage of noninterest expenditure is set to fall from 57% to 55,8%.
Czypionka said the budget implied "very low" public service salary rises
which were likely to spark controversy.
rime prevention. Health and welfare together take the largest slice of
expenditure, rising 7,7% to R53,2bn. This is followed by education which
increases 6,1% to R50,7bn and police, prisons and justice which rises 7,3% to
R23,4bn. The housing and oher social services budget has been sliced by 7,7% to
R4,8bn.

Headline: Zambia: August Inflation Rate Declines By 2.9%
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The inflation rate for the month of August stood at 1.3 per
cent, a decrease of 2.9 per cent on the July rate of 4.2 per cent.
Central Statistics Office (CSO) assistant director Oliver Chinganya,
in a statement on Friday, said the monthly inflation rates for the
urban low and high income and non-urban groups were 1.0, 1.9 and 1.2
per cent respectively.
Chinganya said the general inflation rate stood at 27.0 per cent in
August, measured on a 12 month basis while that of July was at 25.9
for the year 2000.
He said the rate was 1.1 percentage point higher than the
corresponding rate of 25.9 per cent for July, 2000.
He said the year-on-year inflation rate for the low and high urban
income groups were 2.63 and 29.5 per cent respectively, while the
rate for the rural group stood at 25.8 per cent.
"The level of inflation in August, 2000, was partially due to an
increase in food prices. Price increases occurred in cereals and
cereal products, fresh meat, dressed chicken, fish, dried kapenta,
fresh vegetables, oils and fats, fresh fruits, sugar, salt, sweets
and irish potatoes," Chinganya said.
He said the breakfast mealie meal prices had continued to drop while
the price of roller meal and maize grain reflected an increase during
the month.
Chinganya said the price increases in non-food commodities also
contributed to the observed rise in the Consumer Price Index (CPI).
He said increases were registered in the costs of clothing and
footwear, rent and house hold fuel, medical and health services,
textiles and household appliances, personal care products, hotel
accommodation and meals.
"For instance, the average price of a 25 kg bag of roller meal
increased from K13, 070 in July, 2000 to K13, 441 in August, 2000
reflecting a 2.8 per cent increase," Chinganya said. "The price of a
20 litre tin of maize grain increased by 3.1 per cent."
He said the average price of mixed cut (beef) increased by 3.4 per
cent, while the price of 1 kg of dressed chicken went up by 3.8 per
cent.

Headline: Mozambique: Re-establishing a safe water supply
Body_Text:
Mozambique — From the very limits of telecommunication, Andreas
Koestler phones in his order. Mozambican telephone landlines are down
beyond this point and the nation's mobile network has not yetn reached
any farther. Sitting midmorning at a roadside cafe, shopping list
before him, Andreas phones the office in Norway, just as people begin
to arrive for work. He learns about a new government at home. His
colleagues learn about the up to the minute needs of the people in
Mozambique, particularly the thousands living in Chokwe region, some
200 kilometers from capital Maputo.
"If we bring in one pump and one generator*.."
Andreas' words drift off mid-sentence, but he's hardly shifted into
pause mode. His mind is already on the next topic. He's been sent to
assess just how badly hit Mozambique's water and sanitation systems
were, and to assess where best to apply Norwegian Church Aid (NCA)
funding, not only to mitigate suffering, but to help improve overall
water systems to maintain health.
He's just learned from Tulio de Almeida, Chokwe's Chief Water
Engineer that the city's water had been supplied by several boreholes.
Tulio a graduate Maputo's Industrial Institute has worked in Chokwe for
eight years. He explains that his technicians are not lacking in
expertise, only in money. One look around reveals little in the way of
equipment and tools.
According to Tulio, all Chokwe's pumps had been operating before
flooding hit but all the motors, mounted above ground were completely
submerged for days. Although the company that built the motors stocks
parts for ten years, these are sixteen years old. One suggestion is to
bring in submersible pumps for a more flexible solution but its
difficult to tell. They can't even try out the old motors until they
have electricity. Unfortunately, with so many power lines damaged
throughout the country, no one knows how soon the electricity company
will restore power up this particular river valley. Only at that time
will Tulio and his crew be able to assess the damage to their
electrical switchboards.
"We've seen the situation changing so rapidly," explained David
Banks, another consulting engineer sent by ACT-NCA, "its impossible to
plan five days ahead."
Yet a decision had to be made. Despite a less than perfect picture
of the ground situation, (which with situations changing this quickly
may never be feasible), a tight shipping deadline required the
engineers to consider ordering water equipment. It was the last day
they could order from Oslo in time to get it trucked to Amsterdam for
flight out Monday or Tuesday.
Andreas and David ordered approximately US $100,000 worth of the
most versatile water system components they could choose among the
equipment pre-stored in Norway. If Chokwe improves before the equipment
arrives, there is no shortage of places still lacking water and
sanitation upgrade.
Over the past weekend, the engineers toured an area in central
Mozambique that was not only hit with flooding but was also the site
where cyclone Eline made landfall a couple weeks back. They found the
banks of the Buzi River far steeper than those beside the Limpopo. Most
homes where built high enough to have missed the rising waters which
may have reached six meters above normal river level. Even those at
lower levels have already rebuilt, and already replanted sorghum among
their rotting maize. Little seems to be needed in the way of water and
sanitation either. People in the areas preferred the taste of river
water over the brackish water available from local boreholes.
As Andreas packed to leave, his final recommendations were that ACT-
LWF concentrate in the Gaza area for water and sanitation needs. He and
his colleague also suggested that external contractors be used to
develop Congolote, the planned community within the greater urban
Maputo. This area will shortly settle 1500 families displaced by Maputo
flooding and needs additional boreholes and latrines. Gaza Province is
the number one on the priority list here. Not just Chokwe, but farther
up river to Mabalane and across the river to Guija, where LWF projects
were already working when flooding hit.
Elaine Eliah is a press officer currently working for ACT-.LWF in
Mozambique (mobile phone: + 27 82 85 89 106). For further information
you can also contact: Nils Carstensen (mobile + 41 79 358 3171). ACT
Web Site address: http://www.act-intl.org
ACT is a worldwide network of churches and related agencies meeting
human need through coordinated emergency response. The ACT Coordinating
Office is based with the World Council of Churches (WCC) and the
Lutheran World Federation (LWF) in Switzerland.

Original_Search_Title: Judges Grant Defence Motion Barring Four Prosecution Witnesses
Headline: Rwanda: Judges Grant Defence Motion Barring Four Prosecution Witnesses
Body_Text:
Arusha — Judges hearing the case against former Rwandan mayor Ignace
Bagilishema Tuesday ruled that the prosecution could not call four witnesses
whose statements had been disclosed late.
"The chamber rules that witnesses T, U, X and W cannot be called to testify
in trial and requests the prosecution to draw up a new list of witnesses
immediately," said presiding judge Erik Mose.
The written testimony of prosecution witnesses T, U, X and W, judges
observed, had been disclosed to the defence after the mandatory 60 days before
trial had passed.
Tuesday's decision follows a defence motion, which requested that the
witnesses be barred from giving oral testimony because the court had already
refused to admit their written statements. The prosecution had been hoping to
have the four present oral testimonies.
The prosecution is presenting its case against Ignace Bagilishema, the former
mayor of Mabanza commune, in Kibuye, western Rwanda, who is charged with seven
counts, including genocide, complicity in genocide, and various human rights
violations (crimes against humanity)
The court on Tuesday heard the prosecution's tenth witness, known as witness
Q to protect her identity. The witness is a Tutsi woman who told of how commune
officials under Bagilishema's authority engaged in training the Interahamwe
[Hutu militia) in early June 1994.
"These people came from various sectors for military training. They carried
sticks, shaped like guns," she said. "At that time the training was done by
Hakizimana Deo and Appollinaire Nsengimana," she added." Hakizimana was the
commune secretary and Appollinaire was assistant Bourgmestre [mayor]."
Witness Q, who is married to a Hutu, told the court how this saved her life.
"At that point in time when we were being attacked, almost all Tutsis had
been killed.people were attacking Tutsis who were married to Hutus and their
husbands were against this and that created misunderstanding among the Hutus
themselves," witness Q told the court.
"There was a meeting at the Bureau Communal (commune office) presided by
Bagilishema, it was said that if these Tutsis were killed, their husbands were
going to attack other Hutus so it was concluded that Tutsis who had not been
killed should be spared," Witness Q said.
The court also heard the testimony of another protected witness K who told
the court how moslems sheltering her had dressed her up as one of themselves and
taken her to a communal meeting for her own safety.
" He [Bagilishema] was speaking of the Tutsi houses which had been partially
destroyed, he wanted them completely razed down," she said. "The reason he gave
was that white people might come and ask who those houses belonged to, and that
the houses had to be destroyed to avoid such questions."
She also told the court that, when asked what to do with Tutsis found in the
sorghum fields, Bagilishema had answered "take them to the Kinihira [mass
grave]". At this point she fled the meeting.
Witness K also told the court how assistant mayors Celestin Semanza and
Appollinaire Nsengimana and other local leaders led others in torching Tutsi
houses.
Mary Kimani Internews Reporting from the International Criminal Tribunal for
Rwanda (ICTR) press room Phone from Africa 057 504207 up to 11 or 4370 ext 5235
Phone from USA 1(212) 963 2850 ext 5235 Fax 057 504000

Original_Search_Title: Mozambicans prefer a slave's life in rural Mpumalanga
Headline: Mozambique: Mozambicans prefer a slave's life in rural Mpumalanga
Body_Text:
Nelspruit — Mozambique's civil war may be over, but for thousands of illegal
immigrants who prefer eking out a living in rural Mpumalanga, the battle against
poverty is far from over.
Tribal trust areas around Nelspruit are especially riddled with desperate
Mozambicans who work their fingers to the bone for local residents and are often
only paid as little as R10 a month.
In Pienaar tribal trust there is an illegal community of at least 500
Mozambicans who do everything from selling homemade sorghum beer to building
houses for South Africans.
In nearby Zwelisha, three young Mozambican men get free accommodation from a
man who pays them a bag of mealie meal and between R10 and R20 a month to build
houses.
"We have to accept whatever is given to us because we have nowhere to go,"
says David Nkosi. "At least we get accommodation."
The young men said they crossed the border at night with some friends and
family who were later caught and deported.
"We walked through the bush day and night without food for about two weeks
until we met a man who took us to his home and gave us food," says Nkosi.
They've worked for the man who does construction work for two years.
"Sometimes he takes us to work in Johannesburg where we work for weeks
without being paid," says Andreas Nkosi.
"When the clients give us money he takes it."
They're not allowed to complain about their wages as their employer threatens
to deport them. He has apparently promised to help them get South African
identity books.
But the men don't qualify for identity books currently being granted to
refugees, as they have only been in the country for two years.
Only illegal Mozambicans who can prove they've been in South Africa for the
past 15 years qualify for citizenship.
One Mozambican woman who fled her abusive husband with two children got South
African citizenship after paying a R450 bribe to a Home Affairs official.
"I had to pay the money because I needed the ID book urgently. I knew that if
I didn't have one they would take me back to Mozambique," says Maria Mdluli.
Now she gets a South African pension and supplements her income by selling
homemade sorghum beer.
"I get a lot of money by selling sorghum beer because many people buy since
it is affordable," says Mdluli, who is illiterate.
She has been able to build herself a decent house and never wants to return
to Mozambique.
"It's good to know that I can buy myself whatever I want. In Mozambique I
couldn't even afford to buy sweets for my children," she says.

Original_Search_Title: International developers sacrifice 100 cattle to appease ancestral spirits
Headline: Eswatini: International developers sacrifice 100 cattle to appease ancestral spirits
Body_Text:
Piggs Peak , Swaziland — International developers have bowed to Swazi
tradition and are preparing to sacrifice 100 cattle to ancestral spirits in an
attempt to win support for the R343,4-million Maguga Dam project on the
kingdom's border with Mpumalanga.
Maguga Resettlement Committee chairman, Reverend Jameson Mncina, said the
cattle would be given to families who had graves in the dam basin for sacrifice
to appease spirits.
"We believe there are roughly 100 graves in the area and villagers have been
complaining that they are getting threatening dreams and visions from their
ancestors warning of disaster if the graves are flooded," said Mncina.
"We have therefore decided to give each of the affected families one cow per
grave plus an extra R250 as a 'wake fee'. The cows will be sacrificed when the
graves are exhumed and moved to safer ground."
All remains discovered in the graves will be reburied with full ritual near
the new villages created by the developers for communities that have been
displaced by the 1042-hectare dam.
Mncina added that villagers claimed their ancestors had threatened to exact
revenge by causing vehicle accidents, drownings and flooding unless they were
respected.
"There was also talk of river snakes and other monsters infecting the dam and
swallowing anyone who went near it. But we are confident that the proposed
sacrifices will appease the ancestors and at the same time reassure local
communities that this development is for their own good," said Mncina.
The Maguga dam is one in a series of multi-billion catchment projects along
the Komati River in Swaziland and Lomati River in South Africa. Maguga is
expected to begin filling with water in November 2 000 after its 107-metre high
rockfill embankment is completed. The dam is expected to fill completely during
the January to March wet season in 2001 and will provide water to Swaziland's
sugar, forestry and mining industries as well as other smaller planned
industrial development in northern Swaziland.
It's sister Matsamo or Driekoppies Dam in the Mpumalanga province of South
Africa is worth R488 million and was completed last year after some 2 000 people
were relocated.
"All of the developers are being as responsible as possible and are ensuring
that we honour all local customs and other requirements so that villagers cannot
turn round in the future and say they were steamrollered. Our handling of this
scheme sets a best practice standard for Swaziland and is also one of the best
in southern Africa," said Mncina.
The R250 wake fee is meant to be used to brew traditional sorghum and millet
beers for use in the rituals to exhume the graves. The sacrificed cattle will be
eaten during accompanying ceremonies.

Headline: South Africa: Commission rejects proposed beer merger
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — SA's competition watchdog has thwarted SA Breweries' (SAB's)
sorghum beer expansion plans by rejecting National Sorghum Breweries' (NSB's)
proposed acquisition of SAB subsidiary Traditional Beer Investments.
This is the first merger to be rejected by the new competition authority,
which was set up in September. NSB said yesterday it planned to appeal against
the decision.
NSB does not have the capital to pay for the subsidiary and has proposed
giving all its brands to SAB, which would license the brewery to produce the
sorghum beers exclusively for five years. NSB would have to set prices in
consultation with SAB.
The Competition Commission said the proposed merger and licensing agreement
"would effectively place the pricing of all commercially brewed beer products in
SA under the partial or full control of SAB".
The commission also rejected the deal on the grounds that it would result in
NSB controlling production of about 98% of SA's commercially brewed sorghum
beer. This could have a negative effect on beer prices, the commission said.
NSB produces an estimated 72% of SA's commercially brewed sorghum beer while
Traditional Beer Investments' share is estimated at 21%. Awethu Breweries, which
has an arrangement with the subsidiary, accounts for a further 5%.
The commission evaluated the deal because it counts as an "intermediate"
merger, with NSB and Traditional Beer Investments reporting combined annual
turnover of R580m. Only large mergers have to be judged by the competition
tribunal.
NSB executive vice-president (finance) Rajan Ranganathan said his group would
appeal to the tribunal. Commercial breweries represented only a segment of the
sorghum beer market, with informal sector brewing estimated to be three times
the size of the commercial producers.
The commission rejected the argument that the merger would save jobs, saying
it appeared that job losses would occur whether the merger went ahead or not.
